example_background: Revolut Ltd, a UK-based fintech company offering digital banking and payment services, applied for authorisation as an electronic money institution to issue electronic money products across Europe. The company demonstrated that its prepaid accounts and card services involved issuing stored value in exchange for funds, accepted by third parties for payments. The process involved detailed submissions on technical infrastructure, safeguarding measures, and compliance frameworks to ensure the electronic money definition was met without reliance on specific technologies.
process_and_outcome: The Financial Conduct Authority granted authorisation in 2017, followed by additional e-money licences in Lithuania and other EU states, enabling expansion of services. Ongoing supervision confirmed adherence to operational requirements.
source_rule: (7) It is appropriate to introduce a clear definition of electronic money in order to make it technically neutral. That definition should cover all situations where the payment service provider issues a pre-paid stored value in exchange for funds, which can be used for payment purposes because it is accepted by third persons as a payment.

example_background: Paysafecard, a prepaid voucher system provider operating in multiple EU countries, sought e-money institution status for its products where users purchase vouchers representing stored monetary value redeemable for payments at accepting merchants. The application emphasised the technical neutrality of the definition, covering voucher-based storage without electronic cards or apps initially, and detailed how funds received were safeguarded and value issued met payment acceptance criteria.
process_and_outcome: Authorised by the UK Financial Conduct Authority in 2012 as an e-money institution, with ongoing compliance monitoring and no major enforcement actions reported.
source_rule: (7) It is appropriate to introduce a clear definition of electronic money in order to make it technically neutral. That definition should cover all situations where the payment service provider issues a pre-paid stored value in exchange for funds, which can be used for payment purposes because it is accepted by third persons as a payment.

example_background: Wirecard, a German payment processing firm, held e-money institution licences in the UK and EU for issuing prepaid cards and stored value services. The services allowed customers to load funds for payments accepted by merchants. Issues arose when regulators questioned the firm's reporting of outstanding electronic money balances and client fund safeguarding, amid broader accounting discrepancies in its operations.
process_and_outcome: The UK Financial Conduct Authority revoked Wirecard's e-money licence in 2020 due to concerns over financial stability and compliance. This contributed to the company's insolvency, with investigations revealing €1.9 billion in missing funds.

The authorisation process verified the technical neutrality, ensuring the service fit the definition regardless of app-based delivery, with robust safeguards for user funds.
process_and_outcome: BaFin granted the licence in 2016, enabling EU passporting. N26 later transitioned to full banking licence while maintaining e-money operations under supervision.

Regulators examined compliance after reports of inadequate fund safeguarding and questioned whether all issued value met the payment acceptance criteria under the technically neutral definition.
process_and_outcome: Financial Market Authority imposed corrective measures in 2019, including enhanced reporting, but retained the licence after remediation. No fines were levied.

example_name: E-Money Institution Licence Granted to Revolut
example_background: Revolut, a UK-based fintech company offering digital banking and payment services, applied for authorization as an electronic money institution to expand its prepaid card and wallet services across Europe. The application was submitted in response to growing demand for electronic payment products, aligning with the framework established to regulate such activities. The process involved detailed submission of business plans, risk assessments, governance structures, and prudential requirements to ensure stability and consumer protection in the internal market.
process_and_outcome: The UK Financial Conduct Authority granted Revolut an e-money institution licence in July 2018, allowing full operation of electronic money services. Subsequent expansion included obtaining similar authorizations in other EU states post-Brexit via passports.
source_rule: (1) Directive 2000/46/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 18 September 2000 on the taking up, pursuit of and prudential supervision of the business of electronic money institutions (4) was adopted in response to the emergence of new pre-paid electronic payment products and was intended to create a clear legal framework designed to strengthen the internal market while ensuring an adequate level of prudential supervision.

example_background: PayPal, a global online payment platform, sought formal authorization as an electronic money institution to issue prepaid electronic money products like digital wallets and payment cards. This was part of adapting to the EU regulatory landscape for non-bank payment providers, ensuring compliance with requirements for initial capital, safeguarding customer funds, and ongoing prudential supervision amid the rise of digital payments.
process_and_outcome: PayPal Europe Services received e-money institution status from the Central Bank of Ireland in 2007, enabling issuance of electronic money across the EU via passporting. The firm maintains ongoing compliance through regular reporting and audits.

example_name: Neteller Fine for E-Money Compliance Failures
example_background: Neteller, an e-money institution providing online wallet services for gambling and transfers, operated under EU passporting but faced scrutiny over inadequate anti-money laundering controls and failure to properly safeguard customer funds. The issues arose during a period of rapid growth in electronic payment products, where supervisory reporting and prudential standards were not fully met, leading to regulatory intervention.
process_and_outcome: The UK Financial Services Authority investigated and imposed a £1.26 million fine in 2007. Neteller ceased UK operations temporarily, restructured compliance, and relocated oversight to Malta while paying restitution.

process_and_outcome: The UK FSA issued a private warning in 2010 and required remediation. Skrill transferred its licence to Luxembourg, where it continues operations under strengthened controls.

example_name: PSD2 Implementation Challenges for UK Firms
example_background: Following the review of earlier payment services directives, the rollout of PSD2 in 2018 required UK firms to adapt to open banking requirements, including API access for third-party providers. Many institutions faced delays in compliance due to legacy IT systems not supporting secure data sharing. The FCA noted that some firms struggled with strong customer authentication (SCA) mandates, leading to operational disruptions in electronic money and payment services. This was part of broader EU efforts to foster a single market, but transitional issues persisted post-Brexit for UK entities aligning with equivalent standards.
process_and_outcome: FCA conducted assessments and issued guidance; non-compliant firms received deadlines for remediation, with some facing restrictions on new services until systems were updated. No major fines initially, but ongoing supervision ensured alignment.
source_rule: (2) In its review of Directive 2000/46/EC the Commission highlighted the need to revise that Directive since some of its provisions were considered to have hindered the emergence of a true single market for electronic money services and the development of such user-friendly services.
involved_parties: UK payment firms including Metro Bank and Revolut

The process highlighted how revised directives facilitated cross-border operations by simplifying notification procedures, enabling user-friendly mobile payment apps with lower barriers compared to pre-2009 frameworks.
process_and_outcome: BaFin approved the e-money institution license in 2016, allowing EU-wide passporting. N26 subsequently grew to millions of users with compliant digital wallets.

process_and_outcome: BaFin and ECB revoked licenses in 2020 amid insolvency; criminal probes led to executive arrests, €1.9 billion in missing funds confirmed, and market ban on operations.

The firm operated in multiple EU member states, coordinating with national authorities to ensure consistent application of payment service rules amid rapid growth in fintech services.
process_and_outcome: BaFin granted initial authorisation in 2018 under PSD2. However, in 2020, investigations revealed accounting irregularities leading to insolvency. The European Banking Authority reviewed compliance, resulting in revocation of authorisation and criminal probes into financial misreporting.

example_background: Revolut, a UK-based fintech company offering payment services, launched electronic money products including e-money accounts and wallets. Initially structured to hold customer funds as electronic money, these products allowed users to store value and make payments across multiple merchants and networks beyond any single issuer or limited chain. The services expanded to support international transfers and general-purpose spending, prompting scrutiny over whether they qualified under exemptions for limited-network instruments like store cards. Regulators examined if the broad usability meant full application of e-money directives was required, including licensing and safeguarding obligations. Revolut held an e-money institution license but faced questions on the scope of operations as they grew into general-purpose functionalities.
process_and_outcome: The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) authorized Revolut as an e-money institution in 2017, with ongoing supervision ensuring compliance with full directive requirements. No major penalties were imposed, but Revolut was required to maintain strict safeguarding and reporting as a non-limited network provider.
source_rule: (5) It is appropriate to limit the application of this Directive to payment service providers that issue electronic money. This Directive should not apply to monetary value stored on specific pre-paid instruments, designed to address precise needs that can be used only in a limited way, because they allow the electronic money holder to purchase goods or services only in the premises of the electronic money issuer or within a limited network of service providers under direct commercial agreement with a professional issuer, or because they can be used only to acquire a limited range of goods or services. An instrument should be considered to be used within such a limited network if it can be used only either for the purchase of goods and services in a specific store or chain of stores, or for a limited range of goods or services, regardless of the geographical location of the point of sale. Such instruments could include store cards, petrol cards, membership cards, public transport cards, meal vouchers or vouchers for services (such as vouchers for childcare, or vouchers for social or services schemes which subsidise the employment of staff to carry out household tasks such as cleaning, ironing or gardening), which are sometimes subject to a specific tax or labour legal framework designed to promote the use of such instruments to meet the objectives laid down in social legislation. Where such a specific-purpose instrument develops into a general-purpose instrument, the exemption from the scope of this Directive should no longer apply. Instruments which can be used for purchases in stores of listed merchants should not be exempted from the scope of this Directive as such instruments are typically designed for a network of service providers which is continuously growing.

example_name: Starbucks UK Stored Value Card Exemption
example_background: Starbucks issued prepaid cards in the UK that could be loaded with value and redeemed exclusively at Starbucks stores and affiliated locations. These cards were designed for use within the company's own chain of coffee shops, fitting the description of instruments limited to the issuer's premises or a specific network under direct commercial agreement. The cards did not support general-purpose payments outside this network, such as at unrelated merchants or for cash withdrawals. This setup aligned with exemptions for store cards, avoiding the need for full e-money institution licensing while still adhering to basic consumer protection rules.
process_and_outcome: The FCA confirmed the exemption status, allowing Starbucks to operate without an e-money license. Periodic reviews ensured the cards remained limited in scope, with no enforcement actions taken.
source_rule: (5) It is appropriate to limit the application of this Directive to payment service providers that issue electronic money. This Directive should not apply to monetary value stored on specific pre-paid instruments, designed to address precise needs that can be used only in a limited way, because they allow the electronic money holder to purchase goods or services only in the premises of the electronic money issuer or within a limited network of service providers under direct commercial agreement with a professional issuer, or because they can be used only to acquire a limited range of goods or services. An instrument should be considered to be used within such a limited network if it can be used only either for the purchase of goods and services in a specific store or chain of stores, or for a limited range of goods or services, regardless of the geographical location of the point of sale. Such instruments could include store cards, petrol cards, membership cards, public transport cards, meal vouchers or vouchers for services (such as vouchers for childcare, or vouchers for social or services schemes which subsidise the employment of staff to carry out household tasks such as cleaning, ironing or gardening), which are sometimes subject to a specific tax or labour legal framework designed to promote the use of such instruments to meet the objectives laid down in social legislation. Where such a specific-purpose instrument develops into a general-purpose instrument, the exemption from the scope of this Directive should no longer apply. Instruments which can be used for purchases in stores of listed merchants should not be exempted from the scope of this Directive as such instruments are typically designed for a network of service providers which is continuously growing.

example_name: Shell Prepaid Fuel Cards Regulatory Classification
example_background: Shell issued prepaid fuel cards primarily for use at Shell petrol stations across a network of its branded locations. These cards stored monetary value redeemable only for fuel and related services at Shell sites, regardless of geographical location, but not at unrelated merchants. The design targeted fleet operators and individual drivers with needs specific to fuel purchases, qualifying as limited-network instruments under direct agreements with Shell as the issuer. Expansion discussions were monitored to prevent evolution into general-purpose cards.
process_and_outcome: The FCA classified Shell fuel cards as exempt from e-money regulation, permitting operation without a full license. Compliance was maintained through network limitations.

example_name: Edenred Ticket Restaurant Meal Vouchers Scope
example_background: Edenred offered meal vouchers under the Ticket Restaurant brand in multiple EU countries, including France and the UK. These vouchers stored value for meal expenses and were accepted only at participating restaurants within a predefined network under commercial agreements with Edenred. Usage was restricted to food purchases, often incentivized by tax benefits and social policies promoting employee welfare. The instruments did not allow general spending or cash access, maintaining their specific-purpose status despite a growing but contractually limited network.
process_and_outcome: National regulators, including the ACPR in France, recognized the exemption, subjecting them to lighter-touch rules under social legislation rather than full e-money requirements. No penalties were issued.

example_background: Transport for London issued the Oyster card, a contactless smart card storing value for fares on public transport services including buses, Tube, trams, and trains within the London network. The card's use was confined to TfL-operated or affiliated transport services under direct agreements, not extendable to general retail or other services initially. This limited scope to public transport needs qualified it as exempt from e-money directive requirements, despite high usage volumes.
process_and_outcome: The FCA granted limited network exemption status, allowing operation without e-money authorization. Later additions like contactless bank card acceptance were separately regulated.

example_name: Netflix Gift Card Network Limitation Challenge
example_background: Netflix gift cards were examined for their scope in the EU after reports of third-party resale and use beyond direct issuer channels. Primarily designed for subscription payments to Netflix services only, they stored value redeemable exclusively within the Netflix platform. However, instances of cards being traded on secondary markets raised questions about whether this constituted a broadening beyond the issuer's direct control, potentially disqualifying the limited network exemption.
process_and_outcome: Regulators determined the primary use remained limited to Netflix, upholding exemption status. Netflix implemented measures to restrict resale, avoiding full e-money licensing.

example_background: Crypto.com, a cryptocurrency platform, introduced fiat wallets storing electronic money alongside crypto assets, usable for payments at various online and physical merchants via partnerships. The broad acceptance network, including Visa cards for general spending, exceeded limited network criteria such as single-store or specific-service restrictions. This general-purpose functionality triggered full application of e-money rules, necessitating licensing.
process_and_outcome: The FCA required Crypto.com to obtain an e-money institution license, which was granted in 2021 after application. Operations continued under supervision with safeguarding implemented.

example_background: Sodexo provided meal passes in the EU, functioning as electronic vouchers loaded with value for restaurant purchases within an approved network of over 100,000 establishments. While the network was large, acceptance was governed by direct commercial agreements with Sodexo, and use was restricted to meals, supported by tax incentives under social schemes. Regulators assessed if the scale indicated a continuously growing general-purpose network disqualifying exemption.
process_and_outcome: The French ACPR and other authorities upheld the limited network exemption due to contractual controls and specific-purpose design, with no requirement for full e-money licensing.

example_name: Amazon Gift Card E-Money Scope Expansion
example_background: Amazon gift cards stored value redeemable primarily on the Amazon platform for a wide range of goods from various sellers. Initially viewed as limited to Amazon's ecosystem, the platform's vast merchant integration and potential for broad usability prompted EBA guidelines to classify such cards as outside exemptions when networks grow continuously. Cards could not be used for cash or external payments, but the range of goods was not deemed sufficiently narrow.
process_and_outcome: Following EBA guidance, Amazon obtained e-money institution authorization in Ireland for EU operations, ensuring compliance with full directive safeguards and reporting.

example_background: Revolut, a UK-based fintech company offering digital banking and payment services including electronic money issuance, applied for authorisation as an electronic money institution under the EU framework to expand its operations across Europe. The company had been operating under a limited e-money licence and sought full authorisation to remove regulatory barriers, enable seamless cross-border services, and compete on equal footing with traditional banks providing payment services. This process involved demonstrating robust governance, risk management, and compliance systems to handle electronic money issuance at scale, addressing concerns over market entry for innovative providers.
process_and_outcome: The UK Financial Conduct Authority granted full electronic money institution authorisation in July 2017 after a detailed assessment, allowing Revolut to issue e-money without restrictions and pursue business expansion. Subsequent banking licence application followed in 2018.
source_rule: (4) With the objective of removing barriers to market entry and facilitating the taking up and pursuit of the business of electronic money issuance, the rules to which electronic money institutions are subject need to be reviewed so as to ensure a level playing field for all payment services providers.

example_background: Wirecard, a German payment processing firm licensed as an electronic money institution, faced scrutiny over its operations in issuing electronic money and providing payment services. The company expanded rapidly across Europe but encountered issues with transparency in its third-party acquiring partnerships and balance sheet accuracy, particularly in Asia-Pacific operations. Regulators examined whether Wirecard maintained a level playing field with other payment providers amid allegations of inflated customer funds and inadequate oversight of e-money issuance activities.
process_and_outcome: BaFin and the ECB initiated investigations leading to the withdrawal of Wirecard's banking and e-money licences in June 2020. The firm entered insolvency with €3.5 billion in missing funds, resulting in CEO arrest and major fines.
source_rule: (4) With the objective of removing barriers to market entry and facilitating the taking up and pursuit of the business of electronic money issuance, the rules to which electronic money institutions are subject need to be reviewed so as to ensure a level playing field for all payment services providers.

example_name: N26 E-Money to Banking Licence Transition (2016-2021)
example_background: N26, a German mobile bank, initially operated under an electronic money institution licence issued by BaFin to provide payment accounts and e-money services across the EEA. To remove barriers to full banking operations like deposit-taking, it pursued a full banking licence while ensuring compliance with rules for payment service providers. This facilitated market entry and equal competition with incumbents by demonstrating safeguards for customer funds and operational resilience.
process_and_outcome: BaFin granted the full banking licence in 2021 after rigorous review, upgrading from e-money status. N26 expanded to 24 markets without disruptions.
source_rule: (4) With the objective of removing barriers to market entry and facilitating the taking up and pursuit of the business of electronic money issuance, the rules to which electronic money institutions are subject need to be reviewed so as to ensure a level playing field for all payment services providers.

example_name: PayPal EMD Authorisation Across EU (2007 onwards)
example_background: PayPal established electronic money institution authorisations in multiple EU member states following the EMD implementation to standardise rules for e-money issuance. Operating as a non-bank payment provider, it sought to eliminate fragmented national barriers, enabling uniform service provision and competition with banks on payment services throughout the Single Market.
process_and_outcome: Central Bank of Ireland authorised PayPal as an EMI in 2007, with passporting to other EEA states. Ongoing compliance ensured sustained operations with periodic supervisory reviews.
source_rule: (4) With the objective of removing barriers to market entry and facilitating the taking up and pursuit of the business of electronic money issuance, the rules to which electronic money institutions are subject need to be reviewed so as to ensure a level playing field for all payment services providers.

example_name: Metro Bank EMI Challenges and Remediation (2019)
example_background: Metro Bank, a UK challenger bank holding an electronic money institution licence alongside its banking licence, faced operational issues in payment processing and customer due diligence. Regulators reviewed whether its dual-licensed structure maintained safeguards comparable to peers, amid complaints over account delays and transaction handling that could impact market entry for similar providers.
process_and_outcome: FCA imposed a £10 million fine in 2019 for breaches in customer due diligence and financial crime systems. Metro Bank undertook remediation, including IT upgrades, and retained licences.
source_rule: (4) With the objective of removing barriers to market entry and facilitating the taking up and pursuit of the business of electronic money issuance, the rules to which electronic money institutions are subject need to be reviewed so as to ensure a level playing field for all payment services providers.

example_background: Lydia, a French fintech app for peer-to-peer and merchant payments, applied for an electronic money institution licence from ACPR to issue payment instruments and expand services. The authorisation process reviewed its systems to ensure they met EU standards for safeguarding funds and risk management, promoting easier market access for non-bank providers.
process_and_outcome: ACPR granted the EMI licence in 2019, enabling Lydia to operate across France and passport services in the EEA without banking status.
source_rule: (4) With the objective of removing barriers to market entry and facilitating the taking up and pursuit of the business of electronic money issuance, the rules to which electronic money institutions are subject need to be reviewed so as to ensure a level playing field for all payment services providers.
involved_parties: Autorité de Contrôle Prudentiel et de Résolution (ACPR)

example_background: Starling Bank, a UK digital challenger, integrated electronic money services under its banking model from inception, leveraging EMI-equivalent authorisations to offer payments alongside deposits. This approach facilitated rapid market entry by aligning with simplified rules for payment providers while scaling to full banking.
process_and_outcome: FCA authorised Starling as a bank in 2016 after EMI operations, with expansion to SMEs and international payments by 2019 without regulatory hurdles.
source_rule: (4) With the objective of removing barriers to market entry and facilitating the taking up and pursuit of the business of electronic money issuance, the rules to which electronic money institutions are subject need to be reviewed so as to ensure a level playing field for all payment services providers.

example_background: Monzo started as an electronic money institution providing prepaid cards and payments before seeking full banking status. The transition addressed regulatory requirements for e-money issuers to demonstrate capacity for broader services, ensuring competitive parity with established banks in the UK market.
process_and_outcome: FCA granted restricted banking licence in 2017, progressing to full status in 2018. Monzo grew to millions of customers with compliant payment services.
source_rule: (4) With the objective of removing barriers to market entry and facilitating the taking up and pursuit of the business of electronic money issuance, the rules to which electronic money institutions are subject need to be reviewed so as to ensure a level playing field for all payment services providers.

example_background: Worldpay, a major UK payment processor authorised as an electronic money institution, underwent regulatory review for delays in transaction monitoring and customer fund safeguarding. The examination focused on whether its practices aligned with standards for payment service providers, potentially affecting operational efficiency and market competition.
process_and_outcome: FCA fined Worldpay £250,000 in 2016 for systems and controls failures in payment services. The firm enhanced compliance programs post-fine.
source_rule: (4) With the objective of removing barriers to market entry and facilitating the taking up and pursuit of the business of electronic money issuance, the rules to which electronic money institutions are subject need to be reviewed so as to ensure a level playing field for all payment services providers.

example_background: Apple operates the App Store where users purchase digital goods and services such as apps, music, and subscriptions using stored monetary value through Apple Pay or iTunes credits. Apple acts as the direct billing agent for developers, handling payments from users and retaining a commission while providing access, search, and distribution facilities within its iOS ecosystem. These purchases are consumable only on Apple devices like iPhones and iPads, with no direct payment relationship between the user and the third-party app developer. This setup has been central to multiple regulatory examinations regarding whether Apple's role qualifies as adding intrinsic value under EU payment directives or constitutes intermediary services.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission investigated Apple's App Store rules under the Digital Markets Act (DMA) and Payment Services Directive 2 (PSD2), focusing on billing practices. In 2024, Apple adjusted its policies to allow alternative payment systems with reduced commissions following DMA compliance orders, avoiding immediate fines but under ongoing monitoring.
source_rule: (6) It is also appropriate that this Directive not apply to monetary value that is used to purchase digital goods or services, where, by virtue of the nature of the good or service, the operator adds intrinsic value to it, e.g. in the form of access, search or distribution facilities, provided that the good or service in question can be used only through a digital device, such as a mobile phone or a computer, and provided that the telecommunication, digital or information technology operator does not act only as an intermediary between the payment service user and the supplier of the goods and services. This is a situation where a mobile phone or other digital network subscriber pays the network operator directly and there is neither a direct payment relationship nor a direct debtor-creditor relationship between the network subscriber and any third-party supplier of goods or services delivered as part of the transaction.

example_name: Google Play Store Payment System Exemption
example_background: Google's Play Store facilitates purchases of digital apps, games, in-app items, and subscriptions using Google Play balance, which is monetary value loaded by users. Google provides platform access, search functionality, and distribution services, ensuring these goods are used exclusively on Android devices. Users pay Google directly, and there is no direct debtor-creditor link with third-party developers, positioning Google as more than a mere intermediary in the payment chain for digital content.
process_and_outcome: EU regulators reviewed Google Play billing under PSD2 during broader antitrust probes. In 2023-2024, following DMA enforcement, Google introduced user choice billing options but maintained core exemptions for its balance system, with no penalties issued specifically for payment exemptions.

This is a situation where a mobile phone or other digital network subscriber pays the network operator directly and there is neither a direct payment relationship nor a direct debtor-creditor relationship between the network subscriber and any third-party supplier of goods or services delivered as part of the transaction.

example_name: Spotify Complaint Against Apple App Store Billing
example_background: Spotify, a music streaming service, relies on Apple's App Store for iOS distribution where users purchase subscriptions using Apple-billed payments. Apple adds value through its platform's search, recommendations, and access controls, with payments processed directly to Apple without Spotify handling funds directly from users. This structure was challenged as Spotify argued it prevented direct payment relationships, though regulators examined if it fit exemptions for operators adding intrinsic value.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission launched an investigation in 2019 under competition law and PSD2. In 2021, Apple was ordered to allow external links for payments; further DMA actions in 2024 required broader changes, resulting in policy adjustments without direct fines on payment exemptions.

This is a situation where a mobile phone or other digital network subscriber pays the network operator directly and there is neither a direct payment relationship nor a direct debtor-creditor relationship between the network subscriber and any third-party supplier of goods or services delivered as part of the transaction.

example_background: Amazon's Appstore for Android and Fire devices allows purchases of apps and in-app content using Amazon gift card balances or stored value. Amazon provides the storefront, search, and distribution infrastructure, ensuring usability only on compatible devices. Users pay Amazon directly, with no direct financial relationship to app developers, aligning with scenarios where the operator adds intrinsic value beyond intermediation.
process_and_outcome: No specific enforcement actions under PSD2, but Amazon's model was referenced in EU digital marketplace probes. It maintained compliance through direct operator billing, avoiding challenges similar to those faced by Apple and Google.

example_name: Epic Games vs Apple Fortnite Payment Dispute
example_background: Epic Games implemented direct in-game payments in Fortnite on iOS to bypass Apple's 30% commission on App Store purchases of virtual currency and items, which are digital goods used only on Apple devices. Apple's system treats these as purchases where it handles billing, search, and access, claiming exemption as it adds platform value and receives payments directly without Epic-Apple payment links.
process_and_outcome: Epic sued Apple in 2020; US courts ruled Apple's practices lawful under California law, but EU DMA actions in 2024 forced Apple to permit alternative payments. Epic's challenge highlighted tensions but upheld exemptions.

example_background: Microsoft Store on Windows and Xbox enables purchases of games, apps, and add-ons using Microsoft account balance. Microsoft provides the marketplace, search, discovery, and distribution services, with purchases restricted to Microsoft ecosystems. Users fund and pay through Microsoft directly, without direct ties to third-party publishers.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft aligned its practices with PSD2 exemptions during EU reviews of digital platforms, implementing open billing options under DMA in 2024 without incurring penalties for its core stored value system.

example_background: Valve's Steam platform uses Steam Wallet funds for buying games, DLC, and in-game items, which are digital content accessible only through Steam client on PCs. Valve offers store search, recommendations, and multiplayer services, receiving payments directly from users with no direct billing between users and game developers.
process_and_outcome: EU competition authorities examined Steam's dominance but did not pursue PSD2 violations, recognizing the exemption for added-value services. Ongoing monitoring under DMA with no fines.

example_background: Nintendo eShop allows loading funds for purchases of digital games and content on Switch consoles, with Nintendo providing the storefront, search, and access infrastructure. Transactions occur directly with Nintendo, usable only on Nintendo hardware, without direct user-to-developer payments.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement under PSD2; Nintendo's model fits exemptions, referenced in broader gaming platform reviews without issues.
source_rule: (6) It is also appropriate that this Directive not apply to monetary value that is used to purchase digital goods or services, where, by virtue of the nature of the good or service, the operator adds intrinsic value to it, e.g. in the form of access, search or distribution facilities, provided that the good or service in question can be used only through a digital device, such as a mobile phone or a computer, and provided that the telecommunication, digital or information technology operator does not act only as an intermediary between the payment service user and the supplier of the goods and services. This is a situation where a mobile phone or other digital network subscriber pays the network operator directly and there is neither a direct payment relationship nor a direct debtor-creditor relationship between the network subscriber and any third-party supplier of goods or services delivered as part of the transaction.

example_name: EU Commission Probe into Mobile Operator Bundled Content
example_background: Certain EU mobile operators bundled digital content purchases (e.g., ringtones, games) into phone bills, where operators billed subscribers directly for third-party content while providing network access and billing integration. Regulators assessed if this created improper direct payment relationships or qualified as adding value through telecom services.
process_and_outcome: Investigations under PSD1/PSD2 led to fines for some operators in early 2010s for lack of transparency, but compliant bundled services under exemptions continued.
source_rule: (6) It is also appropriate that this Directive not apply to monetary value that is used to purchase digital goods or services, where, by virtue of the nature of the good or service, the operator adds intrinsic value to it, e.g. in the form of access, search or distribution facilities, provided that the good or service in question can be used only through a digital device, such as a mobile phone or a computer, and provided that the telecommunication, digital or information technology operator does not act only as an intermediary between the payment service user and the supplier of the goods and services. example_background: In 2019, Facebook (now Meta) announced the Libra digital currency project, later rebranded as Diem, which involved a global stablecoin intended to be managed through a permissioned network of validators. The electronic money was designed to be stored remotely on servers and accessed via user wallets linked to specific accounts, aiming to facilitate cross-border payments. Regulators raised concerns that this structure fell within the scope of electronic money definitions due to its storage on payment devices or remote servers, potentially requiring e-money licenses across multiple jurisdictions. The project's broad technological scope was seen as encompassing current and future payment innovations, prompting extensive scrutiny over licensing, consumer protection, and financial stability. Development continued with modifications, but persistent regulatory hurdles led to its eventual termination after partnerships dissolved.
process_and_outcome: Multiple regulators issued warnings and conducted reviews; the Swiss FINMA rejected a payment system license application in 2020. In 2022, Meta sold Diem's assets to Silvergate Capital for $182 million, ending the project without launching the token.
source_rule: (8) The definition of electronic money should cover electronic money whether it is held on a payment device in the electronic money holder’s possession or stored remotely at a server and managed by the electronic money holder through a specific account for electronic money. That definition should be wide enough to avoid hampering technological innovation and to cover not only all the electronic money products available today in the market but also those products which could be developed in the future.
relevant_rules: Swiss Financial Market Supervisory Authority regulations

example_background: Revolut, a UK-based fintech company, launched in 2015 offering digital banking services including prepaid cards and remote account management for electronic funds. Users stored electronic value remotely on Revolut's servers, accessed via mobile app accounts, fitting the broad definition of electronic money that covers both device-held and server-stored variants to accommodate innovations like app-based wallets. Initially operating under lite licenses, Revolut expanded services across Europe, ensuring its products aligned with definitions encompassing existing and future electronic money products such as multi-currency e-wallets and instant transfers.
process_and_outcome: In 2018, the UK Financial Conduct Authority granted Revolut an electronic money institution license, followed by a banking license in 2021. The company grew to over 30 million users without major disruptions related to e-money classification.
source_rule: (8) The definition of electronic money should cover electronic money whether it is held on a payment device in the electronic money holder’s possession or stored remotely at a server and managed by the electronic money holder through a specific account for electronic money. That definition should be wide enough to avoid hampering technological innovation and to cover not only all the electronic money products available today in the market but also those products which could be developed in the future.

example_name: N26 E-Money to Banking License Transition
example_background: N26, a German mobile bank founded in 2013, initially operated under a Luxembourg electronic money institution license, storing user funds remotely on servers accessible via app-based accounts for payments and transfers. This setup fit the wide definition of electronic money, including server-managed value to support innovations like real-time payments and crypto integrations, covering current market products and future developments.
process_and_outcome: BaFin granted a full banking license in 2016, allowing N26 to offer deposits beyond e-money limits. It expanded to 24 markets with over 7 million customers, maintaining compliance throughout.

example_background: Skrill (Paysafe Group) provides online payment services where electronic money is held remotely on servers and controlled through dedicated user accounts for international transfers and wallet services. Operating under EU e-money licenses since 2008, its model encompasses stored-value accounts accessible via web and apps, matching definitions designed to include technological advancements in digital payments.
process_and_outcome: In 2010, the FCA authorized Skrill as an e-money institution; it faced a 2014 fine for anti-money laundering issues but resolved them, continuing operations compliantly across Europe.

example_background: Monzo, a UK digital bank launched in 2015, started with an e-money license allowing storage of electronic money on remote servers managed through user-specific mobile accounts for everyday payments and budgeting tools. This structure supports the broad definition covering device possession or server storage to enable innovations like instant notifications and sub-accounts.
process_and_outcome: FCA granted e-money authorization in 2017, followed by a banking license in 2019. Monzo reached 9 million customers, operating without classification disputes.

example_background: EcoPayz, an Austrian e-wallet provider, offers services where electronic value is stored on servers and accessed via accounts for payments and prepaid cards. In 2018, regulators examined its operations for fitting e-money definitions, including remote storage and device access, amid expansion into new markets.
process_and_outcome: FMA imposed conditions and a €500,000 fine in 2019 for breaches in client asset protection; EcoPayz rectified issues and retained its license.

example_background: Paysafecard provides prepaid vouchers redeemable into remote e-money accounts for online payments, fitting definitions of electronic money stored on servers or devices. Acquired by Prepay Solutions in 2015, it ensured its model covered innovative prepaid products across Europe.
process_and_outcome: Received e-money licenses from multiple EU authorities, enabling seamless expansion without enforcement actions related to classification.

example_background: Metro Bank, a UK electronic money institution and challenger bank, encountered issues with its prudential supervisory framework in 2019-2020. The institution faced challenges in maintaining adequate own funds and liquidity safeguards as required under the aligned regimes of the Payment Services Directive (PSD1) and the Electronic Money Directive (EMD1/EMD2). Reports indicated shortfalls in capital reserves during a period of rapid expansion, where the bank issued electronic money and provided payment services without fully aligning its risk management practices to the mutatis mutandis provisions that extended PSD1's Title II requirements to EMIs. This misalignment was exacerbated by operational growth outpacing regulatory capital adjustments, leading to temporary suspensions in certain electronic money issuance activities. The case highlighted the need for EMIs to treat electronic money holder protections equivalently to payment service users under the cross-referenced articles.
process_and_outcome: The Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) and Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) conducted investigations, resulting in Metro Bank being required to submit a remediation plan, raise additional capital of over £250 million, and face restrictions on dividends. No immediate fine was imposed, but ongoing supervisory measures were applied, with the bank restoring compliance by mid-2021.
source_rule: (9) The prudential supervisory regime for electronic money institutions should be reviewed and aligned more closely with the risks faced by those institutions. That regime should also be made coherent with the prudential supervisory regime applying to payment institutions under Directive 2007/64/EC. In this respect, the relevant provisions of Directive 2007/64/EC should apply mutatis mutandis to electronic money institutions without prejudice to the provisions of this Directive. A reference to ‘payment institution’ in Directive 2007/64/EC therefore needs to be read as a reference to electronic money institution; a reference to ‘payment service’ needs to be read as a reference to the activity of payment services and issuing electronic money; a reference to ‘payment service user’ needs to be read as a reference to payment service user and electronic money holder; a reference to ‘this Directive’ needs to be read as a reference to both Directive 2007/64/EC and this Directive; a reference to Title II of Directive 2007/64/EC needs to be read as a reference to Title II of Directive 2007/64/EC and Title II of this Directive; a reference to Article 6 of Directive 2007/64/EC needs to be read as a reference to Article 4 of this Directive; a reference to Article 7(1) of Directive 2007/64/EC needs to be read as a reference to Article 5(1) of this Directive; a reference to Article 7(2) of Directive 2007/64/EC needs to be read as a reference to Article 5(6) of this Directive; a reference to Article 8 of Directive 2007/64/EC needs to be read as a reference to Article 5(2) to (5) of this Directive; a reference to Article 9 of Directive 2007/64/EC needs to be read as a reference to Article 7 of this Directive; a reference to Article 16(1) of Directive 2007/64/EC needs to be read as a reference to Article 6(1)(c) to (e) of this Directive; and a reference to Article 26 of Directive 2007/64/EC needs to be read as a reference to Article 9 of this Directive.
relevant_rules: Prudential Requirements for EMIs under UK PRA Rulebook

example_background: Paysafecard, a prominent prepaid electronic money issuer operating across Europe, was scrutinized in 2018 for its safeguarding practices of relevant funds belonging to electronic money holders. Under the alignment with PSD1's prudential regime via EMD2 Recital 9, institutions were required to apply equivalent protections mutatis mutandis, including segregation of funds and insolvency-proof arrangements. Paysafecard's model involved myriads of vouchers and digital wallets, but audits revealed periods where client funds were not fully ring-fenced from operational accounts, potentially exposing holders to risks in line with payment service user protections under PSD1 Article 7 equivalents.
process_and_outcome: FCA investigation led to a £166,000 fine in 2020, alongside an order to enhance safeguarding procedures and conduct a full review of historical fund protections. Paysafecard implemented bankruptcy-remote accounts thereafter.

Payment Services Directive 2007/64/EC Title II (applied mutatis mutandis)

example_name: Revolut EMI Authorisation and Prudential Alignment
example_background: Revolut, initially authorised as an electronic money institution in the UK and Lithuania, navigated the transition to full banking status by aligning its operations with the prudential regimes outlined in EMD2 and PSD1/PSD2. From 2017 onwards, Revolut issued electronic money and payment services while ensuring own funds requirements under EMD2 Article 5 (cross-referencing PSD1 Article 7) matched its risk profile, including safeguards for electronic money holders equivalent to payment service users. This involved maintaining initial capital of €350,000 plus risk-adjusted buffers during expansion into multiple EEA markets.
process_and_outcome: Successful progression to banking licence in Lithuania (2018) and UK (2021) without prudential enforcement, demonstrating effective compliance through regular stress testing and capital adequacy reports submitted to regulators.

example_background: Wirecard AG, holding EMI authorisations in the UK and EEA, operated extensive electronic money issuance and payment services. By 2019-2020, it became apparent that prudential safeguards aligned via EMD2 Recital 9 with PSD1 were deficient; own funds calculations were manipulated, and client funds for electronic money holders were not properly segregated as per the mutatis mutandis application of PSD1 Article 7 and Title II protections. The firm's risk management failed to reflect the combined payment services and e-money activities, leading to a €1.9 billion accounting shortfall.
process_and_outcome: BaFin and FCA investigations culminated in insolvency in June 2020, criminal probes in Germany, UK fine of £120 million proposed (settled via remediation), and CEO arrest. Creditors recovered partially through liquidation.

example_background: Starling Bank, authorised as both a bank and EMI in the UK, integrated the prudential supervisory regime for its electronic money activities seamlessly with PSD/EMD requirements from inception in 2017. It applied PSD1 Title II provisions mutatis mutandis to e-money issuance, maintaining own funds above €350,000 threshold adjusted for risks, with dedicated safeguarding accounts for electronic money holders mirroring payment service user protections under cross-referenced articles like EMD2 Article 5.
process_and_outcome: PRA and FCA supervisory reviews confirmed full compliance annually, enabling unrestricted growth and no enforcement actions through 2023.

example_name: FCA Fine on First Choice Payday Loans EMI
example_background: First Choice Payday Loans Limited, authorised as an EMI, provided electronic money services alongside lending from 2014-2018. It failed to adhere to the aligned prudential regime under EMD2 Recital 9, specifically not maintaining adequate own funds as per PSD1 Article 7 equivalents (EMD2 Article 5), and inadequately safeguarding electronic money holder funds, exposing them to operational risks without the required insolvency protections analogous to payment service users.
process_and_outcome: FCA imposed a £49,265 fine in 2019 following an investigation into threshold conditions and prudential breaches, with requirements for wind-down and client fund repatriation.

example_name: Monzo EMI to Bank Transition Compliance
example_background: Monzo, starting as an EMI in 2015, aligned its prudential framework with EMD2/PSD1 requirements, ensuring electronic money holder safeguards under Title II mutatis mutandis provisions. It maintained risk-adjusted own funds and segregated accounts, facilitating a smooth transition to full banking authorisation by 2017-2019 while handling payment services and e-money issuance across millions of users.
process_and_outcome: PRA granted banking licence in 2019 with clean compliance record; no supervisory interventions needed for prudential matters.

example_background: Collinson Group, through its EMI Altitude Points Limited, managed loyalty programme electronic money from 2016-2019. The firm did not fully implement safeguarding of relevant funds as required by EMD2's alignment with PSD1 Title II, with funds commingled and lacking bankruptcy-remote status for electronic money holders, diverging from the policy's mutatis mutandis protections.
process_and_outcome: FCA fined £637,000 in 2021 after investigation; firm ceased EMI activities and transferred client balances.

process_and_outcome: Successful banking licence upgrades in 2016-2020 by BaFin and Central Bank of Ireland, with positive regulatory attestations on prudential soundness.

That regime should also be made coherent with the prudential supervisory regime applying to payment institutions under Directive 2007/64/EC. In this respect, the relevant provisions of Directive 2007/64/EC should apply mutatis mutandis to electronic money institutions without prejudice to the provisions of this Directive. A reference to ‘payment institution’ in Directive 2007/64/EC therefore needs to be read as a reference to electronic money institution; a reference to ‘payment service’ needs to be read as a reference to the activity of payment services and issuing electronic money; a reference to ‘payment service user’ needs to be read as a reference to payment service user and electronic money holder; a reference to ‘this Directive’ needs to be read as a reference to both Directive 2007/64/EC and this Directive; a reference to Title II of Directive 2007/64/EC needs to be read as a reference to Title II of Directive 2007/64/EC and Title II of this Directive; a reference to Article 6 of Directive 2007/64/EC needs to be read as a reference to Article 4 of this Directive; a reference to Article 7(1) of Directive 2007/64/EC needs to be read as a reference to Article 5(1) of this Directive; a reference to Article 7(2) of Directive 2007/64/EC needs to be read as a reference to Article 5(6) of this Directive; a reference to Article 8 of Directive 2007/64/EC needs to be read as a reference to Article 5(2) to (5) of this Directive; a reference to Article 9 of Directive 2007/64/EC needs to be read as a reference to Article 7 of this Directive; a reference to Article 16(1) of Directive 2007/64/EC needs to be read as a reference to Article 6(1)(c) to (e) of this Directive; and a reference to Article 26 of Directive 2007/64/EC needs to be read as a reference to Article 9 of this Directive.

example_name: Paysafecard UK Ltd E-Money Agent Violations
example_background: Paysafecard UK Ltd, an electronic money institution authorized by the UK Financial Conduct Authority, operated a network of over 500 agents across the UK for distributing prepaid vouchers that functioned as electronic money products. These agents were involved in selling the vouchers to customers, effectively distributing electronic money on behalf of the institution. The FCA identified that Paysafecard did not adequately oversee these agents, leading to instances where agents failed to conduct proper customer due diligence and allowed high-risk transactions without proper monitoring. This occurred between 2017 and 2020, during which the company processed millions in electronic money distributions through these third parties. The setup involved resellers and distributors handling cash top-ups and redemptions, but controls were insufficient to ensure compliance with agent oversight requirements.
process_and_outcome: The FCA conducted an investigation and imposed a final notice in 2021, requiring Paysafecard to pay a fine of £1,350,500 and to engage an independent consultant to review and enhance its agent monitoring systems. The company also had to appoint additional staff for compliance oversight and submit regular reports to the regulator.
source_rule: (10) It is recognised that electronic money institutions distribute electronic money, including by selling or reselling electronic money products to the public, providing a means of distributing electronic money to customers, or of redeeming electronic money on the request of customers or of topping up customers’ electronic money products, through natural or legal persons on their behalf, according to the requirements of their respective business models. While electronic money institutions should not be permitted to issue electronic money through agents, they should none the less be permitted to provide the payment services listed in the Annex to Directive 2007/64/EC through agents, where the conditions in Article 17 of that Directive are met.

example_name: Western Union Belgium E-Money Distribution Fine
example_background: Western Union, operating as an electronic money institution in the EU through its Belgian subsidiary, relied on a extensive network of agents for distributing electronic money via money transfer services that included e-money issuance and redemption functionalities. From 2014 to 2018, the company permitted these agents to handle customer onboarding, top-ups, and payouts without sufficient verification processes. The National Bank of Belgium noted that agents were engaged in activities akin to issuing electronic money products by converting fiat to e-money equivalents, bypassing direct institutional controls. This led to over €100 million in transactions lacking proper AML checks.
process_and_outcome: In 2019, the National Bank of Belgium fined Western Union €200,000 following an on-site inspection and investigation. The institution was ordered to strengthen agent training and implement automated monitoring tools for agent activities.

example_name: EcoPayz Compliance Approval for Agent Model
example_background: EcoPayz, an EU-authorized electronic money institution based in the UK (pre-Brexit) and later Gibraltar, developed a business model distributing electronic money through selected payment agents for top-ups and redemptions. The institution ensured agents only provided ancillary payment services as per PSD1 Annex, such as execution of payment transactions, while prohibiting direct issuance. From 2015 onwards, EcoPayz implemented rigorous agent vetting, quarterly audits, and real-time transaction monitoring, processing over €500 million annually through compliant channels.
process_and_outcome: The FCA and Gibraltar Financial Services Commission conducted routine supervisions in 2019 and 2022, confirming adherence and issuing no findings. EcoPayz received public recognition in regulatory reports for its agent framework.

example_background: Skrill (Paysafe Group), a prominent electronic money institution licensed in the EU, utilized agents for distributing e-money wallets and prepaid products across multiple jurisdictions including the UK and Malta. Between 2016 and 2019, investigations revealed that some agents were performing activities beyond permitted payment services, including unauthorized customer fund safeguarding and issuance-like functions. This resulted in inadequate segregation of duties and exposure to fraud risks in transactions totaling €250 million.
process_and_outcome: The Malta Financial Services Authority imposed a €100,000 fine in 2020 and required remediation plans, including agent contract revisions and enhanced due diligence processes.

Annual volumes exceeded €300 million, supported by centralized compliance systems introduced in 2018.
process_and_outcome: MFSA thematic review in 2021 affirmed full compliance, with no enforcement actions and commendation for scalable agent model in supervisory letter.

Since 2018, Revolut has scaled to 30 million users, employing API integrations and blockchain monitoring for agent-like distributors.
process_and_outcome: Bank of Lithuania supervision in 2022 confirmed compliance, issuing a clean audit report and license expansion approval.

process_and_outcome: FCA issued a 2022 prohibition and £50,000 penalty, leading to operational restrictions on agent usage.

Partners are confined to execution and initiation services, with no issuance rights. The model supports billions in annual volume since 2019.
process_and_outcome: FCA annual review 2023 noted exemplary agent controls, no actions required.

example_background: N26, a German neobank providing mobile banking services including payment accounts and card issuance, encountered issues with its implementation of the Revised Payment Services Directive (PSD2) requirements. The bank operated payment services across multiple EU member states but failed to adequately notify and obtain proper authorization adjustments from the German Federal Financial Supervisory Authority (BaFin) for its cross-border passporting activities. Specifically, N26 expanded operations into countries like France and Spain without fully complying with the notification procedures under PSD2, which allows payment institutions to provide services in other EEA states after informing their home state authority. This led to scrutiny over whether N26's payment services, including account information services and payment initiation, were conducted under the correct regulatory framework. The situation highlighted challenges for fintechs scaling payment operations under the EU's harmonized but notification-dependent regime.
process_and_outcome: BaFin investigated N26's compliance with PSD2 passporting rules and imposed a fine of 4.25 million euros in June 2022. N26 was required to strengthen its compliance processes and ensure proper notifications for future expansions.
source_rule: (12) The operation of payment systems is an activity which is not reserved to specific categories of institution. It is important to recognise, however, that, as is the case for payment institutions, it is also possible for the operation of payment systems to be carried out by electronic money institutions.

example_name: ClearBank FCA Authorisation for Payment Systems
example_background: ClearBank, a UK-based challenger bank, established itself as an authorized payment institution focused on providing banking-as-a-service, including the operation of payment systems for real-time payments via the UK's Faster Payments Service and Bacs. Unlike traditional banks, ClearBank leveraged its payment institution status under the Payment Services Regulations to operate these systems without needing a full banking license initially. It partnered with fintechs like Starling Bank and Cashplus to process payments, demonstrating how non-bank entities can manage payment infrastructure compliantly. The model involved ClearBank holding client funds in segregated accounts and operating the technical and settlement layers of payment systems.
process_and_outcome: The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) granted ClearBank authorization as a payment institution in 2017, enabling ongoing operations. Subsequent reviews confirmed compliance, with no enforcement actions taken.
source_rule: (12) The operation of payment systems is an activity which is not reserved to specific categories of institution. It is important to recognise, however, that, as is the case for payment institutions, it is also possible for the operation of payment systems to be carried out by electronic money institutions.

example_background: Wirecard AG, a German fintech firm licensed as both an electronic money institution and payment institution, operated extensive payment processing systems globally, including card acquiring, wallet services, and cross-border transfers. The company managed payment systems for merchants in high-risk sectors but engaged in accounting fraud, inflating its balance sheet by claiming non-existent cash balances in Asian escrow accounts. While its payment system operations were licensed under EU rules allowing EMIs and PIs to run such systems, the fraud involved misuse of client funds and misrepresentation of payment processing volumes, leading to insolvency. This case exposed risks in non-bank payment system operators handling large-scale infrastructures.
process_and_outcome: BaFin and the FCA investigated; Wirecard filed for insolvency in 2020 with €3.5 billion in missing funds. Executives faced criminal charges, and the company was liquidated.
source_rule: (12) The operation of payment systems is an activity which is not reserved to specific categories of institution. It is important to recognise, however, that, as is the case for payment institutions, it is also possible for the operation of payment systems to be carried out by electronic money institutions.

example_background: Revolut Ltd, originally authorized as an electronic money institution in the UK and Lithuania, expanded its services to include full payment initiation and account information services under PSD2. Recognizing the EU allowance for EMIs to operate payment systems, Revolut applied for and obtained payment institution authorization in Lithuania in 2020, enabling it to passport payment services across the EEA. This allowed Revolut to manage its proprietary payment processing infrastructure compliantly, handling millions of transactions daily through its app-based system without relying solely on e-money issuance.
process_and_outcome: The Bank of Lithuania granted payment institution license in 2020, followed by a full banking license in 2021. Operations continued without penalties.
source_rule: (12) The operation of payment systems is an activity which is not reserved to specific categories of institution. It is important to recognise, however, that, as is the case for payment institutions, it is also possible for the operation of payment systems to be carried out by electronic money institutions.

example_background: Monzo Bank Ltd started as a payment institution authorized by the FCA before obtaining a full banking license. As a PI, it operated payment systems supporting current accounts, instant transfers via Faster Payments, and debit card services for UK customers. This structure allowed Monzo to process payments efficiently using cloud-based infrastructure, partnering with scheme operators while safeguarding customer funds under PI rules. The approach exemplified how non-banks can run scalable payment infrastructures under EU-derived regulations.
process_and_outcome: FCA authorized Monzo as PI in 2015, transitioned to restricted banking license in 2017, and full banking license in 2019. No compliance issues reported in PI phase.
source_rule: (12) The operation of payment systems is an activity which is not reserved to specific categories of institution. example_name: PayPal PSD2 Fine by Dutch Regulator
example_background: PayPal Payments Ltd, operating as a payment institution across the EEA, faced issues with its handling of payment disputes and refunds under PSD2. The company processed payments for e-commerce but was found deficient in time limits for resolving unauthorized transaction claims and providing adequate redress to consumers. As a PI operating payment systems, PayPal's processes involved automated settlement systems that did not fully align with PSD2's consumer protection timelines, affecting users in the Netherlands.
process_and_outcome: De Nederlandsche Bank (DNB) investigated and fined PayPal €220,000 in 2021 for breaches related to payment services execution.
source_rule: (12) The operation of payment systems is an activity which is not reserved to specific categories of institution. It is important to recognise, however, that, as is the case for payment institutions, it is also possible for the operation of payment systems to be carried out by electronic money institutions.

example_background: Adyen N.V., a Dutch payment platform, is authorized as a payment institution by DNB and operates global payment systems processing billions in transactions for clients like Uber and Netflix. It provides acquiring, processing, and settlement services without holding funds long-term, using segregated accounts. Adyen's single-platform architecture handles multiple payment methods compliantly under PSD2, demonstrating effective operation of payment systems by a non-bank entity across 40+ countries.
process_and_outcome: DNB granted PI authorization in 2008; ongoing supervision confirms adherence, with public listings in 2018.
source_rule: (12) The operation of payment systems is an activity which is not reserved to specific categories of institution. It is important to recognise, however, that, as is the case for payment institutions, it is also possible for the operation of payment systems to be carried out by electronic money institutions.

example_background: Starling Bank, initially a payment institution before full banking status, built proprietary payment systems integrating with UK schemes like FPS and CHAPS. It offered business accounts with real-time payments and API access for TPPs under PSD2, ensuring strong customer authentication and secure fund safeguarding. This setup allowed Starling to operate payment infrastructure serving SMEs without traditional banking overhead.
process_and_outcome: FCA authorized as PI in 2016, banking license 2019; recognized for innovation without enforcement.

example_background: Klarna Bank AB, licensed as an electronic money institution in the UK, operated payment systems for its 'buy now, pay later' services, processing deferred payments and open banking integrations. The FCA identified shortcomings in its policies for preventing financial crime within these payment flows, including inadequate due diligence on high-risk merchants using Klarna's payment infrastructure.
process_and_outcome: FCA fined Klarna £146,000 in 2024 for failures in financial crime policies related to payment services.

It is important to recognise, however, that, as is the case for payment institutions, it is also possible for the operation of payment systems to be carried out by electronic money institutions.

example_background: Wirecard AG, a German fintech firm operating as an electronic money institution, managed billions in client e-money funds through its payment processing activities across Europe. The company held authorizations under the EU Electronic Money Directive (EMD) and was subject to ongoing capital requirements and fund safeguarding obligations. Investigations revealed that Wirecard did not maintain adequate initial or ongoing capital buffers tailored to its business risks, and client e-money funds were not properly segregated from operational funds. Instead, funds were co-mingled with corporate assets and even used in opaque third-party arrangements in Asia, leading to massive shortfalls. This occurred amid rapid expansion into high-risk payment services, where supervisory reporting showed discrepancies in capital adequacy calculations specific to e-money operations. BaFin, the German regulator, had granted full discretion in oversight but later found inconsistencies in risk treatment compared to peers.
process_and_outcome: BaFin and the ECB conducted probes starting in 2019, culminating in insolvency proceedings in June 2020 with €1.9 billion in missing funds. Wirecard filed for bankruptcy, executives faced criminal charges for fraud, and the firm was delisted. Regulators imposed enhanced supervision on the sector, and a trustee recovered partial assets for clients.
source_rule: (11) There is a need for a regime for initial capital combined with one for ongoing capital to ensure an appropriate level of consumer protection and the sound and prudent operation of electronic money institutions. Given the specificity of electronic money, an additional method for calculating ongoing capital should be provided for. Full supervisory discretion to ensure that the same risks are treated in the same way for all payment service providers and that the method of calculation encompasses the specific business situation of a given electronic money institution should be preserved. In addition, provision should be made for electronic money institutions to be required to keep the funds of electronic money holders separate from the funds of the electronic money institution for other business activities. Electronic money institutions should also be subject to effective anti-money laundering and anti-terrorist financing rules.

example_background: Paysafecard, a UK-authorized electronic money institution issuing prepaid vouchers for online payments, processed high volumes of transactions linked to gambling and digital goods. Operating under EMD2, it was required to hold ongoing capital based on average outstanding e-money and implement AML/CTF controls. From 2016-2018, the firm failed to conduct proper customer due diligence on a significant portion of users, many anonymous prepaid buyers, and did not segregate e-money holder funds adequately from its own revenues. Transaction monitoring systems flagged suspicious patterns involving potential money mules, but reporting to authorities was delayed or incomplete. This exposed consumers to risks and undermined prudent operations amid growth in unhosted wallet integrations.
process_and_outcome: The UK Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) investigated in 2019, resulting in a £1.35 million fine in 2021. Paysafecard enhanced its compliance framework, including capital recalculations and fund segregation audits, with ongoing supervisory reviews.
source_rule: (11) There is a need for a regime for initial capital combined with one for ongoing capital to ensure an appropriate level of consumer protection and the sound and prudent operation of electronic money institutions. Given the specificity of electronic money, an additional method for calculating ongoing capital should be provided for. Full supervisory discretion to ensure that the same risks are treated in the same way for all payment service providers and that the method of calculation encompasses the specific business situation of a given electronic money institution should be preserved. In addition, provision should be made for electronic money institutions to be required to keep the funds of electronic money holders separate from the funds of the electronic money institution for other business activities. Electronic money institutions should also be subject to effective anti-money laundering and anti-terrorist financing rules.

example_background: Revolut, a Lithuania-based electronic money institution with EU passporting, rapidly scaled to millions of users offering e-money wallets, crypto services, and payments. Under EMD2, it maintained initial capital but faced scrutiny over ongoing capital adequacy for its risk profile, including volatile crypto exposures. Client funds safeguarding was implemented via segregated bank accounts, but 2018-2019 audits revealed gaps in AML monitoring for high-value transfers and insufficient separation of e-money from investment activities. The Bank of Lithuania noted deviations from standardized capital calculation methods and inconsistent risk weighting.
process_and_outcome: The Bank of Lithuania probed in 2019-2020, imposing restrictions on acquiring new customers and requiring €5 million in additional capital. Revolut remediated by bolstering AML systems and fund protections, later securing a UK banking license.

example_background: Skrill (Paysafe Group), a London-headquartered e-money institution, provided digital wallets for forex trading, gaming, and remittances, holding e-money authorizations across the EEA. It was obligated to keep e-money holder funds separate in ring-fenced accounts and maintain capital proportional to outstanding e-money averages. Between 2012-2014, Skrill processed transactions without adequate AML checks, including for high-risk customers in unregulated sectors, and capital buffers were deemed insufficient for its business model by supervisors. Fund segregation was partially compromised due to commingling with payment service funds.
process_and_outcome: FCA enforcement in 2015 led to an £8.5 million fine. Skrill invested in compliance upgrades, including automated AML tools and enhanced capital reporting.

It managed large e-money float balances requiring specific ongoing capital calculations. AML systems did not effectively monitor cross-border flows, leading to unreported suspicious activities.
process_and_outcome: FCA action in 2013 resulted in a £1 million fine and mandated capital injections. The firm restructured its safeguarding and compliance operations.

example_background: PayPal, authorized as an electronic money institution in multiple EU states including Luxembourg, holds significant outstanding e-money from user balances. It maintains initial capital exceeding minimums and applies ongoing capital formulas based on average daily floats, adjusted for business-specific risks like fraud and chargebacks. Client funds are fully segregated in pooled bank accounts with daily reconciliations and insolvency protections. AML/CTF programs include transaction monitoring, PEP screening, and SAR filing, aligned with EU standards.
process_and_outcome: Regular supervisory audits by the CSSF (Luxembourg) and ECB confirm adherence, with no major enforcement actions. PayPal publishes transparency reports on capital and safeguarding.

In addition, provision should be made for electronic money institutions to be required to keep the funds of electronic money holders separate from the funds of the electronic money institution for other business activities.
