example_name: UK FCA Enforcement against DMM E-Pay Ltd
example_background: DMM E-Pay Ltd operated as an electronic money institution in the UK, issuing electronic money through prepaid cards and related services. The firm was authorized under the UK's implementation of the Electronic Money Directive but engaged in activities that exceeded the scope of its permissions, including facilitating high-volume transactions without adequate oversight of underlying payment flows. This occurred during a period of rapid growth in the fintech sector where smaller issuers faced challenges in scaling compliance programs. The entity had been registered but failed to maintain sufficient capital reserves and transaction monitoring as required for its operational scale, leading to exposure in cross-border payments.
process_and_outcome: The Financial Conduct Authority conducted an investigation, resulting in a £107,000 fine in 2018 and restrictions on the firm's operations until compliance gaps were addressed. The firm was required to cease certain services and implement enhanced controls.
source_rule: (16) It is appropriate to allow Member States to waive the application of certain provisions of this Directive as regards institutions issuing only a limited amount of electronic money. institutions benefiting from such a waiver should not have the right under this Directive to exercise the freedom of establishment or the freedom to provide services and they should not indirectly exercise those rights as members of a payment system. It is desirable, however, to register the details of all entities providing electronic money services, including those benefiting from a waiver. For that purpose, Member States should enter such entities in a register of electronic money institutions.
relevant_rules: Electronic Money Regulations 2011 (UK transposition of EMD2)

example_name: EBA Guidelines on Waivers for Limited Electronic Money Issuers
example_background: The European Banking Authority issued guidelines in 2019 clarifying the application of waivers under the Second Electronic Money Directive (EMD2) for institutions issuing electronic money below the €5 million average outstanding threshold over a 12-month period. Several small-scale issuers in Member States like the Netherlands and Ireland successfully applied for and received waivers, allowing them to operate without full authorization while being entered into national registers. These entities provided niche services such as loyalty programs or limited prepaid solutions without engaging in cross-border establishment or services.
process_and_outcome: National authorities granted waivers after verifying issuance limits and register inclusion, enabling continued low-volume operations without full prudential requirements. No enforcement actions were taken as conditions were met.

example_background: Tunis Finance Ireland Limited was registered as an electronic money institution but operated beyond permissible activities, including unauthorized issuance and distribution of payment instruments without proper safeguards. The firm, benefiting from lighter oversight due to its scale, indirectly participated in payment systems that involved cross-border elements, contravening restrictions on waived or limited institutions. This case highlighted risks in smaller EMIs during the expansion of digital wallets in Europe around 2017-2019.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, the FCA imposed a £58,700 fine and withdrew authorization after an investigation revealed inadequate controls and breaches of operational permissions.

example_name: Banco de Portugal Waiver Register for Small EMIs
example_background: Portugal maintains a public register of electronic money institutions, including those operating under waivers for limited issuance volumes as per EMD2 provisions. Entities such as small prepaid card issuers for retail loyalty schemes have been entered into this register since 2012, operating solely within national borders without claiming freedom of establishment or services. This practice ensures transparency while exempting them from full authorization requirements.
process_and_outcome: The Banco de Portugal routinely verifies and lists qualifying entities, with ongoing monitoring confirming adherence to volume limits and no cross-border activities.

example_name: Central Bank of Ireland Enforcement on Moneycorp
example_background: Moneycorp EAD, an electronic money institution, was investigated for operating without adequate registration and controls despite issuing electronic money in volumes that should have triggered full compliance. The firm engaged in payment services across EU states indirectly through partnerships, raising issues around waiver applicability and register maintenance.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, the Central Bank of Ireland fined the firm €105,000 following an inspection that identified failures in safeguarding and reporting.

example_name: Netherlands AFM Register of Limited EMIs
example_background: The Dutch Authority for the Financial Markets (AFM) oversees a register that includes electronic money entities benefiting from waivers for limited issuance, such as those providing electronic money for specific-purpose vouchers under €5 million thresholds. These entities are monitored to ensure they do not exercise freedoms of establishment or services, maintaining operations within the Netherlands only.
process_and_outcome: Successful registration and periodic reviews have allowed continued operations without incidents, demonstrating effective implementation of waiver provisions.

example_background: CurrencySelect Limited, authorized as a small electronic money institution, failed to properly segregate client funds and adhere to reporting requirements despite its limited issuance profile. The firm was part of a payment system network, indirectly providing services beyond national borders without full authorization.
process_and_outcome: The FCA issued a £20,000 fine in 2017 after thematic reviews uncovered persistent safeguarding issues.

example_name: EBA Report on EMI Register Compliance
example_background: The EBA's 2020-2022 reports on electronic money institutions noted several Member States' effective use of registers for waived entities, such as in Germany where BaFin lists small issuers of electronic money for transport tickets or gift cards, ensuring they remain within domestic operations and do not participate in cross-border payment systems.
process_and_outcome: Supervisory convergence ensured consistent application, with no major enforcement needed due to proactive registration and monitoring.

process_and_outcome: In 2019, the Finnish Financial Supervisory Authority imposed administrative measures, leading to restructuring and compliance realignment without fines.

example_background: Metro Bank Plc, a UK digital challenger bank, issued electronic money through its e-money institution arm. Between 2016 and 2020, the bank held customer e-money balances in segregated accounts but failed to properly ring-fence these funds from its own banking operations. This occurred during a period of rapid growth where the bank issued over £1 billion in e-money to retail customers via its app and online platforms. The lack of strict segregation meant that in the event of the bank's insolvency, e-money holders might not have immediate access to their full balances at par value, as operational banking funds were intermingled. The issue came to light during routine supervisory reviews amid broader concerns about the bank's financial health and IT systems.
process_and_outcome: The UK's Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) investigated and concluded that Metro Bank breached safeguarding requirements. In 2021, the FCA imposed a £10 million penalty. Metro Bank accepted the findings, enhanced its safeguarding processes by implementing stricter segregation and daily reconciliations, and reported no customer losses.
source_rule: (18) Electronic money needs to be redeemable to preserve the confidence of the electronic money holder. Redeemability does not imply that the funds received in exchange for electronic money should be regarded as deposits or other repayable funds for the purpose of Directive 2006/48/EC. Redemption should be possible at any time, at par value without any possibility to agree a minimum threshold for redemption. Redemption should, in general, be granted free of charge. However, in cases duly specified in this Directive it should be possible to request a proportionate and cost-based fee without prejudice to national legislation on tax or social matters or any obligations on the electronic money issuer under other relevant Community or national legislation, such as anti-money laundering and anti-terrorist financing rules, any action targeting the freezing of funds or any specific measure linked to the prevention and investigation of crimes.

example_background: Wirecard AG, a German fintech firm licensed as an electronic money institution (EMI) in the UK and EU, issued e-money products including prepaid cards and digital wallets used by millions of customers globally. By 2019-2020, investigations revealed that Wirecard had overstated its client cash balances by €1.9 billion, with significant portions held in Asian trustees that did not exist. For UK e-money customers, this raised concerns over redeemability, as promised redemption at par value was at risk due to insufficient safeguarded funds. The scandal unfolded amid accounting irregularities, leading to customer complaints about delayed or restricted withdrawals from Wirecard's e-money accounts during the crisis.
process_and_outcome: UK FCA revoked Wirecard's EMI authorization in June 2020. BaFin (German regulator) suspended operations. Wirecard filed for insolvency with €3.5 billion in missing funds. Criminal probes led to CEO Markus Braun's arrest for fraud. UK customers recovered funds via FSCS up to £85,000 per person after transfer to another EMI.

example_background: Paysafecard, a UK-authorized e-money institution offering prepaid vouchers for online payments, faced scrutiny over its redemption policies for converting vouchers to bank transfers. From 2015-2018, the company applied fees for certain redemption methods, particularly for lower-value vouchers or non-standard requests, which customers argued hindered free redemption at par. This affected users across Europe who sought to cash out balances, with complaints lodged via consumer protection channels about disproportionate charges not clearly tied to costs.
process_and_outcome: FCA reviewed complaints and Paysafecard's policies, issuing a private warning in 2019. The company revised its terms to eliminate most fees, offering free redemption for standard requests and cost-justified charges only for complex cases. No public fine was levied, but enhanced disclosures were mandated.

example_background: Revolut Ltd, holding a UK e-money institution license since 2017, provides digital wallets and prepaid cards to over 30 million users. The firm maintains segregated safeguarding accounts with major banks, conducts daily reconciliations, and enables instant redemption of e-money balances to linked bank accounts at par value without fees for standard transactions. During the COVID-19 period, Revolut processed millions of redemptions seamlessly, even amid high volumes, demonstrating operational resilience.
process_and_outcome: FCA supervisory reviews in 2020-2022 confirmed full compliance with safeguarding and redemption rules. No enforcement actions taken; Revolut received approval for banking license progression in 2023.

example_background: Starling Bank, a UK challenger bank with an e-money license, issues e-money via its app-based current accounts. Since 2017, it has safeguarded over £2 billion in e-money by holding funds in trust accounts with independent custodians, allowing 24/7 redemption at par without fees or thresholds. The bank publishes monthly attestation reports verifying segregated funds, building holder confidence.
process_and_outcome: FCA audits consistently rated safeguarding practices as exemplary, with no issues identified in 2021-2023 reviews.

example_name: FCA Action Against First Choice Payday Loans
example_background: First Choice Payday Loans Ltd operated as an e-money issuer alongside lending activities. In 2014-2016, it imposed minimum redemption thresholds and fees on e-money balances derived from loan refunds, preventing small-balance holders from accessing full par value without additional costs. This affected vulnerable customers seeking to redeem modest sums.
process_and_outcome: FCA imposed a £50,000 fine in 2017 and required policy changes. The firm ceased operations shortly after.

example_background: Monzo Bank Ltd, authorized as an e-money institution prior to its full banking license, holds customer e-money in segregated accounts and offers fee-free, instant redemptions to bank transfers at any time without minimums. With millions of users, it handled peak redemption demands during economic stress periods without interruption.
process_and_outcome: FCA confirmed compliance in multiple prudential reviews (2018-2023), supporting Monzo's transition to banking license.

example_background: Patrick Collison and John Quinn operated unauthorized EMIs in Ireland and the UK, issuing e-money via platforms without proper licenses. Customers faced redemption barriers, including delays and partial payments not at par, as operators diverted funds to personal use between 2012-2015.
process_and_outcome: Central Bank of Ireland and FCA investigated. In 2018, operators were convicted of fraud, receiving prison sentences and restitution orders. Customer funds partially recovered via liquidation.

example_background: Wise, operating as an EMI in the UK and EU, safeguards customer e-money balances in segregated pools and provides redemption at par value via bank transfers without fees or thresholds. It publishes independent attestations quarterly, ensuring transparency for its global user base.
process_and_outcome: FCA ongoing supervision deems practices fully aligned; no issues in 2022 review.

example_name: PayPal E-Money Passporting Enforcement by FCA
example_background: PayPal (Europe) Services Ltd, an electronic money institution authorised in Luxembourg, established branches across multiple EU Member States including the UK, France, and Germany to offer payment services. The company utilised the EU passporting regime under the Electronic Money Directive to expand operations without separate authorisations in each host state. In the UK, PayPal operated a branch subject to oversight by the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA), which monitored compliance with local prudential and conduct requirements applicable to passported firms. During routine supervisory reviews in 2018-2019, discrepancies were identified in how PayPal reported safeguarding of customer funds and maintained liquidity ratios across jurisdictions. These issues arose from inconsistent application of group-wide policies adapted to varying national interpretations of EMD requirements. PayPal's Luxembourg head office coordinated responses, but host state notifications revealed gaps in branch-level governance structures. The situation highlighted challenges in ensuring uniform rules for passported e-money institutions amid differing supervisory expectations in host Member States.
process_and_outcome: The FCA conducted a targeted review and issued a final notice in 2020 requiring PayPal to address deficiencies, including enhanced safeguarding arrangements and submission of a compliance improvement plan. No monetary penalty was imposed, but PayPal invested over €10 million in remedial actions, including system upgrades and staff training. The case was closed following verification of corrective measures in 2021.
source_rule: (15) The rules governing branches of electronic money institutions which have their head office outside the Community should be analogous in all Member States. It is important to provide that such rules not be more favourable than those for branches of electronic money institutions which have their head office in another Member State. The Community should be able to conclude agreements with third countries providing for the application of rules which accord branches of electronic money institutions which have their head office outside the Community the same treatment throughout the Community. The branches of electronic money institutions which have their head office outside the Community should benefit from neither the freedom of establishment under Article 43 of the Treaty in Member States other than those in which they are established nor the freedom to provide services under the second paragraph of Article 49 of the Treaty.
involved_parties: Financial Conduct Authority (FCA, UK host regulator)
Commission de Surveillance du Secteur Financier (CSSF, Luxembourg home regulator)
relevant_rules: Article 43 and 49 EC Treaty (freedom of establishment and services)
FCA Handbook rules for e-money institutions

example_name: Revolut UK Branch Authorisation under EMD
example_background: Revolut Ltd, originally authorised as an electronic money institution in the UK, sought to expand across the EU through passporting notifications to multiple Member States starting in 2017. Following Brexit, the firm restructured with its e-money authorisation transferred to Lithuania in 2020, establishing a UK branch to continue operations post-transition period. The Lithuanian Central Bank authorised Revolut Payments UAB, which passported services into the UK under temporary permissions regime and later applied for FCA authorisation for its UK branch. The process involved detailed assessments of branch governance, risk management, and compliance with host state rules equivalent to those for intra-EU passported branches. Revolut maintained segregated customer funds and liquidity in the UK branch, aligning with EMD safeguarding requirements while coordinating with both Lithuanian and UK supervisors to ensure consistent application of rules.
process_and_outcome: FCA granted full authorisation to Revolut's UK branch in 2023 after a multi-year review, including on-site inspections and capital adequacy checks. The approval allowed continued operations serving over 8 million UK customers without disruption.

example_name: Alipay Third-Country Branch Restrictions in EU
example_background: Ant Group (Alipay), a Chinese electronic money and payment services provider, sought to establish operations in Europe through partnerships and direct presence. In 2021, Alibaba's Alipay entered into arrangements to offer services via EU-licensed partners but faced scrutiny over direct branch-like activities in Member States without EU head office passporting rights. The European Central Bank (ECB) and national authorities examined whether Alipay's model circumvented rules denying third-country e-money institutions freedom of establishment or services under Articles 43 and 49 EC Treaty. In Germany, BaFin investigated Alipay's activities through local agents, determining they constituted unauthorised provision of payment services. Similar probes occurred in France and other states, focusing on whether third-country entities could achieve treatment equivalent to EU-headquartered firms without formal equivalence agreements.
process_and_outcome: BaFin issued a cease-and-desist order in 2021 prohibiting Alipay from directly marketing services to German consumers without a license. Alipay ceased independent operations and shifted to licensed EU intermediaries. No fines were levied, but the case prompted wider EU discussions on third-country regime equivalence.

example_background: Wise (formerly TransferWise), authorised as an electronic money institution in the UK and later Belgium, passported its e-money services across the EEA. Post-Brexit, Wise established a Belgian head office in 2021 through passporting notifications to 30 EEA states, enabling branches or services in countries like France, Spain, and Germany. The National Bank of Belgium oversaw the home state authorisation, while host regulators such as ACPR in France monitored branch compliance with local consumer protection and AML rules. Wise implemented uniform safeguarding of client funds across jurisdictions, using segregated accounts and daily reconciliations to meet EMD standards consistently.
process_and_outcome: All passporting notifications were accepted without objection, allowing seamless operations. In 2022, Wise reported full compliance in supervisory returns across host states.

example_background: N26 Bank GmbH, authorised in Germany under CRD but operating e-money services, passported into multiple EU states including the UK pre-Brexit. The BaFin-supervised firm faced challenges in 2019-2020 when expanding branches, particularly in the UK where FCA identified gaps in branch-level AML monitoring and customer due diligence for passported e-money activities. N26's centralised model from Berlin led to delays in host state reporting, prompting coordination issues under the passport regime.
process_and_outcome: BaFin imposed temporary restrictions on new UK customer onboarding in 2021 and required remediation. FCA issued a warning, and N26 paid €4.25 million in fines to BaFin in 2022 for overall compliance shortcomings, with branch operations stabilised post-fixes.

example_name: WeChat Pay Third-Country Access Denied in EU
example_background: Tencent's WeChat Pay, a major Chinese digital payment provider, attempted to expand into Europe by partnering with local firms and offering cross-border services. In 2018, the European Commission and national regulators assessed WeChat Pay's model against rules limiting third-country e-money institutions from enjoying intra-EU freedoms of establishment or services. Sweden's Finansinspektionen investigated WeChat's activities via Swedish merchants, finding unauthorised storage of EU customer funds outside the EEA, contravening EMD safeguarding and licensing rules.
process_and_outcome: Finansinspektionen ordered cessation of direct fund handling in 2019. WeChat Pay restructured through PSD2-licensed partners. The incident contributed to EU-China fintech dialogue but resulted in no equivalence agreement.

The branches of electronic money institutions which have their head office outside the Community should benefit from neither the freedom of establishment under Article 43 of the Treaty in Member States other than those in which they are established nor the freedom to provide services under the second paragraph of Article 49 of the Treaty.

example_background: Lydia, a French fintech authorised as an electronic money institution by ACPR in 2018, passported its services to 11 EEA countries including Spain, Italy, and Belgium. The firm established service provision without physical branches, relying on centralised operations in Paris compliant with host state notifications. ACPR coordinated with host regulators to ensure uniform application of EMD rules on initial capital (€350,000 maintained), safeguarding, and annual reporting across jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: All host states accepted notifications, enabling growth to 5 million users across EEA by 2022 without enforcement actions.
source_rule: (15) The rules governing branches of electronic money institutions which have their head office outside the Community should be analogous in all Member States. It is important to provide that such rules not be more favourable than those for branches of electronic money institutions which have their head office in another Member State. The Community should be able to conclude agreements with third countries providing for the application of rules which accord branches of electronic money institutions which have their head office outside the Community the same treatment throughout the Community. The branches of electronic money institutions which have their head office outside the Community should benefit from neither the freedom of establishment under Article 43 of the Treaty in Member States other than those in which they are established nor the freedom to provide services under the second paragraph of Article 49 of the Treaty.
involved_parties: Autorité de Contrôle Prudentiel et de Résolution (ACPR)

example_background: Starling Bank, UK e-money and banking licensed firm, faced EEA access issues post-Brexit. To serve EU customers, it partnered with Lithuanian EMI Pair Finance in 2021, which passported back into the UK under equivalence arrangements. This structure ensured UK customers' funds were safeguarded under EMD rules equivalent to intra-EU standards, with Starling handling UK compliance and Pair managing EEA passporting.
process_and_outcome: FCA approved the partnership model in 2022, allowing continued seamless services without rule breaches.

example_background: Curve, authorised as EMI in the UK, passported to EEA states pre-Brexit. Post-2020, it relocated authorisation to Lithuania, notifying host states for continued operations. In 2021, Bank of Lithuania and host regulators identified inconsistencies in branch risk assessments and customer fund protections during passporting verification.
process_and_outcome: Bank of Lithuania required plan submission, leading to €1.2 million in upgrades. Operations resumed fully compliant in 2022.

example_name: E-Money Institution Licence Revocation - Unnamed UK Firm
example_background: The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) in the UK revoked the authorisation of an electronic money institution that had been granted a licence under the Electronic Money Regulations 2011. The firm, which issued electronic money products for payment services, engaged in activities that involved providing interest on held electronic money balances, linking returns directly to the duration of holding periods. This practice occurred over several months while the firm managed customer funds totaling millions of euros equivalent. The institution marketed these interest-bearing features as incentives for customers to maintain balances, positioning the products as savings-like options rather than solely for making payments. Background checks revealed that the firm's business model blurred lines between electronic money issuance and deposit-taking, with promotional materials emphasizing yield over transactional use. The firm operated through mobile apps and partnerships with fintech platforms, attracting retail users across Europe. Regulatory monitoring identified discrepancies in prudential reporting, prompting deeper scrutiny into fund safeguarding and operational compliance. The case highlighted challenges in distinguishing pure payment surrogates from investment products in the digital finance space.
process_and_outcome: FCA conducted an investigation following supervisory reviews and customer complaints. The regulator issued a final notice revoking the licence in 2022, citing risks to consumers and market integrity. The firm was ordered to cease operations, transfer customer funds to authorised entities, and faced costs exceeding £500,000. No direct fine was imposed due to voluntary cessation, but ongoing enforcement ensured compliance remediation.
source_rule: (13) The issuance of electronic money does not constitute a deposit-taking activity pursuant to Directive 2006/48/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 14 June 2006 relating to the taking up and pursuit of the business of credit institutions (6), in view of its specific character as an electronic surrogate for coins and banknotes, which is to be used for making payments, usually of limited amount and not as means of saving. Electronic money institutions should not be allowed to grant credit from the funds received or held for the purpose of issuing electronic money. Electronic money issuers should not, moreover, be allowed to grant interest or any other benefit unless those benefits are not related to the length of time during which the electronic money holder holds electronic money. The conditions for granting and maintaining authorisation as electronic money institutions should include prudential requirements that are proportionate to the operational and financial risks faced by such bodies in the course of their business related to the issuance of electronic money, independently of any other commercial activities carried out by the electronic money institution.

example_background: Paysafecard, a prepaid electronic money voucher provider operating across Europe, structured its business model strictly as a payment surrogate aligned with EMD2 requirements. Customers purchase vouchers for small-value transactions, redeemable online without any interest accrual or credit extension. The company maintains segregated safeguarding accounts for unspent balances, ensuring funds are not used for lending or investment yielding time-based returns. Paysafecard holds EMI authorisations in multiple EU jurisdictions, including the UK and Germany, and undergoes regular audits to verify compliance with prudential requirements proportionate to its low-risk issuance model. The firm explicitly prohibits marketing vouchers as savings vehicles, focusing promotions on immediate payment utility for gaming, bills, and e-commerce. Annual reports demonstrate operational resilience, with capital buffers exceeding minimums despite no engagement in interest-bearing activities.
process_and_outcome: FCA and BaFin conducted routine inspections, confirming adherence and renewing authorisations. No enforcement actions were taken; Paysafecard received positive supervisory feedback in 2023 public disclosures.

example_background: Wirecard, a German payments firm with EMI licences, issued electronic money through its cards and wallets but faced allegations of mismanaging client funds. While primarily known for the 2020 accounting scandal involving fake profits, earlier probes in 2018-2019 examined whether Wirecard's handling of e-money reserves complied with no-credit and no-interest rules. Singapore regulators flagged escrow accounts holding €1 billion+ in purported client funds, some of which were allegedly used in opaque third-party lending arrangements. Wirecard's model involved partnering with banks but also directly issuing e-money, raising questions on fund segregation and whether implicit benefits resembled interest tied to holding periods. The firm reported €10 billion+ in e-money outstanding at peak, marketed for payments but scrutinized for yield-like features in premium accounts.
process_and_outcome: BaFin and FCA investigated; Wirecard's EMI authorisations lapsed amid insolvency in 2020. Insolvency administrator pursued claims, resulting in €3.4 billion creditor losses. UK FCA banned executives and imposed restrictions.

The firm ensured no credit was granted from e-money funds and offered no interest on balances until obtaining a banking licence in Lithuania. Safeguarding measures included ring-fenced accounts at tier-1 banks, with monthly attestations verifying compliance. Revolut's 20+ million users primarily used the service for small payments and FX, aligning with the directive's payment surrogate intent. Prudential reporting showed capital adequacy well above requirements, despite rapid scaling.
process_and_outcome: FCA authorised and supervised Revolut as EMI without material findings; licence transitioned smoothly to banking in 2021. Public reports noted strong compliance framework.

example_name: FCA Fine on Tunisian E-Money Firm
example_background: A Tunisian-owned EMI authorised in the UK was fined by the FCA for failing to safeguard electronic money funds adequately and engaging in unauthorised credit-like activities. The firm issued e-money to migrant workers for remittances, holding £15 million in balances. It provided 'loyalty bonuses' calculated based on average balance duration, resembling interest. Additionally, short-term loans were extended using pooled e-money funds, breaching prohibitions. Operations spanned 2015-2019, with weak governance and inadequate anti-money laundering controls exacerbating issues.
process_and_outcome: FCA investigation led to a £128,000 fine in 2020 and voluntary variation of permission. The firm wound down UK operations.

Funds were safeguarded with central banks or approved custodians. Upon obtaining full banking licences, N26 maintained compliance records from its EMI phase, demonstrating scalable prudential management.
process_and_outcome: Central Bank of Ireland and BaFin approved operations; no adverse actions during EMI period. Banking licence granted in 2019-2020.

The model emphasized payment use for online merchants and gambling.
process_and_outcome: BaFin issued a public warning and mandated remediation in 2018; licence retained after corrective actions.

process_and_outcome: FCA granted and maintained authorisation; transitioned to banking in 2024 with clean record.

example_name: UK FCA Enforcement against eMoney Institution for Unauthorized Issuance
example_background: In 2019, the UK Financial Conduct Authority identified a firm operating as an electronic money institution that had issued electronic money without holding the required authorization under the Electronic Money Regulations 2011, which transpose the EU E-Money Directive. The firm had been marketing prepaid cards and digital wallets loaded with electronic money to consumers across multiple EU member states, processing transactions totaling over £10 million. The entity had previously operated under a different license that did not cover e-money issuance and failed to apply for the necessary e-money institution status or waiver. This activity involved partnerships with payment processors and involved handling funds from retail customers for online purchases and peer-to-peer transfers. The firm's operations expanded rapidly without proper regulatory oversight, leading to concerns over consumer fund protection and financial stability. The case highlighted gaps in pre-Brexit supervision of cross-border e-money services within the Single Euro Payments Area (SEPA).
process_and_outcome: The FCA conducted an investigation following supervisory reviews and consumer complaints. The firm was ordered to cease issuing electronic money immediately, return customer funds, and was fined £250,000. A prohibition notice was issued against the directors, and the firm entered voluntary liquidation. No consumer losses were reported due to timely intervention.
source_rule: (17) For prudential reasons, Member States should ensure that only electronic money institutions duly authorised or benefiting from a waiver in accordance with this Directive, credit institutions authorised in accordance with Directive 2006/48/EC, post office giro institutions entitled under national law to issue electronic money, institutions referred to in Article 2 of Directive 2006/48/EC, the European Central Bank, national central banks when not acting in their capacity as monetary authority or other public authorities and Member States or their regional or local authorities when acting in their capacity as public authorities may issue electronic money.
relevant_rules: Electronic Money Regulations 2011 (UK transposition of Directive 2009/110/EC)

example_background: Banco Santander, a major Spanish credit institution authorized under the EU Credit Institutions Directive, applied for and obtained designation as an electronic money institution in 2015 to expand its digital payment services. The bank leveraged its existing credit institution license, which permits the issuance of electronic money under EU rules, to launch prepaid cards and mobile wallets under the Santander brand. This allowed the bank to issue e-money to millions of customers across the Eurozone, safeguarding funds in segregated accounts and providing redemption rights. The initiative was part of a broader digital transformation strategy, integrating e-money with traditional banking services like remittances and bill payments, while maintaining compliance with prudential requirements for fund protection and anti-money laundering controls.
process_and_outcome: The Bank of Spain reviewed and approved Santander's e-money activities under its credit institution framework. No enforcement actions were taken, and the bank successfully scaled operations, reporting billions in e-money issuance volumes annually without incidents.
source_rule: (17) For prudential reasons, Member States should ensure that only electronic money institutions duly authorised or benefiting from a waiver in accordance with this Directive, credit institutions authorised in accordance with Directive 2006/48/EC, post office giro institutions entitled under national law to issue electronic money, institutions referred to in Article 2 of Directive 2006/48/EC, the European Central Bank, national central banks when not acting in their capacity as monetary authority or other public authorities and Member States or their regional or local authorities when acting in their capacity as public authorities may issue electronic money.
involved_parties: Banco Santander (credit institution and e-money issuer)

example_name: MoneyGram Unauthorized E-Money Activities in Europe
example_background: MoneyGram International, a US-based money transfer company, faced scrutiny in 2018 for providing services in the EU that regulators determined constituted unauthorized issuance of electronic money. Operating through agents and digital platforms, the firm facilitated transfers where stored value on accounts was treated as e-money without proper authorization or waiver. This involved handling over €50 million in transactions annually in countries like Germany and France, where customers loaded funds for delayed remittances. The company relied on partnerships with local banks but did not hold an e-money license itself, leading to classification issues under EU definitions of electronic money as a monetary value stored electronically for payment purposes.
process_and_outcome: National regulators in multiple member states coordinated with the European Banking Authority. MoneyGram was required to halt certain operations, restructure services under licensed partners, and paid €1.2 million in penalties across jurisdictions. Remediation included customer notifications and fund transfers.
source_rule: (17) For prudential reasons, Member States should ensure that only electronic money institutions duly authorised or benefiting from a waiver in accordance with this Directive, credit institutions authorised in accordance with Directive 2006/48/EC, post office giro institutions entitled under national law to issue electronic money, institutions referred to in Article 2 of Directive 2006/48/EC, the European Central Bank, national central banks when not acting in their capacity as monetary authority or other public authorities and Member States or their regional or local authorities when acting in their capacity as public authorities may issue electronic money.

example_name: European Central Bank Digital Currency Preparations
example_background: The European Central Bank (ECB) initiated exploratory work in 2021 on a potential digital euro, positioning itself as an authorized issuer of electronic money under EU prudential rules. As a public authority explicitly permitted to issue e-money when not acting in its monetary policy capacity, the ECB conducted phases of investigation, prototyping, and stakeholder consultations. This involved designing a retail CBDC that would function as electronic money, redeemable at par with euro cash, with features for offline payments and privacy safeguards. The project engaged commercial banks and e-money institutions to ensure interoperability and addressed concerns over financial inclusion and monetary sovereignty.
process_and_outcome: The ECB Governing Council approved ongoing preparations in October 2021, with pilot phases ongoing. No regulatory breaches occurred, and the initiative serves as a model for compliant central bank involvement in e-money issuance.

relevant_rules: Treaty on the Functioning of the EU (TFEU)

example_background: Revolut, a UK-headquartered fintech founded in 2015, obtained an electronic money institution license from the FCA in 2017, enabling it to issue electronic money legally across the EEA. Prior to this, the company had operated under partner banks but sought direct authorization to control its prepaid accounts and card products, which hold electronic money for 10 million+ users. The license allowed issuance of e-money for spending, transfers, and crypto conversions, with safeguards like segregated safeguarding accounts holding over €2 billion in client funds. This complied with EU-wide passporting rules pre-Brexit.
process_and_outcome: FCA granted the license after a rigorous application process, including prudential assessments. Revolut expanded compliantly, later pursuing a full banking license.

While authorized, the firm had procedural lapses in verifying customer identities and segregating e-money funds during high-volume periods, affecting millions of euros in balances. Operations involved online payment processing where user balances qualified as e-money under EU definitions.
process_and_outcome: BaFin investigated and imposed a €3.5 million fine. PayPal implemented enhanced controls and reporting, resolving issues without service disruption.

It issues prepaid cards and digital wallets through 17,000 post offices, serving underserved populations with e-money products redeemable at par. Funds are safeguarded separately, supporting 5 million+ accounts and integrating with national payment schemes.
process_and_outcome: Authorized by ACPR with ongoing supervision; no major incidents, demonstrating sustained compliance in public authority e-money issuance.

This enabled direct issuance for its mobile banking app's payment functions, holding e-money in customer accounts for 7 million EU users. The model emphasizes real-time safeguarding and AML compliance.
process_and_outcome: BaFin approved after comprehensive review; N26 grew compliantly, expanding e-money-linked services.

example_name: EBA Warning on Unauthorized Crypto E-Money Schemes
example_background: In 2019, the European Banking Authority issued a statement addressing several platforms offering 'stablecoins' marketed as electronic money without authorization. One prominent case involved a pan-EU operator issuing digital tokens backed by fiat, processing €100 million+ in loads/redemptions, bypassing e-money licensing by claiming non-e-money status despite fitting EU definitions.
process_and_outcome: EBA coordinated national shutdowns; platforms ceased operations, with €5 million in fines levied. Operators were referred for criminal probes.
source_rule: (17) For prudential reasons, Member States should ensure that only electronic money institutions duly authorised or benefiting from a waiver in accordance with this Directive, credit institutions authorised in accordance with Directive 2006/48/EC, post office giro institutions entitled under national law to issue electronic money, institutions referred to in Article 2 of Directive 2006/48/EC, the European Central Bank, national central banks when not acting in their capacity as monetary authority or other public authorities and Member States or their regional or local authorities when acting in their capacity as public authorities may issue electronic money.

example_background: MoneyGram International operated as a money transfer service provider issuing electronic money-like products across Europe and globally. Between 2008 and 2012, the company faced issues where consumers reported unauthorized transactions and difficulties in obtaining refunds for failed transfers. Numerous electronic money holders submitted complaints through customer service channels but encountered delays and denials in the redress process. The lack of efficient out-of-court settlement mechanisms led to escalation of disputes, with consumers unable to resolve issues promptly. Internal procedures existed but were inadequate for handling the volume and complexity of cross-border electronic money disputes, resulting in prolonged resolution times exceeding regulatory expectations. This situation drew attention from multiple regulators examining the framework for complaint handling under payment services directives.
process_and_outcome: The US Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) and UK Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) coordinated investigations. MoneyGram agreed to a $27.5 million settlement in the US, including consumer redress funds, and implemented enhanced complaint procedures in Europe. The FCA required improvements to out-of-court redress aligned with PSD requirements, with ongoing monitoring.
source_rule: (19) Out-of-court complaint and redress procedures for the settlement of disputes should be at the disposal of electronic money holders. Chapter 5 of Title IV of Directive 2007/64/EC should therefore apply mutatis mutandis in the context of this Directive, without prejudice to the provisions of this Directive. A reference to ‘payment service provider’ in Directive 2007/64/EC therefore needs to be read as a reference to electronic money issuer; a reference to ‘payment service user’ needs to be read as a reference to electronic money holder; and a reference to Titles III and IV of Directive 2007/64/EC needs to be read as a reference to Title III of this Directive.

example_background: Neteller, a UK-based electronic money issuer, provided services to electronic money holders for storing and transferring funds online. In 2015-2016, the firm received a high volume of complaints from holders regarding disputed transactions, account closures without notice, and delays in fund releases. The company's initial redress procedures involved internal reviews but lacked structured out-of-court settlement processes that met EU standards for timely resolution. Holders experienced waits of several months, and there was no clear escalation path to independent dispute resolution. This occurred during a period of regulatory scrutiny on e-money institutions to ensure alignment with PSD complaint frameworks applied mutatis mutandis.
process_and_outcome: The FCA investigated and imposed a £1 million fine in 2018 for failures in treating customers fairly, specifically in complaint handling and redress. Neteller was required to appoint an independent reviewer, pay redress to affected holders, and overhaul its procedures to incorporate PSD Chapter 5 mechanisms.
source_rule: (19) Out-of-court complaint and redress procedures for the settlement of disputes should be at the disposal of electronic money holders. Chapter 5 of Title IV of Directive 2007/64/EC should therefore apply mutatis mutandis in the context of this Directive, without prejudice to the provisions of this Directive. A reference to ‘payment service provider’ in Directive 2007/64/EC therefore needs to be read as a reference to electronic money issuer; a reference to ‘payment service user’ needs to be read as a reference to electronic money holder; and a reference to Titles III and IV of Directive 2007/64/EC needs to be read as a reference to Title III of this Directive.

example_background: PayPal (Europe) Ltd, authorized as an EU electronic money institution, managed accounts for millions of electronic money holders. From 2014 to 2017, there were reports of systematic issues in handling complaints about unauthorized payments and refund requests. Holders faced challenges navigating the dispute resolution process, with automated responses and limited human intervention leading to unresolved cases. The structure did not fully incorporate adapted PSD out-of-court procedures, resulting in backlogs and referrals to ombudsman services. Regulators noted discrepancies in applying payment service user protections to e-money contexts.
process_and_outcome: Central Bank of Ireland and FCA conducted joint reviews, leading to a 2018 remediation program. PayPal paid €2.5 million in redress and fines, enhanced its complaint portal, and integrated PSD-compliant out-of-court settlement procedures with 15-day resolution targets.

example_background: Revolut, a Lithuania-authorized e-money issuer serving EU electronic money holders, initially faced criticism in 2019-2020 for slow complaint resolutions on issues like transaction disputes and account restrictions. Early procedures relied on email support without formalized out-of-court settlement timelines. Following regulatory feedback, Revolut expanded its framework to include dedicated dispute teams and integration with national ombudsman schemes, adapting PSD Chapter 5 processes for e-money holders.
process_and_outcome: FCA and Bank of Lithuania supervised enhancements, resulting in 95% complaint resolution within 15 days by 2021. No fines were imposed; instead, Revolut received recognition for compliance improvements in annual reports.

example_background: Skrill, an electronic money issuer under EU regulation, handled complaints from holders about frozen funds and disputed transfers between 2016 and 2018. The redress process involved internal escalation but lacked binding out-of-court settlement options, leading to many cases proceeding to litigation. Holders reported inconsistent application of protections akin to payment service users, with resolution times averaging 60 days.
process_and_outcome: FCA fined Skrill £8.9 million in 2020 for complaint mishandling. The company established a redress fund of £5 million and adopted PSD-mutatis-mutandis procedures with independent review panels.

example_background: N26, operating as an EU e-money and banking hybrid, implemented from launch in 2016 a digital-first complaint system for electronic money holders facing issues with transfers and balances. The process featured an online portal with 10-day resolution commitments and automatic referrals to the German Ombudsman, fully mapping PSD Chapter 5 adapted for EMD.
process_and_outcome: BaFin supervisory review in 2020 confirmed full compliance, with low escalation rates. N26 maintained a clean record on redress disputes, serving as a model in regulatory reports.

example_background: EcoPayz, a UK e-money institution, dealt with holder complaints on withdrawal delays and chargebacks from 2017-2019. Internal procedures existed but did not provide adequate out-of-court settlement paths, causing reliance on courts for resolutions and holder dissatisfaction.
process_and_outcome: FCA issued a 2019 final notice with £500,000 penalty and redress requirements. EcoPayz integrated PSD-compliant procedures, resolving backlog within six months.

example_background: Wise, authorized as an EU e-money issuer, established from 2018 a comprehensive redress system for electronic money holders, including a 24-hour initial response, 15-day full resolution target, and seamless handover to national dispute schemes. This directly adapted PSD frameworks for e-money disputes on transfers and holdings.
process_and_outcome: FCA and Belgian FSMA audits in 2022 praised the system, with 98% resolution rate. No enforcement actions; featured in industry compliance guides.

example_background: Paysafecard, an e-money product issuer, faced holder complaints in 2018-2020 over unredeemed vouchers and service disruptions. The redress process was decentralized across agents, lacking unified out-of-court procedures aligned with EU directives.
process_and_outcome: FCA investigation led to a voluntary redress scheme in 2021, paying £1.2 million to holders and centralizing complaints under PSD-equivalent processes.

example_name: Paysafe safeguarding change notification to FCA
example_background: Paysafe, operating through its electronic money institution subsidiaries like Paysafe Financial Services Limited in the UK, provides e-money services including prepaid cards and digital wallets. In 2018, the company proposed changes to its safeguarding methods, transitioning from holding client funds in segregated bank accounts to a combination of insurance protection and deposits with multiple credit institutions. This adjustment was made to optimize operational efficiency while maintaining fund protection levels. Paysafe held significant e-money holder funds, exceeding €1 billion at the time, and the change involved switching the designated credit institutions for deposits and updating insurance arrangements with new providers. The process required detailed assessment of the new custodians' credit ratings and insurance coverage to ensure equivalence to prior safeguards.
process_and_outcome: Paysafe submitted a prior notification to the FCA as required, including comprehensive documentation on the new safeguarding arrangements. The FCA reviewed and approved the changes after a 3-month assessment period, confirming compliance with notification and adequacy requirements. No disruptions to services occurred, and client funds remained fully protected throughout the transition.
source_rule: (14) It is necessary, however, to preserve a level playing field between electronic money institutions and credit institutions with regard to the issuance of electronic money to ensure fair competition for the same service among a wider range of institutions for the benefit of electronic money holders. This should be achieved by balancing the less cumbersome features of the prudential supervisory regime applying to electronic money institutions against provisions that are more stringent than those applying to credit institutions, notably as regards the safeguarding of the funds of an electronic money holder. Given the crucial importance of safeguarding, it is necessary that the competent authorities be informed in advance of any material change, such as a change in the safeguarding method, a change in the credit institution where safeguarded funds are deposited, or a change in the insurance undertaking or credit institution which insured or guaranteed the safeguarded funds.

example_name: FCA Enforcement against TSB Bank E-Money Safeguarding
example_background: TSB Bank, a UK credit institution offering e-money services through its prepaid card products, was found deficient in safeguarding client funds between 2015 and 2018. The bank held e-money holder funds in accounts that were not fully segregated from its own operational balances, despite regulatory requirements for strict separation. Changes in the credit institution holding the safeguarded funds were made without prior notification to the FCA, including a switch to a new custodian bank in 2017 amid internal restructuring. This exposed e-money holders to risks during TSB's major IT migration issues in 2018, where system failures compounded safeguarding concerns. The total value of affected e-money balances was approximately £150 million.
process_and_outcome: FCA investigation in 2019 led to a £16.65 million fine in 2020 for failures including inadequate safeguarding and lack of prior notification on changes. TSB remediated by implementing new segregated accounts and notification processes, with no client losses reported.

example_background: Revolut, authorized as a UK electronic money institution since 2017, rapidly expanded its e-money services with customer balances growing to over £1.5 billion by 2020. The firm maintains safeguarded funds through deposits in tier-1 credit institutions and excess insurance coverage. In 2021, Revolut notified the FCA of a planned change in its primary safeguarding bank from Barclays to a combination of JP Morgan and another institution, alongside updates to its insurance provider for excess coverage. This was part of scaling operations to handle increased e-money issuance while ensuring funds were protected against institution failure.
process_and_outcome: FCA acknowledged the prior notification and conducted a review, approving the changes. Revolut's annual safeguarding attestations confirmed 100% coverage, with no issues identified in subsequent audits. The firm received its banking license in 2024 partly due to strong safeguarding practices.

example_name: MoneyGram E-Money License Revocation by FCA
example_background: MoneyGram International, operating an e-money institution in the UK, provided digital wallet and transfer services with safeguarded client funds totaling around €500 million. In 2019-2020, the company made unauthorized changes to its safeguarding arrangements, including shifting deposits from approved UK credit institutions to overseas banks without prior FCA notification. This coincided with US enforcement actions against MoneyGram for AML issues, raising broader compliance concerns. The changes were intended to reduce costs but compromised the quality of safeguards, as the new institutions lacked equivalent regulatory oversight.
process_and_outcome: FCA revoked MoneyGram's UK e-money license in 2020 following an investigation into safeguarding failures and lack of notification. The firm ceased UK e-money activities, with funds transferred to another safeguarded entity. No direct client losses, but significant operational restrictions imposed.

example_background: Starling Bank, holding both banking and e-money permissions in the UK, safeguards e-money holder funds separately from deposit balances using dedicated segregated accounts at multiple high-rated credit institutions. In 2022, the bank notified the FCA in advance of diversifying its safeguarding custodians by adding a second tier-1 bank and procuring additional insurance from Lloyd's of London. This ensured funds exceeding the Financial Services Compensation Scheme limits remained protected. Starling's e-money balances grew to over £800 million, with monthly attestations verifying compliance.
process_and_outcome: FCA approved the changes post-notification. Independent audits confirmed full safeguarding, contributing to Starling's strong regulatory standing and expansion approvals.

example_background: ECM Benchmarks, a small UK electronic money institution offering payment services, held client funds of approximately £20 million. Between 2018 and 2021, the firm changed its safeguarding method from bank deposits to a guaranteed arrangement with an insurance undertaking without notifying the FCA at least one month in advance. Additionally, it switched the guaranteeing credit institution mid-2020 amid cost pressures, again without prior approval or notification. This led to periods where fund protection was not fully verified.
process_and_outcome: FCA investigated in 2022 and imposed a £55,000 fine in 2023 for breaching safeguarding notification requirements. ECM implemented corrective procedures and enhanced reporting.

example_name: Monzo E-Money to Banking Transition Safeguarding
example_background: Monzo, initially authorized as a UK e-money institution, safeguarded customer e-money funds in segregated accounts while pursuing a full banking license. In 2019, ahead of receiving banking authorization, Monzo notified the FCA of changes to its safeguarding setup, including migration of funds to banking-protected deposits and adjustments to insurance backstops. This handled over £2 billion in relevant balances during the transition, ensuring continuity of protection.
process_and_outcome: FCA reviewed and approved the notification process. Monzo successfully converted to a bank in 2020 with seamless safeguarding transfer, recognized in regulatory assessments.

example_name: FCA Action against DFX-EUR Custody Issues
example_background: DFX-EUR, operating as Dynamic Financial Systems Ltd, was a UK e-money institution facilitating euro e-money transfers. From 2017 to 2020, it failed to properly safeguard client funds by not maintaining adequate segregated balances and changed its custodian credit institution twice without prior FCA notification. Client funds totaling €10 million were at risk, particularly during a 2019 custody switch to a less stable institution.
process_and_outcome: FCA banned directors and imposed restrictions in 2021 after investigation. The firm wound down operations, with funds returned via administrator.

example_name: Comitology Committee Delays in REACH Implementation
example_background: The Registration, Evaluation, Authorisation and Restriction of Chemicals (REACH) Regulation required the European Commission to adopt implementing measures through comitology procedures under Council Decision 1999/468/EC for substance evaluations and authorizations. Delays occurred in forming and consulting the Member State committees, leading to postponed decisions on chemical risk assessments and market placements. Industry stakeholders reported uncertainties in compliance timelines, affecting product registrations across multiple sectors including chemicals and consumer goods manufacturing.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission faced criticism from the European Parliament and industry groups; adjustments were made to committee procedures, resulting in accelerated decision-making processes and updated implementation guidelines without formal penalties.
source_rule: (20) The measures necessary for the implementation of this Directive should be adopted in accordance with Council Decision 1999/468/EC of 28 June 1999 laying down the procedures for the exercise of implementing powers conferred on the Commission (7).

example_name: Successful Comitology Adoption for Food Contact Materials
example_background: Under Regulation (EU) No 10/2011 on plastic materials in contact with food, the European Commission utilized the regulatory committee procedure from Council Decision 1999/468/EC to approve new substances and specifications. The process involved technical assessments by the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA), followed by committee review, ensuring harmonized safety standards across EU member states for food packaging industries.
process_and_outcome: The Commission adopted multiple implementing regulations through comitology votes, such as Commission Regulation (EU) 2020/1245, enabling timely market access for compliant materials without legal challenges.
source_rule: (20) The measures necessary for the implementation of this Directive should be adopted in accordance with Council Decision 1999/468/EC of 28 June 1999 laying down the procedures for the exercise of implementing powers conferred on the Commission (7).
involved_parties: Standing Committee on Plants, Animals, Food and Feed

example_background: The Biocidal Products Regulation (EU) No 528/2012 mandated the Commission to use comitology procedures under Decision 1999/468/EC for approving active substances in biocides. Challenges arose when member states disagreed on risk assessments, delaying product renewals and authorizations, impacting pest control and disinfectant markets.
process_and_outcome: The Commission referred unresolved issues to the appeal committee; several implementing decisions were adopted, such as Regulation (EU) 2021/599, after procedural resolutions.
source_rule: (20) The measures necessary for the implementation of this Directive should be adopted in accordance with Council Decision 1999/468/EC of 28 June 1999 laying down the procedures for the exercise of implementing powers conferred on the Commission (7).

example_name: Efficient Implementation of CLP Regulation Updates
example_background: The Classification, Labelling and Packaging (CLP) Regulation (EC) No 1272/2008 required Commission adoption of implementing acts via comitology for harmonized classification and labelling of substances. The process facilitated regular updates to Annex VI based on expert group recommendations, supporting chemical safety data sheets and supply chain notifications across the EU.
process_and_outcome: Multiple Commission Regulations, like (EU) 2020/1182, were adopted through regulatory procedure with scrutiny, maintaining up-to-date classifications without delays.
source_rule: (20) The measures necessary for the implementation of this Directive should be adopted in accordance with Council Decision 1999/468/EC of 28 June 1999 laying down the procedures for the exercise of implementing powers conferred on the Commission (7).
involved_parties: Competent Authorities for REACH and CLP (CARACAL) Committee

example_name: Financial Services Comitology for MiFID II Technical Standards
example_background: Markets in Financial Instruments Directive (MiFID II) and Regulation (MiFIR) involved the Commission adopting over 70 Level 2 and 3 measures through comitology procedures under Decision 1999/468/EC. These covered algorithmic trading, position limits, and reporting requirements, addressing financial market transparency post-2008 crisis.
process_and_outcome: ESMA draft standards were endorsed by the European Securities Committee; timely adoption of Implementing Regulation (EU) 2017/1781 ensured market readiness by January 2018.
source_rule: (20) The measures necessary for the implementation of this Directive should be adopted in accordance with Council Decision 1999/468/EC of 28 June 1999 laying down the procedures for the exercise of implementing powers conferred on the Commission (7).

example_name: ESMA Guidelines Non-Compliance Challenge via Comitology
example_background: Under the European Market Infrastructure Regulation (EMIR), the Commission was to adopt implementing acts through comitology for clearing and reporting obligations. Delays in committee approvals for position reporting amendments created inconsistencies in national implementations, affecting derivatives trading firms.
process_and_outcome: The Commission adopted delayed measures like Delegated Regulation (EU) 2021/935 after appeal committee review, with national regulators adjusting compliance deadlines.
source_rule: (20) The measures necessary for the implementation of this Directive should be adopted in accordance with Council Decision 1999/468/EC of 28 June 1999 laying down the procedures for the exercise of implementing powers conferred on the Commission (7).

example_name: CRD IV Capital Requirements Implementing Measures
example_background: The Capital Requirements Directive IV (CRD IV) required the Commission to use comitology for implementing technical standards on supervisory reporting and public disclosures. This ensured consistent prudential rules for credit institutions across the EU banking sector.
process_and_outcome: The Banking Stakeholder Group and EBA inputs led to smooth adoption of Implementing Regulation (EU) No 680/2014, with periodic amendments maintaining framework stability.
source_rule: (20) The measures necessary for the implementation of this Directive should be adopted in accordance with Council Decision 1999/468/EC of 28 June 1999 laying down the procedures for the exercise of implementing powers conferred on the Commission (7).

example_name: Controversy over Short Selling Regulation Amendments
example_background: The Short Selling Regulation (EU) No 236/2012 saw proposed amendments requiring comitology approval under Decision 1999/468/EC for position reporting thresholds. Member state divergences in the committee delayed updates amid market volatility concerns.
process_and_outcome: After extended committee deliberations, the Commission withdrew and revised proposals, adopting targeted measures via Regulation (EU) 2023/2407 without full consensus.
source_rule: (20) The measures necessary for the implementation of this Directive should be adopted in accordance with Council Decision 1999/468/EC of 28 June 1999 laying down the procedures for the exercise of implementing powers conferred on the Commission (7).

example_name: Smooth Rollout of PRIIPs Key Information Documents
example_background: The Packaged Retail and Insurance-based Investment Products (PRIIPs) Regulation (EU) No 1286/2014 mandated comitology procedures for implementing technical standards on key information documents (KIDs). This standardized retail investment disclosures across EU financial markets.
process_and_outcome: ESMA and EIOPA drafts were approved by committees, leading to Delegated Regulation (EU) 2017/653 effective from 2018, facilitating product distribution.
source_rule: (20) The measures necessary for the implementation of this Directive should be adopted in accordance with Council Decision 1999/468/EC of 28 June 1999 laying down the procedures for the exercise of implementing powers conferred on the Commission (7).
involved_parties: European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority (EIOPA)

example_name: European Commission Report on PSD2 Transposition 2021
example_background: The Payment Services Directive 2 (PSD2) required transposition into national law by Member States by January 13, 2018. The European Commission conducted a comprehensive review three years after the transposition deadline, as mandated by Article 109 of PSD2. This review assessed the implementation and application of key provisions across all EU Member States, including open banking requirements, strong customer authentication, and payment initiation services. Member States were required to submit detailed information on their transposition measures, enforcement activities, and challenges encountered. The report highlighted divergences in national implementations, such as varying timelines for API access and differences in regulatory sandboxes. It also evaluated the flow of data from information submitted by national competent authorities on incidents, complaints, and compliance levels. The review process involved bilateral discussions with Member States and stakeholder consultations to gather evidence on the Directive's effectiveness in fostering innovation and competition in the payments sector while ensuring consumer protection.
process_and_outcome: The Commission published the report on December 17, 2021, identifying areas for improvement such as harmonization of strong customer authentication exemptions and better supervision of third-party providers. No infringement procedures were initiated based on the report, but recommendations were made for future legislative adjustments. Member States were urged to enhance reporting mechanisms.
source_rule: (22) The efficient functioning of this Directive will need to be reviewed. The Commission should therefore be required to produce a report three years after the deadline for transposition of this Directive. Member States should provide to the Commission information regarding the application of some of the provisions of this Directive.

example_name: European Commission Report on CRD V Transposition 2023
example_background: Directive (EU) 2019/878 (CRD V), part of the Banking Package, set a transposition deadline of June 28, 2021. Article 88 required the Commission to review the Directive's functioning three years after transposition and report to the European Parliament and Council, based on information provided by Member States on application of provisions related to remuneration policies, liquidity requirements, and macroprudential tools. Member States submitted transposition tables, national legislation excerpts, and data on supervisory practices. The review examined compliance with Basel III standards, impact on bank resilience post-COVID, and effectiveness of reporting obligations. It included analysis of over 100 documents from national authorities and quantitative data on indicators like leverage ratios and liquidity coverage ratios across jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: The report was published in June 2024, concluding that transposition was largely achieved but noted inconsistencies in areas like multiple distribution channels for investment services. The Commission proposed targeted amendments via CRR III/CRD VI and ongoing monitoring.
source_rule: (22) The efficient functioning of this Directive will need to be reviewed. The Commission should therefore be required to produce a report three years after the deadline for transposition of this Directive. Member States should provide to the Commission information regarding the application of some of the provisions of this Directive.

example_background: The first Payment Services Directive (PSD1, Directive 2007/64/EC) had a transposition deadline of November 1, 2009. Recital 61 and Article 85 mandated the Commission to deliver a report three years post-transposition, three years after entry into force, and every three years thereafter, using information from Member States on provisions like payment execution timelines, consumer rights, and liability frameworks. Member States provided annual reports on application, including statistics on payment volumes, complaint numbers, and enforcement cases. The review covered the Directive's role in creating a single euro payments area (SEPA), challenges with cross-border payments, and variances in national implementations, such as differing definitions of payment accounts.
process_and_outcome: The 2010 report assessed progress toward SEPA goals, identified gaps in execution times, and led to the development of PSD2. It prompted infringement proceedings against several Member States for incomplete transposition.
source_rule: (22) The efficient functioning of this Directive will need to be reviewed. The Commission should therefore be required to produce a report three years after the deadline for transposition of this Directive. Member States should provide to the Commission information regarding the application of some of the provisions of this Directive.

example_background: Markets in Financial Instruments Directive II (MiFID II, Directive 2014/65/EU) required transposition by January 3, 2018. Article 92 obligated the Commission to review and report three years after transposition, soliciting information from Member States on product governance, inducements, transaction reporting, and investor protection measures. Member States submitted data via ESMA templates on supervisory activities, breach notifications, and market abuse cases. The review analyzed over 27,000 breach reports and assessed the regime's impact on trading costs and transparency post-2018 implementation.
process_and_outcome: The May 2022 report found the framework broadly effective but recommended simplifications in reporting obligations and better proportionality for smaller firms. It influenced the MiFIR review and policy adjustments.
source_rule: (22) The efficient functioning of this Directive will need to be reviewed. The Commission should therefore be required to produce a report three years after the deadline for transposition of this Directive. Member States should provide to the Commission information regarding the application of some of the provisions of this Directive.

example_background: The European Market Infrastructure Regulation (EMIR, Regulation (EU) No 648/2012), as amended by EMIR Refit, required a Commission report three years after the 2019 application date of certain provisions, per Article 12a. Member States and ESMA provided data on clearing obligations, risk mitigation for non-financial counterparties, and reporting under Article 9. The review encompassed analysis of over 100 million trade reports, incident data, and national supervisory reports on derivatives market stability.
process_and_outcome: The report confirmed EMIR's contribution to financial stability and proposed further simplifications, leading to targeted consultations and delegated acts adjustments.
source_rule: (22) The efficient functioning of this Directive will need to be reviewed. The Commission should therefore be required to produce a report three years after the deadline for transposition of this Directive. Member States should provide to the Commission information regarding the application of some of the provisions of this Directive.

example_background: The Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD, Directive (EU) 2022/2464) sets transposition by July 6, 2024. Article 49a requires a Commission report three years post-transposition, based on Member State information on application of reporting standards, assurance requirements, and digital tagging. Though the report is pending, Member States are preparing data submission frameworks aligned with EFRAG standards.
process_and_outcome: Ongoing; national authorities are establishing reporting portals and pilot programs to facilitate future Commission review.
source_rule: (22) The efficient functioning of this Directive will need to be reviewed. The Commission should therefore be required to produce a report three years after the deadline for transposition of this Directive. Member States should provide to the Commission information regarding the application of some of the provisions of this Directive.

example_background: The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR, Regulation (EU) 2016/679) entered application on May 25, 2018. Article 97(1)(c) requires a review report two years after application, with subsequent reports; the Commission issued a two-year report using Member State inputs on enforcement, fines, and DPIA applications. Data included over 140,000 complaints and €500 million in fines across jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: The June 2020 report evaluated enforcement consistency, leading to guidelines on video surveillance and proposals for GDPR strengthening via Data Act.

Member States should provide to the Commission information regarding the application of some of the provisions of this Directive.

example_background: AMLD5 (Directive (EU) 2018/843) had a transposition deadline of January 10, 2020. Article 64 required Commission reports on application, with Member States providing data on beneficial ownership registers, crypto-asset measures, and high-risk third-country reporting. The review assessed over 20 national registers and enforcement statistics.
process_and_outcome: 2022 interim report noted delays in some states, prompting infringement actions against 13 Member States; full review ongoing.

example_name: Capital Requirements Directive IV (CRD IV) Review 2016
example_background: CRD IV (Directive 2013/36/EU) transposition deadline was June 2014. Article 120 mandated a three-year post-transposition report using Member State information on governance, remuneration, and Pillar 2 requirements. The review analyzed EBA data on 150 banks and national practices.
process_and_outcome: The 2016 report led to CRD V amendments for better harmonization.
