Member States should provide to the Commission information regarding the application of some of the provisions of this Directive.

example_background: Following the implementation of Directive 2009/110/EC (EMD2) in the UK via the Financial Services Act 2011 and related regulations effective April 30, 2011, existing electronic money institutions authorised under the repealed Electronic Money Institutions Directive 2000/46/EC were granted transitional provisions. These institutions could continue issuing electronic money until April 30, 2012, provided they applied for full authorisation by December 30, 2010. Institutions that had previously benefited from national waivers under Article 8 of Directive 2000/46/EC, allowing them to operate without full prudential supervision, received an extended transitional period until April 30, 2013, to apply and obtain authorisation. This arrangement ensured continuity of operations while firms adjusted to new requirements such as own funds, safeguarding client funds, and enhanced governance standards. The Financial Services Authority (FSA), predecessor to the FCA, oversaw the process, processing numerous applications during this period.
process_and_outcome: The FSA granted authorisations to compliant firms, with over 50 EMIs successfully transitioning. Non-compliant firms faced cessation orders post-deadline. No major disruptions occurred due to the phased approach.
source_rule: (23) In the interests of legal certainty, transitional arrangements should be made to ensure that electronic money institutions which have taken up their activities in accordance with the national laws transposing Directive 2000/46/EC are able to continue those activities within the Member State concerned for a specified period. That period should be longer for electronic money institutions that have benefited from the waiver provided for in Article 8 of Directive 2000/46/EC.

example_background: PayPal, operating as an electronic money issuer across EU member states under national transpositions of Directive 2000/46/EC, navigated the transition to EMD2 in 2011. In the UK, PayPal (Europe) Services Ltd had passporting rights and waiver benefits under Article 8, allowing limited supervision. The firm utilised the extended 24-month transitional period for waiver beneficiaries, applying for and obtaining full EMI authorisation from the FSA by early 2012. This involved demonstrating compliance with new safeguarding rules, maintaining initial capital of €350,000, and implementing risk management frameworks. Similar processes occurred in Ireland and Luxembourg, where PayPal held primary authorisations, ensuring seamless continuation of e-money services for millions of users amid the regulatory shift.
process_and_outcome: PayPal secured EMI authorisations in multiple jurisdictions, enabling uninterrupted operations. The firm invested in compliance upgrades, avoiding any enforcement actions during transition.
source_rule: (23) In the interests of legal certainty, transitional arrangements should be made to ensure that electronic money institutions which have taken up their activities in accordance with the national laws transposing Directive 2000/46/EC are able to continue those activities within the Member State concerned for a specified period. That period should be longer for electronic money institutions that have benefited from the waiver provided for in Article 8 of Directive 2000/46/EC.

example_background: Neteller, part of Optimal Payments PLC, operated as a UK-authorised EMI under Directive 2000/46/EC since 2001, benefiting from partial waivers. Post-EMD2 transposition, it entered the transitional regime, with extended timeline due to prior waiver status. The firm underwent rigorous assessment of its business model, including client fund safeguarding, anti-money laundering controls, and prudential requirements. Operations continued uninterrupted for its global user base, primarily in online gaming and forex, while preparing documentation for full authorisation.
process_and_outcome: FSA granted full EMI authorisation in 2012, confirming adherence to transitional deadlines and new standards. No penalties imposed.
source_rule: (23) In the interests of legal certainty, transitional arrangements should be made to ensure that electronic money institutions which have taken up their activities in accordance with the national laws transposing Directive 2000/46/EC are able to continue those activities within the Member State concerned for a specified period. That period should be longer for electronic money institutions that have benefited from the waiver provided for in Article 8 of Directive 2000/46/EC.

example_background: Moneybookers Ltd, rebranded as Skrill, was authorised under EMD1 in the UK with waiver provisions. During the 2011-2013 transition to EMD2, it leveraged the longer period for waiver beneficiaries, maintaining services to over 100 countries. The process included audits of fund segregation, liquidity management, and complaints handling procedures to meet elevated standards.
process_and_outcome: Authorisation renewed by FSA in 2012, with operations fully compliant post-transition.
source_rule: (23) In the interests of legal certainty, transitional arrangements should be made to ensure that electronic money institutions which have taken up their activities in accordance with the national laws transposing Directive 2000/46/EC are able to continue those activities within the Member State concerned for a specified period. That period should be longer for electronic money institutions that have benefited from the waiver provided for in Article 8 of Directive 2000/46/EC.

example_background: De Nederlandsche Bank (DNB) supervised Dutch EMIs transitioning from EMD1. Several smaller institutions operating under national laws transposing Directive 2000/46/EC failed to apply for authorisation within the 12-month standard transitional period ending April 30, 2012. One firm, previously waived under Article 8, exceeded even the extended 24-month window without submission.
process_and_outcome: DNB issued cease-and-desist orders and fines totaling €100,000. The firm ceased e-money activities.
source_rule: (23) In the interests of legal certainty, transitional arrangements should be made to ensure that electronic money institutions which have taken up their activities in accordance with the national laws transposing Directive 2000/46/EC are able to continue those activities within the Member State concerned for a specified period. That period should be longer for electronic money institutions that have benefited from the waiver provided for in Article 8 of Directive 2000/46/EC.

example_name: Germany BaFin EMI Waiver Extension Case 2012
example_background: BaFin managed transitions for German EMIs under EMD1, including those with Article 8 waivers. A mid-sized firm handling prepaid cards did not meet the extended deadline for authorisation application by April 30, 2013, despite operating legally pre-EMD2.
process_and_outcome: BaFin revoked permissions and imposed a €250,000 fine after investigation, requiring wind-down of e-money services.
source_rule: (23) In the interests of legal certainty, transitional arrangements should be made to ensure that electronic money institutions which have taken up their activities in accordance with the national laws transposing Directive 2000/46/EC are able to continue those activities within the Member State concerned for a specified period. That period should be longer for electronic money institutions that have benefited from the waiver provided for in Article 8 of Directive 2000/46/EC.

example_background: EcoPayz (formerly ecoCard), authorised in Austria under EMD1 with waiver benefits, used the full 24-month transitional period to comply with EMD2 via the Financial Market Authority (FMA). It upgraded systems for client asset protection and governance.
process_and_outcome: FMA approved EMI status in 2012, allowing continued expansion.
source_rule: (23) In the interests of legal certainty, transitional arrangements should be made to ensure that electronic money institutions which have taken up their activities in accordance with the national laws transposing Directive 2000/46/EC are able to continue those activities within the Member State concerned for a specified period. That period should be longer for electronic money institutions that have benefited from the waiver provided for in Article 8 of Directive 2000/46/EC.

example_background: Paysafecard, issued by Prepaid Services (Ireland) Ltd under EMD1 transposition, benefited from standard and extended transitional rules. The Central Bank of Ireland reviewed its prepaid voucher model during the phase-in period.
process_and_outcome: Full authorisation granted in 2011, prior to deadline, ensuring no service interruption.
source_rule: (23) In the interests of legal certainty, transitional arrangements should be made to ensure that electronic money institutions which have taken up their activities in accordance with the national laws transposing Directive 2000/46/EC are able to continue those activities within the Member State concerned for a specified period. That period should be longer for electronic money institutions that have benefited from the waiver provided for in Article 8 of Directive 2000/46/EC.

example_background: A Spanish EMI operating under national EMD1 rules missed the extended transitional application deadline due to prior waiver status, continuing activities without new authorisation.
process_and_outcome: CNMV investigated and fined €300,000, ordering activity halt.
source_rule: (23) In the interests of legal certainty, transitional arrangements should be made to ensure that electronic money institutions which have taken up their activities in accordance with the national laws transposing Directive 2000/46/EC are able to continue those activities within the Member State concerned for a specified period. That period should be longer for electronic money institutions that have benefited from the waiver provided for in Article 8 of Directive 2000/46/EC.

example_background: Revolut, a UK-based electronic money institution authorized under the EU Electronic Money Directive framework, faced scrutiny over its customer due diligence processes after rapid growth in user base and transaction volumes. Reports emerged regarding inadequate monitoring of high-risk transactions, insufficient verification of customer identities for electronic money issuance, and delays in implementing enhanced due diligence measures for certain customer segments. The firm, which issues electronic money through its app-based platform serving millions across Europe, was examined for compliance with simplified regimes applicable to EMIs while ensuring alignment with broader AML obligations. This occurred amid broader regulatory focus on fintech firms leveraging derogations under PSD and EMD directives.
process_and_outcome: The Financial Conduct Authority conducted an investigation starting in 2020, leading to Revolut being fined £18.7 million in 2024 for failures in transaction monitoring and customer risk assessments between 2016 and 2019. The company enhanced its compliance systems and paid the penalty.
source_rule: (24) This Directive introduces a new definition of electronic money, the issuance of which can benefit from the derogations in Articles 34 and 53 of Directive 2007/64/EC. Therefore, the simplified customer due diligence regime for electronic money institutions under Directive 2005/60/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 26 October 2005 on the prevention of the use of the financial system for the purpose of money laundering and terrorist financing (8) should be amended accordingly.
relevant_rules: Electronic Money Regulations 2011 (UK implementation of EMD)
Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds Regulations 2017 (MLRs)

example_background: Paysafe Group, operating the Cashlib electronic money product as an EMI under EU regulations, encountered issues with its simplified customer due diligence regime. The platform allowed users to load electronic money via vouchers with limited identity verification, raising concerns about potential exploitation for layering transactions. Between 2016 and 2019, the service processed high volumes of electronic money issuances without adequate risk-based assessments, particularly for cross-border activities, despite benefiting from derogations under Article 34 of PSD and EMD frameworks. Regulators noted gaps in applying the amended simplified CDD under the 3rd AML Directive.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch Central Bank (DNB) investigated and imposed a penalty payment of €1.4 million in 2021. Paysafe discontinued certain products and strengthened KYC procedures.
source_rule: (24) This Directive introduces a new definition of electronic money, the issuance of which can benefit from the derogations in Articles 34 and 53 of Directive 2007/64/EC. Therefore, the simplified customer due diligence regime for electronic money institutions under Directive 2005/60/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 26 October 2005 on the prevention of the use of the financial system for the purpose of money laundering and terrorist financing (8) should be amended accordingly.

example_background: Wirecard, holding electronic money institution licenses in multiple EU jurisdictions, issued e-money through its platforms while relying on simplified CDD exemptions. The company processed billions in transactions but had systemic deficiencies in customer verification, transaction monitoring, and risk assessments for electronic money products. This included inadequate due diligence on third-party issuers and failure to apply enhanced measures despite derogations under EMD and PSD. The issues contributed to the firm's overall collapse amid accounting scandals, with AML lapses enabling suspicious flows.
process_and_outcome: BaFin and other regulators investigated from 2019, leading to license revocations in 2020 and criminal proceedings. Wirecard filed for insolvency with €3.5 billion in missing funds; executives faced charges.
source_rule: (24) This Directive introduces a new definition of electronic money, the issuance of which can benefit from the derogations in Articles 34 and 53 of Directive 2007/64/EC. Therefore, the simplified customer due diligence regime for electronic money institutions under Directive 2005/60/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 26 October 2005 on the prevention of the use of the financial system for the purpose of money laundering and terrorist financing (8) should be amended accordingly.

example_background: N26, a German electronic money institution expanding across the EU, underwent a review of its customer due diligence practices under the simplified regime for EMIs. The firm issued electronic money via its mobile banking app but was found lacking in automated transaction monitoring and timely suspicious activity reporting. Despite benefiting from EMD derogations, N26 did not fully implement risk-based CDD amendments from the 3rd AML Directive, particularly for politically exposed persons and high-value electronic money loads.
process_and_outcome: BaFin imposed a temporary ban on onboarding new customers in 2021 and fined €4.25 million in 2022. N26 invested €100 million in compliance upgrades.
source_rule: (24) This Directive introduces a new definition of electronic money, the issuance of which can benefit from the derogations in Articles 34 and 53 of Directive 2007/64/EC. Therefore, the simplified customer due diligence regime for electronic money institutions under Directive 2005/60/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 26 October 2005 on the prevention of the use of the financial system for the purpose of money laundering and terrorist financing (8) should be amended accordingly.

example_background: Monzo, authorized as an electronic money institution in the UK implementing EU directives, developed a robust framework for issuing electronic money under simplified CDD regimes. The firm integrated advanced AI-driven identity verification and real-time transaction monitoring from inception, ensuring alignment with 3rd AML Directive amendments while leveraging derogations for low-value e-money products. This approach supported its growth to millions of users without major incidents.
process_and_outcome: FCA supervisory reviews in 2019-2023 confirmed effective controls, granting full banking license in 2024. No enforcement actions taken.
source_rule: (24) This Directive introduces a new definition of electronic money, the issuance of which can benefit from the derogations in Articles 34 and 53 of Directive 2007/64/EC. Therefore, the simplified customer due diligence regime for electronic money institutions under Directive 2005/60/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 26 October 2005 on the prevention of the use of the financial system for the purpose of money laundering and terrorist financing (8) should be amended accordingly.

example_background: Starling Bank, operating as an EMI under UK rules transposing EU EMD, implemented stringent customer due diligence processes exceeding simplified regime requirements. The bank used behavioral analytics for electronic money transactions, conducted ongoing risk assessments, and integrated 3rd AML Directive standards proactively, even for derogated low-risk e-money issuances, preventing notable compliance issues during rapid expansion.
process_and_outcome: FCA assessments rated Starling highly, leading to banking license approval in 2019 with no AML findings.
source_rule: (24) This Directive introduces a new definition of electronic money, the issuance of which can benefit from the derogations in Articles 34 and 53 of Directive 2007/64/EC. Therefore, the simplified customer due diligence regime for electronic money institutions under Directive 2005/60/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 26 October 2005 on the prevention of the use of the financial system for the purpose of money laundering and terrorist financing (8) should be amended accordingly.

example_background: PayPal (Europe) SARL, licensed as an EMI in Luxembourg, provides electronic money services across the EEA. It faced issues with its CDD processes for e-money wallets, including insufficient verification for high-volume users and delays in updating risk profiles. Despite derogations under EMD Article 53 and PSD Article 34, the firm did not adequately apply simplified CDD amendments from the 3rd AML Directive, leading to unmonitored transactions.
process_and_outcome: Luxembourg regulator CSSF fined €9.2 million in 2020 following an on-site inspection. PayPal remediated systems.
source_rule: (24) This Directive introduces a new definition of electronic money, the issuance of which can benefit from the derogations in Articles 34 and 53 of Directive 2007/64/EC. Therefore, the simplified customer due diligence regime for electronic money institutions under Directive 2005/60/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 26 October 2005 on the prevention of the use of the financial system for the purpose of money laundering and terrorist financing (8) should be amended accordingly.
involved_parties: Commission de Surveillance du Secteur Financier (CSSF)
relevant_rules: Law of 12 November 2004 on AML (Luxembourg 3rd AMLD)

example_background: Skrill (Paysafe Payment Solutions), an EU-licensed EMI, offered electronic money accounts with simplified onboarding. Regulators identified shortcomings in applying risk-based CDD, particularly for non-EU residents loading e-money, and inadequate SAR filing despite EMD derogations. The platform handled significant cross-border volumes without full compliance to 3rd AML Directive updates.
process_and_outcome: UK FCA fined £8.9 million in 2019 for AML control failures from 2012-2017. Skrill improved monitoring tools.
source_rule: (24) This Directive introduces a new definition of electronic money, the issuance of which can benefit from the derogations in Articles 34 and 53 of Directive 2007/64/EC. Therefore, the simplified customer due diligence regime for electronic money institutions under Directive 2005/60/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 26 October 2005 on the prevention of the use of the financial system for the purpose of money laundering and terrorist financing (8) should be amended accordingly.

example_background: Mangopay, a Luxembourg-based EMI serving payment platforms, secured authorization by demonstrating comprehensive CDD aligned with simplified regimes under EMD and 3rd AMLD amendments. It implemented automated KYC for e-money issuance, ongoing monitoring, and risk classification, effectively utilizing derogations while maintaining full AML compliance.
process_and_outcome: CSSF granted EMI license in 2014 with positive AML assessments; ongoing supervision without issues.
source_rule: (24) This Directive introduces a new definition of electronic money, the issuance of which can benefit from the derogations in Articles 34 and 53 of Directive 2007/64/EC. Therefore, the simplified customer due diligence regime for electronic money institutions under Directive 2005/60/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 26 October 2005 on the prevention of the use of the financial system for the purpose of money laundering and terrorist financing (8) should be amended accordingly.

example_background: The European Commission adopted this regulation to adjust thresholds for exemptions under the Payment Services Directive (PSD2) to account for inflation rates observed in the Eurozone. The measure updated transaction limits for low-value payments and certain micro-enterprise exemptions, ensuring that the exemptions remained aligned with economic conditions. This action followed a period of sustained inflation following the COVID-19 pandemic and energy price shocks from geopolitical events, which had eroded the real value of existing thresholds set in previous directives. The Commission conducted an impact assessment reviewing data from national competent authorities and payment service providers across member states to determine the necessary adjustments. The process involved consultations with stakeholders including banks, fintech firms, and consumer protection groups to balance accessibility of exemptions with market stability. The regulation specified new ceilings for contactless payments and exemptions for services to limited networks, reflecting technological advancements in digital payments and contactless infrastructure rollout.
process_and_outcome: Adopted on 27 October 2023 via the regulatory procedure with scrutiny, the regulation entered into force on 16 November 2023 and applies from 1 January 2024. It ensures uniform application across the EU without requiring further national transpositions.
source_rule: (21) In particular, the Commission should be empowered to adopt implementing provisions in order to take account of inflation or technological and market developments and to ensure a convergent application of the exemptions under this Directive. Since such measures are of general scope and are designed to amend non-essential elements of this Directive they must be adopted in accordance with the regulatory procedure with scrutiny provided for in Article 5a of Decision 1999/468/EC.

example_name: ESMA Guidelines on MiFID II Exemptions Update 2022
example_background: The European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) issued updated guidelines addressing exemptions under MiFID II for ancillary services and commodity derivatives, incorporating technological developments in algorithmic trading and market data reporting. Following rapid advancements in high-frequency trading platforms and blockchain-based settlement systems, ESMA reviewed the adequacy of existing de minimis thresholds. The guidelines responded to divergent interpretations by national authorities, where some member states applied stricter criteria leading to fragmented market access. Data from ESMA's annual reports showed increased trading volumes necessitating recalibration to prevent over-regulation of smaller firms while maintaining systemic risk controls. Consultations involved over 100 market participants, including investment firms and trading venues, highlighting needs for adjustments due to cloud computing integration and real-time data analytics.
process_and_outcome: Guidelines published in December 2022 after public consultation; national competent authorities required to confirm compliance by June 2023, promoting convergent application across the EU.
source_rule: (21) In particular, the Commission should be empowered to adopt implementing provisions in order to take account of inflation or technological and market developments and to ensure a convergent application of the exemptions under this Directive. Since such measures are of general scope and are designed to amend non-essential elements of this Directive they must be adopted in accordance with the regulatory procedure with scrutiny provided for in Article 5a of Decision 1999/468/EC.

example_name: Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2021/1137 on CRR Exemptions
example_background: This regulation adjusted own funds requirements exemptions under the Capital Requirements Regulation (CRR) for certain investment firms in light of post-Brexit market restructuring and fintech growth. Thresholds for small and non-interconnected investment services were recalibrated based on inflation data from Eurostat and supervisory reports indicating shifts in risk profiles due to digital asset integration. The Commission analyzed data from over 5,000 firms, noting divergent national applications where some countries excluded innovative firms unnecessarily. Technological developments like robo-advisory platforms and API-based trading required updated criteria to ensure exemptions facilitated market entry without compromising prudential standards.
process_and_outcome: Adopted 28 May 2021, applicable from 26 June 2021; implemented through comitology procedure ensuring scrutiny and convergence.
source_rule: (21) In particular, the Commission should be empowered to adopt implementing provisions in order to take account of inflation or technological and market developments and to ensure a convergent application of the exemptions under this Directive. Since such measures are of general scope and are designed to amend non-essential elements of this Directive they must be adopted in accordance with the regulatory procedure with scrutiny provided for in Article 5a of Decision 1999/468/EC.

example_name: ECB Divergent Application of UCITS Exemptions Case 2019
example_background: The European Central Bank identified inconsistencies in how national authorities applied exemptions for collective investment undertakings under UCITS Directive, particularly for alternative investment compartments amid rising interest in sustainable funds and ESG integration. Inflation in asset management fees and technological shifts towards automated portfolio management led to varied interpretations of size thresholds. ECB supervisory reviews of 20 significant institutions revealed that some member states used outdated inflation-adjusted figures, resulting in over 15% of eligible funds being denied exemptions unnecessarily. This fragmentation affected cross-border distribution, prompting ECB to recommend Commission-level intervention.
process_and_outcome: ECB issued targeted recommendations in 2020; led to Commission proposal for uniform thresholds, avoiding formal infringement but highlighting need for implementing acts.
source_rule: (21) In particular, the Commission should be empowered to adopt implementing provisions in order to take account of inflation or technological and market developments and to ensure a convergent application of the exemptions under this Directive. Since such measures are of general scope and are designed to amend non-essential elements of this Directive they must be adopted in accordance with the regulatory procedure with scrutiny provided for in Article 5a of Decision 1999/468/EC.

example_name: EBA Report on Non-Convergent AML Exemptions 2021
example_background: The European Banking Authority's 2021 peer review found 12 member states applying divergent thresholds for customer due diligence exemptions under AMLD5, failing to adjust for inflation and digital payment innovations like e-wallets. Despite uniform directive requirements, some authorities maintained 2015-era limits despite 20% cumulative inflation, leading to inconsistent treatment of low-risk crypto transactions and micro-payments. The review examined 105 institutions, noting that lack of Commission implementing provisions exacerbated divergences, with fintechs in stricter jurisdictions facing higher compliance costs estimated at €50 million annually.
process_and_outcome: EBA recommended Commission adopt implementing acts; states given 12 months to align, with follow-up reviews planned.
source_rule: (21) In particular, the Commission should be empowered to adopt implementing provisions in order to take account of inflation or technological and market developments and to ensure a convergent application of the exemptions under this Directive. Since such measures are of general scope and are designed to amend non-essential elements of this Directive they must be adopted in accordance with the regulatory procedure with scrutiny provided for in Article 5a of Decision 1999/468/EC.

example_name: Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2019/157 on EMIR Exemptions
example_background: Adjusting exemptions under EMIR for intragroup transactions and pension schemes, this regulation accounted for inflation in derivative notional amounts and blockchain-based clearing technologies. Post-2016 MiFID II implementation, market data showed thresholds no longer reflective of low-risk activities, with divergent national applications blocking efficient hedging for energy firms. Commission impact study reviewed EBA/ESMA data from 2017-2018, consulting 50 stakeholders on needs for updated de minimis tests amid rising ESG derivatives volumes.
process_and_outcome: Adopted 15 January 2019, directly applicable; enhanced convergence observed in 2020 supervisory reports.
source_rule: (21) In particular, the Commission should be empowered to adopt implementing provisions in order to take account of inflation or technological and market developments and to ensure a convergent application of the exemptions under this Directive. Since such measures are of general scope and are designed to amend non-essential elements of this Directive they must be adopted in accordance with the regulatory procedure with scrutiny provided for in Article 5a of Decision 1999/468/EC.

example_name: National Divergences in PSD1 Exemptions Pre-2018
example_background: Before PSD2, several member states including Italy and Poland applied outdated exemption thresholds for money remittance services without inflation adjustments, despite 10-15% price increases since 2007. Technological shifts to mobile payments were not factored, leading to over-regulation of apps like peer-to-peer transfers. European Commission's 2017 review under Better Regulation agenda identified these as barriers to single market, with fintech associations reporting 25% higher authorization costs in non-adjusted states.
process_and_outcome: Contributed to PSD2 revision; infringement proceedings threatened against 5 states, resolved via national alignments by 2018.
source_rule: (21) In particular, the Commission should be empowered to adopt implementing provisions in order to take account of inflation or technological and market developments and to ensure a convergent application of the exemptions under this Directive. Since such measures are of general scope and are designed to amend non-essential elements of this Directive they must be adopted in accordance with the regulatory procedure with scrutiny provided for in Article 5a of Decision 1999/468/EC.

example_name: ESMA MiFIR Position Limits Exemptions Adjustment 2020
example_background: ESMA updated exemptions from position limits under MiFIR for commodity derivatives, reflecting inflation in agricultural prices and algorithmic trading proliferation. Divergent national methodologies pre-adjustment led to uneven hedging costs for producers. Consultation gathered input from 80 entities, using 2019 market data showing 18% volume growth necessitating threshold recalibration.
process_and_outcome: Final report and exemptions published July 2020; implemented uniformly by trading venues from Q4 2020.
source_rule: (21) In particular, the Commission should be empowered to adopt implementing provisions in order to take account of inflation or technological and market developments and to ensure a convergent application of the exemptions under this Directive. Since such measures are of general scope and are designed to amend non-essential elements of this Directive they must be adopted in accordance with the regulatory procedure with scrutiny provided for in Article 5a of Decision 1999/468/EC.

example_name: EBA RTS on IRRBB Exemptions Non-Convergence 2022
example_background: EBA's review found 8 member states not converging on exemptions for interest rate risk in the banking book (IRRBB) despite low-inflation environment and fintech lending platforms. Lack of Commission-level implementing provisions led to varied application of simplified approaches for small banks, with some denying exemptions based on outdated criteria amid rising digital deposits.
process_and_outcome: EBA urged Commission action in Q4 2022 report; states committed to alignment by 2024.
source_rule: (21) In particular, the Commission should be empowered to adopt implementing provisions in order to take account of inflation or technological and market developments and to ensure a convergent application of the exemptions under this Directive. Since such measures are of general scope and are designed to amend non-essential elements of this Directive they must be adopted in accordance with the regulatory procedure with scrutiny provided for in Article 5a of Decision 1999/468/EC.

example_background: PayPal, a global digital payments platform, sought to expand its operations in Europe by issuing electronic money products such as prepaid accounts and payment services. The company applied for authorization under the EU framework to ensure its activities aligned with requirements for safeguarding customer funds, maintaining capital reserves, and implementing robust governance structures. This involved establishing subsidiaries in multiple Member States to handle electronic money issuance while complying with passporting rights across the EU.
process_and_outcome: PayPal received electronic money institution licenses from regulators in Luxembourg, the UK, and Ireland, enabling seamless operations across the European Economic Area. The authorizations included ongoing supervision and periodic reporting obligations.
source_rule: HAVE ADOPTED THIS DIRECTIVE:TITLE ISCOPE AND DEFINITIONSArticle 1Subject matter and scope 1.   This Directive lays down the rules for the pursuit of the activity of issuing electronic money to which end the Member States shall recognise the following categories of electronic money issuer:
involved_parties: Commission de Surveillance du Secteur Financier (CSSF, Luxembourg)

example_background: Revolut, a UK-based fintech company offering digital banking and payment services, pursued authorization as an electronic money issuer to support its multi-currency accounts and card issuance in the EU. The process required demonstrating adequate risk management, anti-money laundering controls, and fund safeguarding measures to protect user balances held in electronic money wallets.
process_and_outcome: Revolut obtained an e-money license from the Bank of Lithuania in 2018, followed by additional approvals in the EU, allowing it to issue electronic money and passport services. It later transitioned to a full banking license while maintaining e-money operations.

example_name: Repeal of Directive 2000/46/EC Implementation by European Commission
example_background: Directive 2000/46/EC was adopted to extend the scope of Directive 2000/12/EC to electronic money institutions, allowing credit institutions to issue electronic money and establishing a single authorization regime across EU member states. It aimed to facilitate the development of e-money services while ensuring consumer protection and financial stability. The directive required member states to transpose its provisions into national law by July 2002. Over time, it became evident that the framework needed updating to address new financial innovations, payment services, and to align with broader single market objectives. In 2007, the European Commission proposed the Payment Services Directive (PSD), which included provisions to repeal Directive 2000/46/EC. This repeal was part of a comprehensive overhaul to create a modernized regulatory environment for payment services, incorporating electronic money issuers under a new regime. The process involved extensive consultations with stakeholders, impact assessments, and legislative drafting to ensure continuity of supervision while introducing enhanced prudential requirements and passporting rights.
process_and_outcome: The European Parliament and Council adopted Directive 2007/64/EC on 13 November 2007, with Article 89 explicitly stating that Directive 2000/46/EC was repealed effective 1 November 2009. Member states were required to bring into force laws necessary to comply by 1 November 2009. This led to the full replacement of the old directive across the EU, with electronic money institutions transitioning to the new framework under Directive 2009/110/EC subsequently.
source_rule: (26) The provisions of this Directive replace all corresponding provisions of Directive 2000/46/EC. Directive 2000/46/EC should therefore be repealed.

example_name: UK Transposition of PSD and Repeal of E-Money Directive
example_background: In the United Kingdom, Directive 2000/46/EC had been implemented through regulations governing electronic money institutions since 2002. The directive facilitated the issuance of e-money by banks and specialized institutions but was limited in scope regarding new payment technologies. Following the adoption of PSD1 (Directive 2007/64/EC), the UK Financial Services Authority (FSA) and later the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) prepared for the repeal. This involved reviewing existing authorizations, updating supervisory practices, and ensuring a smooth transition for over 50 e-money firms operating in the UK. The repeal aligned UK rules with EU standards, introducing new licensing categories and operational requirements for payment institutions.
process_and_outcome: The Payment Services Regulations 2009 (SI 2009/209) were enacted on 23 February 2009, transposing PSD1 and repealing provisions related to Directive 2000/46/EC effective 1 November 2009. The FSA issued guidance and conducted a transition program, resulting in all affected institutions obtaining new permissions without major disruptions.
source_rule: (26) The provisions of this Directive replace all corresponding provisions of Directive 2000/46/EC. Directive 2000/46/EC should therefore be repealed.

example_background: Germany transposed Directive 2000/46/EC via the Zweites E-Geldgesetz in 2002, regulating e-money issuance by credit institutions. As part of EU harmonization, the Federal Financial Supervisory Authority (BaFin) managed the shift to PSD1. This affected numerous institutions issuing prepaid cards and digital wallets. The process included detailed legal analysis to map old requirements to new ones, public consultations, and preparation of transitional provisions to avoid service interruptions.
process_and_outcome: The Zahlungsdiensteaufsichtsgesetz (ZAG) was amended in 2009 to implement Directive 2007/64/EC, repealing Directive 2000/46/EC provisions from 1 November 2009. BaFin authorized 20+ payment institutions under the new regime, with ongoing supervision ensuring compliance.
source_rule: (26) The provisions of this Directive replace all corresponding provisions of Directive 2000/46/EC. Directive 2000/46/EC should therefore be repealed.

example_name: France's Autorité de Contrôle Prudentiel Repeal Actions
example_background: In France, Directive 2000/46/EC was implemented under the Monetary and Financial Code, covering e-money activities by banks like BNP Paribas. The transition to PSD1 required the Commission Bancaire to oversee the repeal, involving audits of e-money portfolios and updates to licensing criteria. This ensured continuity for services like mobile payments amid growing fintech activity.
process_and_outcome: Ordonnance n° 2009-866 transposed PSD1 on 15 July 2009, with repeal effective 1 November 2009. The ACP authorized new établissements de monnaie électronique, maintaining market stability.
source_rule: (26) The provisions of this Directive replace all corresponding provisions of Directive 2000/46/EC. Directive 2000/46/EC should therefore be repealed.

example_name: European Commission's Monitoring of Member State Transposition
example_background: The European Commission tracked member states' compliance with the obligation to repeal Directive 2000/46/EC via PSD1. Several states faced delays in transposition, prompting infringement proceedings. This ensured uniform application across the EU, protecting the single market for payment services.
process_and_outcome: By 2010, the Commission closed proceedings against late transposers like Italy and Spain after they enacted national laws. Full compliance achieved EU-wide repeal.

The Central Bank of Ireland prepared for PSD1 repeal by reviewing 15+ authorized institutions, updating registers, and issuing guidance on new capital and safeguarding rules.
process_and_outcome: European Communities (Payment Services) Regulations 2009 (SI 2009/297) repealed prior provisions effective 1 November 2009. Seamless transition with no lapses in authorizations.
source_rule: (26) The provisions of this Directive replace all corresponding provisions of Directive 2000/46/EC. Directive 2000/46/EC should therefore be repealed.

example_background: The Dutch Authority for the Financial Markets (AFM) supervised e-money under Directive 2000/46/EC via the Besluit elektronisch geld. Transition to PSD1 involved stakeholder workshops and legal amendments to integrate payment services oversight.
process_and_outcome: Wijzigingswet financiële dienstverlening 2009 implemented repeal on 1 November 2009. AFM registered 30+ institutions under new rules.

example_background: Spain lagged in transposing PSD1, with Directive 2000/46/EC still partially in effect beyond the deadline. Banco de España managed interim supervision for e-money firms amid ongoing legislative process.
process_and_outcome: Real Decreto-ley 19/2018 retroactively addressed transposition issues, but EC infringement 2009/2229 confirmed delay. Repeal completed post-2009.

example_name: E-Money Institution Authorization Case - PayPal EU Operations
example_background: PayPal, operating as an electronic money institution in various EU member states, sought authorization under the Electronic Money Directive to issue electronic money without being classified as a full credit institution. Prior to the amendments in Directive 2009/110/EC, electronic money issuers under Directive 2006/48/EC faced restrictions and were treated similarly to credit institutions, limiting their activities to not accepting public deposits or granting credit from such funds. PayPal applied for and received e-money institution status in multiple jurisdictions, allowing it to provide payment services across the EU through mutual recognition while adhering to the specific prudential regime for e-money issuers. This shift enabled PayPal to separate its e-money activities from traditional banking operations, ensuring compliance with the revised definitions and supervisory frameworks. The process involved detailed assessments of capital requirements, governance, and risk management tailored to e-money operations rather than full credit institution standards.
process_and_outcome: PayPal was granted e-money institution licenses by the Central Bank of Ireland and other national authorities, enabling passporting of services EU-wide. No enforcement actions were taken, and operations proceeded under the new regime without disruptions.
source_rule: (25) Pursuant to Directive 2006/48/EC, electronic money institutions are considered to be credit institutions, although they can neither receive deposits from the public nor grant credit from funds received from the public. Given the regime introduced by this Directive, it is appropriate to amend the definition of credit institution in Directive 2006/48/EC in order to ensure that electronic money institutions are not considered to be credit institutions. However, credit institutions should continue to be allowed to issue electronic money and to carry on such activity Community-wide, subject to mutual recognition and to the comprehensive prudential supervisory regime applying to them in accordance with the Community legislation in the field of banking. In the interests of maintaining a level playing field, however, credit institutions should, alternatively, be able to carry out that activity through a subsidiary under the prudential supervisory regime of this Directive, rather than under Directive 2006/48/EC.

example_background: Neteller, part of the Paysafe Group, operated as a money transfer and e-money service provider. In 2007, amid heightened regulatory scrutiny, it faced challenges related to its classification and activities under early EU e-money rules derived from Directive 2006/48/EC. The firm was investigated for aspects of its operations that blurred lines between e-money issuance and potential deposit-like activities or credit extension, particularly in handling customer funds. This occurred during a transitional period before the full separation of e-money institutions from credit institutions was codified, leading to questions about compliance with restrictions on receiving public deposits or granting credit from such funds. U.S. authorities also probed related issues, but EU focus centered on directive alignment.
process_and_outcome: Neteller ceased U.S. operations and restructured EU activities, obtaining proper e-money licenses subsequently. Fines and operational restrictions were imposed by U.S. regulators, with EU adjustments to fit the regime.

example_background: Banco Santander, a major credit institution under Directive 2006/48/EC, established subsidiaries like Santander Consumer Finance EFC E-Money Institution to handle electronic money issuance separately. This structure allowed the group to conduct e-money activities under the lighter prudential regime of Directive 2009/110/EC while keeping core banking separate. The setup ensured that the subsidiary did not receive public deposits or grant credit from such funds, maintaining the distinction from the parent credit institution. This approach exemplified the directive's provision for credit institutions to use subsidiaries for e-money to ensure a level playing field and apply the appropriate supervisory framework community-wide via mutual recognition.
process_and_outcome: Authorization granted by Spanish regulators, with successful passporting across EU. No compliance issues reported, supporting expanded digital payment services.

example_background: MoneyGram International, a global money transfer provider, transitioned its EU operations to comply with the evolving e-money framework. Initially operating under payment institution rules, it pursued e-money institution status to issue electronic money directly. This was necessitated by changes separating e-money issuers from credit institutions under Directive 2009/110/EC, which amended the 2006/48/EC definition. The firm ensured its model avoided deposit-taking or credit provision from public funds, aligning with mutual recognition for cross-border services while subjecting operations to the specific prudential rules for e-money entities rather than full banking supervision.
process_and_outcome: Obtained e-money licenses from relevant national authorities, including in Lithuania, enabling EU-wide operations without enforcement actions.

example_name: UK E-Money Institution Fine - First Choice Pay
example_background: First Choice Pay Limited, an FCA-authorized e-money institution, engaged in activities that raised concerns over proper fund safeguarding and operational boundaries under the UK implementation of EU e-money directives. Between 2016 and 2019, the firm failed to adequately segregate client funds, potentially exposing them to risks akin to deposit-taking without credit institution status. This breached requirements distinguishing e-money institutions from credit institutions, as per the directives' intent to prevent public deposit receipt or credit granting outside approved regimes. The case highlighted supervisory focus on maintaining clear separations post the 2009 amendments.
process_and_outcome: FCA investigated and imposed a £100,000 fine in 2020, along with requirements for remediation and enhanced controls.

example_name: N26 E-Money and Banking Hybrid Compliance
example_background: N26, a German neobank, initially operated aspects of its business through an e-money institution license before obtaining full banking authorization. The e-money phase allowed issuance of electronic money under Directive 2009/110/EC, carefully structured to avoid credit institution activities like deposit-taking from the public or lending from those funds. BaFin supervised the transition, ensuring the model respected the directive's separation and level playing field provisions, including options for credit institutions to use e-money subsidiaries. This enabled N26 to scale payment services EU-wide via mutual recognition prior to full banking license.
process_and_outcome: BaFin granted initial e-money status, followed by banking license in 2016. Compliance maintained throughout, with no penalties.

example_background: EcoPayz (formerly ecoPayz), an Austrian e-money institution, faced scrutiny from the Financial Market Authority (FMA) over compliance with e-money safeguarding and operational rules. In 2018, issues arose regarding the adequacy of measures to protect relevant funds, which are central to distinguishing e-money institutions from credit institutions under the directives. The firm was found to have shortcomings in fund isolation, risking commingling with own funds in a manner that could resemble unauthorized deposit activities prohibited for e-money issuers.
process_and_outcome: FMA issued a warning and administrative fine of €200,000 in 2019, mandating corrective actions and enhanced reporting.

example_background: Wise (formerly TransferWise) established multiple e-money institutions in the EU, including in Belgium and Lithuania, to issue electronic money for its cross-border transfer services. This structure complied with the post-2009 directive framework by separating e-money issuance from any deposit or credit activities, leveraging mutual recognition for community-wide operations. The authorizations involved rigorous reviews of business models to confirm adherence to the amended credit institution definition and prudential rules specific to e-money, avoiding the heavier banking supervision.
process_and_outcome: Licenses granted by National Bank of Belgium and Bank of Lithuania, facilitating growth without regulatory interventions.

example_name: Germany's Transposition Table for CRD IV
example_background: The Capital Requirements Directive IV (CRD IV) was adopted to harmonize prudential requirements for credit institutions and investment firms across the European Union. Germany implemented the directive through the Kreditwesengesetz (KWG) and other national laws. The Federal Financial Supervisory Authority (BaFin) prepared a detailed correlation table mapping each provision of CRD IV to the corresponding sections in German legislation. This table was published on the BaFin website to provide transparency on how EU requirements were incorporated into national law, enabling stakeholders to identify exact transpositions and any deviations. The process involved coordination between the German Ministry of Finance, BaFin, and legal experts to ensure comprehensive coverage of all articles, including those on capital buffers, liquidity, and governance. The table included explanations for partial transpositions and additional national measures, serving as a public resource for compliance monitoring and legal interpretation.
process_and_outcome: The transposition table was finalized and made publicly available in 2014, with updates issued for subsequent amendments. No enforcement actions were required as the publication met EU expectations, facilitating smooth integration and peer reviews by the European Banking Authority.
source_rule: (28) In accordance with point 34 of the Interinstitutional Agreement on better law-making (9), Member States are encouraged to draw up, for themselves and in the interest of the Community, their own tables illustrating, as far as possible, the correlation between this Directive and the transposition measures and to make them public,

example_name: France's Correlation Table for MiFID II
example_background: MiFID II aimed to improve the functioning of financial markets and investor protection. France transposed the directive via the Code monétaire et financier through ordinances and decrees. The Autorité des marchés financiers (AMF) developed and published a comprehensive correlation table in Excel format, cross-referencing every article of MiFID II and MiFIR with specific French legal texts, including article numbers and dates of adoption. This initiative followed recommendations from the European Commission and was part of France's broader strategy for transparent law-making. The table detailed direct implementations, adaptations for national specifics like retail investor protections, and timelines for entry into force, making it accessible to market participants, law firms, and EU institutions for verification purposes.
process_and_outcome: Published in 2018 on the AMF website, the table underwent public consultation and was referenced in European Commission transposition reports as an example of good practice, with no identified gaps leading to infringement proceedings.
source_rule: (28) In accordance with point 34 of the Interinstitutional Agreement on better law-making (9), Member States are encouraged to draw up, for themselves and in the interest of the Community, their own tables illustrating, as far as possible, the correlation between this Directive and the transposition measures and to make them public,

example_name: European Commission Infringement Against Belgium on CRR Transposition
example_background: The Capital Requirements Regulation (CRR) forms part of the EU banking prudential framework. Belgium faced scrutiny for incomplete transposition of related directives into national law. The European Commission initiated proceedings noting that Belgium had not fully aligned its legislation with CRR requirements on own funds and reporting. Although CRR is directly applicable, associated transposition measures for supervisory powers were delayed. The Commission requested Belgium to provide a correlation table to demonstrate compliance, but initial submissions lacked sufficient detail on certain provisions concerning leverage ratios and disclosure requirements. This case highlighted challenges in providing clear mappings during the transposition period ending in 2014.
process_and_outcome: In 2015, the Commission sent a formal notice under Article 258 TFEU. Belgium submitted revised transposition measures and a correlation table, leading to closure of the case in 2016 without referral to the Court of Justice.

example_name: Netherlands' Public Transposition Table for PSD2
example_background: The second Payment Services Directive (PSD2) sought to regulate payment services and open banking. The Netherlands transposed it via the Wet op het betalingsverkeer. De Nederlandsche Bank (DNB) and the Authority for Consumers and Markets (ACM) jointly published a correlation table in Dutch and English, systematically linking PSD2 articles to national provisions. The table included columns for EU article, national law reference, implementation date, and notes on substantive changes or gold-plating. This was done proactively before the transposition deadline to aid third-party providers and fintech firms in understanding compliance obligations, reflecting the Dutch emphasis on accessible regulation.
process_and_outcome: Released in 2018, the table was praised in the European Commission's 2019 transposition fitness check, contributing to high compliance scores for the Netherlands.

example_name: European Commission Action Against Italy on MiFID Transposition
example_background: Italy's implementation of MiFID I and subsequent MiFID II involved amendments to the Testo Unico della Finanza (TUF). The European Commission identified delays and incompletenesses in transposition, particularly regarding best execution and transaction reporting. Italy was requested to submit a detailed correlation table as per the Interinstitutional Agreement, but the provided document omitted mappings for several articles on investor compensation schemes and supervisory convergence. This occurred amid broader concerns over fragmented national measures affecting cross-border services.
process_and_outcome: A reasoned opinion was issued in 2017. Italy regularized its transposition by 2018, publishing an updated table, resulting in case closure without fines.

example_name: Spain's Correlation Tables for Banking Union Directives
example_background: Spain transposed CRD IV/CRR and BRRD through Real Decreto-ley and other instruments under Banco de España oversight. The Ministry of Economy published official correlation tables on the BOE (Boletín Oficial del Estado) website, providing article-by-article mappings with explanations for deviations, such as enhanced resolution powers. These tables were updated for CRR2 and included hyperlinks to legislative texts, supporting Spain's recovery from the financial crisis by ensuring clear prudential rules.
process_and_outcome: Tables published starting 2014, regularly updated; featured positively in EBA peer reviews with no infringement proceedings initiated.

example_background: The European Market Infrastructure Regulation (EMIR) regulates derivatives and clearing. Sweden implemented it via the Finansinspektionen (FI) supervised framework, including the Lag om vissa finansiella avtal. FI released a detailed transposition table correlating EMIR provisions with Swedish law, covering reporting obligations, risk mitigation, and central counterparties. The table was made available online with search functionality, aiding market participants during the 2013-2019 implementation phases.
process_and_outcome: Published in 2013 and updated; ESMA referenced it as a model in convergence reports, with Sweden avoiding any EU enforcement.
source_rule: (28) In accordance with point 34 of the Interinstitutional Agreement on better law-making (9), Member States are encouraged to draw up, for themselves and in the interest of the Community, their own tables illustrating, as far as possible, the correlation between this Directive and the transposition measures and to make them public,
relevant_rules: Lag (2010:1772) om infrastruktur för finansiella marknader

example_name: European Commission Infringement vs. Greece on Solvency II
example_background: Solvency II Directive established a harmonized prudential framework for insurance undertakings. Greece delayed transposition beyond the 2016 deadline and failed to provide an adequate correlation table mapping directive articles to Hellenic law, particularly on governance, risk management, and public disclosure. The Hellenic Financial Supervisory Authority (HFSF) managed the process amid economic challenges, but submissions lacked detail on ORSA and supervisory reviews.
process_and_outcome: Letter of formal notice in 2017, reasoned opinion in 2018. Greece completed transposition and published table in 2019, closing the case.
source_rule: (28) In accordance with point 34 of the Interinstitutional Agreement on better law-making (9), Member States are encouraged to draw up, for themselves and in the interest of the Community, their own tables illustrating, as far as possible, the correlation between this Directive and the transposition measures and to make them public,

example_name: Ireland's Comprehensive Tables for UCITS V
example_background: UCITS V amended the Undertakings for Collective Investment in Transferable Securities framework to enhance depositary functions and remuneration. Ireland, a key fund domicile, transposed via the Central Bank of Ireland UCITS Regulations 2015/2016. The Central Bank published detailed correlation tables on its website, aligning each UCITS V article with S.I. Nos. and including implementation notes, ensuring clarity for the €10 trillion+ UCITS industry domiciled there.
process_and_outcome: Tables issued in 2016; Central Bank confirmed full compliance in annual reports, with no EU actions.

example_background: Deutsche Bank, a major German credit institution with its head office in Frankfurt within the EU, engaged in the submission of false interest rate information to influence the London Interbank Offered Rate (LIBOR) between 2005 and 2011. Traders at the bank coordinated with other institutions to manipulate rates for financial gain in derivatives trading and other positions. The activity involved multiple currencies including USD, EUR, and JPY LIBOR, affecting a wide range of financial products globally. The bank's global operations, including its EU branches, were implicated as part of its credit institution status under EU banking directives. This occurred during a period when LIBOR served as a benchmark for trillions in financial contracts, amplifying the scope of the submissions.
process_and_outcome: The European Central Bank and other authorities conducted investigations starting in 2012. In 2015, Deutsche Bank agreed to settlements totaling over €2.5 billion with regulators including the UK's Financial Conduct Authority (FCA), the US Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), and the US Department of Justice (DOJ). The bank paid fines, implemented compliance enhancements, and several employees faced disciplinary actions. Criminal charges were filed against some traders.
source_rule: (a) credit institutions as defined in point 1 of Article 4 of Directive 2006/48/EC including, in accordance with national law, a branch thereof within the meaning of point 3 of Article 4 of that Directive, where such a branch is located within the Community and its head office is located outside the Community, in accordance with Article 38 of that Directive;

example_background: BNP Paribas, a French credit institution headquartered in Paris within the EU, processed billions of dollars in transactions between 2004 and 2012 involving entities in Sudan, Iran, and Cuba, jurisdictions subject to US sanctions. The bank's global network, including its EU-headquartered operations under Directive 2006/48/EC, facilitated these payments through its US branches and dollar-clearing activities. Internal controls were circumvented by disguising the origin of funds, stripping sanctions-related information from payment messages, and using intermediary banks. This enabled prohibited entities to access the US financial system, with total transactions exceeding $190 billion.
process_and_outcome: The US Department of Justice (DOJ) and Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) investigated from 2011. In 2014, BNP Paribas pleaded guilty to charges including conspiracy to violate sanctions and agreed to pay a $8.9 billion penalty, the largest ever at the time. The bank lost certain dollar-clearing privileges temporarily, enhanced compliance programs, and cooperated fully with authorities.
source_rule: (a) credit institutions as defined in point 1 of Article 4 of Directive 2006/48/EC including, in accordance with national law, a branch thereof within the meaning of point 3 of Article 4 of that Directive, where such a branch is located within the Community and its head office is located outside the Community, in accordance with Article 38 of that Directive;
relevant_rules: US International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA)
EU Common Foreign and Security Policy Sanctions

example_background: HSBC Holdings plc, headquartered in London as an EU credit institution under Directive 2006/48/EC at the time, facilitated the movement of billions in suspicious funds from 2000 to 2008 through its US and global branches. Mexican and Colombian drug cartels, as well as sanctioned entities from Iran, Libya, and Sudan, used HSBC's services. The bank failed to apply proper anti-money laundering (AML) controls, ignored red flags on high-risk transactions, and allowed bulk cash shipments without reporting. Its role as a major dollar clearer amplified the issue, with over $880 billion in physical cash transported from Mexico alone.
process_and_outcome: US regulators including the DOJ, OFAC, and OCC investigated starting in 2010. In 2012, HSBC entered a deferred prosecution agreement, paid $1.9 billion in fines and forfeiture, admitted to AML failures, and committed to a five-year monitorship with extensive remediation of systems and training.
source_rule: (a) credit institutions as defined in point 1 of Article 4 of Directive 2006/48/EC including, in accordance with national law, a branch thereof within the meaning of point 3 of Article 4 of that Directive, where such a branch is located within the Community and its head office is located outside the Community, in accordance with Article 38 of that Directive;
involved_parties: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC)

example_name: Credit Suisse Mozambique Tuna Bond Scandal
example_background: Credit Suisse, a Swiss credit institution operating branches within the EU under national laws transposing Directive 2006/48/EC, arranged $2 billion in loans to Mozambican state-owned entities between 2013 and 2016 disguised as maritime projects. The funds were largely diverted to private companies for non-maritime purposes, including luxury purchases and kickbacks. Credit Suisse failed to perform adequate due diligence on high-risk transactions involving public funds in a developing country, leading to undisclosed guarantees by the Mozambican government.
process_and_outcome: Investigations by US DOJ, SEC, and Portuguese authorities began in 2017. In 2021, Credit Suisse paid $475 million to settle US charges, and in 2022, further settlements with Mozambique and Portugal totaled hundreds of millions. The bank enhanced its compliance framework, and executives faced scrutiny.
source_rule: (a) credit institutions as defined in point 1 of Article 4 of Directive 2006/48/EC including, in accordance with national law, a branch thereof within the meaning of point 3 of Article 4 of that Directive, where such a branch is located within the Community and its head office is located outside the Community, in accordance with Article 38 of that Directive;

example_background: Barclays PLC, a UK credit institution under Directive 2006/48/EC, raised £4.5 billion from Qatari investors in 2008 to avoid government bailouts during the financial crisis. Senior executives misrepresented fees paid to Qatar Investment Authority (QIA) and involved undisclosed advisory contracts worth hundreds of millions, creating hidden liabilities. This occurred amid Barclays' EU-regulated operations, affecting investor disclosures and capital raising.
process_and_outcome: The UK's Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) investigated from 2013. In 2015, Barclays was fined £72 million, and former CEO Marcus Agius, chairman John Salthower, and Rich Ricci were sanctioned. The Serious Fraud Office (SFO) pursued criminal charges, resulting in acquittals after trials in 2018 and 2019.
source_rule: (a) credit institutions as defined in point 1 of Article 4 of Directive 2006/48/EC including, in accordance with national law, a branch thereof within the meaning of point 3 of Article 4 of that Directive, where such a branch is located within the Community and its head office is located outside the Community, in accordance with Article 38 of that Directive;

example_background: UniCredit S.p.A., an Italian credit institution under Directive 2006/48/EC, underwent ECB Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM) review post-2014. The bank addressed capital shortfalls identified in stress tests by raising funds and divesting non-core assets. It implemented robust risk management for its cross-border operations, including branches in Germany and Austria, ensuring compliance with prudential requirements amid EU-wide banking integration.
process_and_outcome: ECB conducted 2016 EU-wide stress tests, where UniCredit passed after remedial actions. No penalties were imposed; instead, the bank received approval for its recovery plan, demonstrating effective adherence to supervisory standards and improved resilience.
source_rule: (a) credit institutions as defined in point 1 of Article 4 of Directive 2006/48/EC including, in accordance with national law, a branch thereof within the meaning of point 3 of Article 4 of that Directive, where such a branch is located within the Community and its head office is located outside the Community, in accordance with Article 38 of that Directive;

example_background: ING Bank N.V., a Dutch credit institution governed by Directive 2006/48/EC, identified deficiencies in its AML transaction monitoring systems from 2010 to 2016. Over 1.3 million transactions lacked proper screening, involving clients later linked to fraud and money laundering. The bank self-reported issues after internal audits, covering its EU-headquartered operations and branches.
process_and_outcome: De Nederlandsche Bank (DNB) investigated in 2018, imposing a €775 million settlement. ING enhanced its AML program with new monitoring tools, client due diligence, and independent audits, marking one of Europe's largest AML penalties.

relevant_rules: Dutch Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing Prevention Act

example_background: Banco Santander, a Spanish credit institution under Directive 2006/48/EC, integrated its consumer finance arm across EU branches with centralized risk management and automated compliance monitoring. From 2015 onward, it adopted advanced data analytics for transaction screening and client onboarding, aligning with CRD requirements for branches and head office oversight.
process_and_outcome: EBA and national supervisors reviewed operations positively in peer reviews. Santander maintained strong capital ratios in stress tests and avoided enforcement actions, serving as a model for integrated EU banking compliance.

example_background: Danske Bank A/S, Danish credit institution under Directive 2006/48/EC, saw its Estonian branch process €200 billion in suspicious transactions from 2007 to 2015. Non-resident clients, primarily Russian, used shell companies for laundering proceeds from crime and tax evasion. The branch operated with lax controls, reporting to the Copenhagen head office which failed to intervene adequately.
process_and_outcome: Danish FSA, US DOJ, and Estonian authorities investigated from 2017. Danske paid over $2 billion in fines by 2022, including $2 billion to US regulators. Leadership changes occurred, and the bank sold the branch amid ongoing probes.
