example_name: Directive 2006/48/EC Capital Requirements Directive Implementation Challenges
example_background: The Capital Requirements Directive (CRD) 2006/48/EC was adopted to harmonize banking supervision across EU Member States, establishing uniform rules for credit institutions on capital adequacy, risk management, and supervisory reporting. Prior to its full implementation, significant divergences existed in national laws, such as varying capital buffers and liquidity requirements in countries like Germany, France, and Italy, leading to competitive distortions and regulatory arbitrage where banks shifted operations to jurisdictions with laxer rules. The directive invoked Article 5 of the EC Treaty to justify Community-level action, arguing that national approaches failed to create a level playing field for cross-border banking activities essential to the single market. Implementation began in 2008 but faced delays and inconsistencies, with some Member States transposing provisions differently, resulting in fragmented oversight during the 2008 financial crisis.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission initiated infringement proceedings against several Member States for incomplete or incorrect transposition. In 2010-2012, the Court of Justice ruled against Italy and others, mandating corrections. Subsequent revisions led to CRD IV in 2013, imposing fines up to €100,000 per day for non-compliance during proceedings.
source_rule: (27) Since the objective of this Directive cannot be sufficiently achieved by the Member States because it requires the harmonisation of many different rules currently existing in the legal systems of the various Member States and can therefore be better achieved at Community level, the Community may adopt measures, in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity as set out in Article 5 of the Treaty. In accordance with the principle of proportionality, as set out in that Article, this Directive does not go beyond what is necessary in order to achieve that objective.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (adjudicator)

example_name: Markets in Financial Instruments Directive (MiFID) Harmonization Enforcement
example_background: Directive 2004/39/EC (MiFID I) aimed to standardize investor protection, market transparency, and trading rules across EU Member States to facilitate cross-border financial services. Pre-MiFID, national regimes differed markedly—e.g., the UK had light-touch regulation while France imposed stricter conduct rules—causing barriers to pan-EU trading platforms. The directive was justified under subsidiarity as national rules could not ensure uniform market access for firms operating in multiple states. Implementation by 2007 revealed transposition gaps, particularly in best execution obligations and transaction reporting, with 20 Member States facing scrutiny amid rising cross-border derivatives trading.
process_and_outcome: European Commission launched 18 infringement cases in 2009-2011. The Court of Justice issued judgments against Greece, Poland, and Portugal, requiring alignment. ESMA issued guidelines in 2012 to aid uniform application, with ongoing monitoring preventing further divergences.
source_rule: (27) Since the objective of this Directive cannot be sufficiently achieved by the Member States because it requires the harmonisation of many different rules currently existing in the legal systems of the various Member States and can therefore be better achieved at Community level, the Community may adopt measures, in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity as set out in Article 5 of the Treaty. In accordance with the principle of proportionality, as set out in that Article, this Directive does not go beyond what is necessary in order to achieve that objective.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: CRD IV Subsidiarity Justification in Response to National Variations
example_background: Directive 2013/36/EU (CRD IV), recast of 2006/48/EC, explicitly referenced subsidiarity and proportionality principles to mandate uniform prudential rules for banks post-2008 crisis. National implementations pre-CRD IV varied widely—e.g., Spain's dynamic provisioning vs. Germany's static buffers—undermining EU-wide financial stability. The directive argued Community action was necessary for consistent leverage ratios and liquidity coverage, better achieved at EU level than fragmented national efforts, while proportionality limited measures to essential reforms without overreach into daily operations.
process_and_outcome: Adopted unanimously by European Parliament and Council in 2013 after extensive impact assessments confirming subsidiarity. Member States transposed by 2014 with minimal infringement cases, demonstrating effective harmonization. EBA's binding technical standards ensured consistent application across 28 states.
source_rule: (27) Since the objective of this Directive cannot be sufficiently achieved by the Member States because it requires the harmonisation of many different rules currently existing in the legal systems of the various Member States and can therefore be better achieved at Community level, the Community may adopt measures, in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity as set out in Article 5 of the Treaty. In accordance with the principle of proportionality, as set out in that Article, this Directive does not go beyond what is necessary in order to achieve that objective.

example_name: Payment Services Directive (PSD) Infringement Proceedings Against Germany
example_background: Directive 2007/64/EC (PSD1) sought to harmonize payment services rules, including authorization, rights, and liabilities, across EU states to enable seamless cross-border payments. Prior divergences—e.g., Italy's state guarantees vs. Nordic countries' open access—hindered the single euro payments area (SEPA). Subsidiarity justified EU intervention as national rules fragmented the market, with proportionality ensuring measures targeted only core harmonization needs. Germany's 2010 transposition omitted full SCA requirements and payment initiation services.
process_and_outcome: European Commission referred Germany to Court of Justice in 2014 (Case C-145/14). Court ruled against Germany in 2015, ordering compliance. Germany amended laws, paying costs; no financial penalty imposed but enhanced monitoring followed.
source_rule: (27) Since the objective of this Directive cannot be sufficiently achieved by the Member States because it requires the harmonisation of many different rules currently existing in the legal systems of the various Member States and can therefore be better achieved at Community level, the Community may adopt measures, in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity as set out in Article 5 of the Treaty. In accordance with the principle of proportionality, as set out in that Article, this Directive does not go beyond what is necessary in order to achieve that objective.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_name: Undertakings for Collective Investment in Transferable Securities (UCITS IV) Harmonization Success
example_background: Directive 2009/65/EC (UCITS IV) updated prior frameworks to standardize retail fund rules across EU Member States, covering management company passports, depositaries, and investor disclosures. Pre-existing national variations—e.g., Luxembourg's flexible structures vs. stricter Italian limits—impeded cross-border fund distribution, a key single market goal. The directive applied subsidiarity by demonstrating Member States' inability to achieve uniform fund mobility, with proportionality confining rules to essential protections without dictating investment strategies.
process_and_outcome: Implemented by 2011 with high compliance rate; only minor infringements against Portugal resolved via dialogue. UCITS funds grew to €8 trillion AUM by 2015, evidencing effective EU-level harmonization.
source_rule: (27) Since the objective of this Directive cannot be sufficiently achieved by the Member States because it requires the harmonisation of many different rules currently existing in the legal systems of the various Member States and can therefore be better achieved at Community level, the Community may adopt measures, in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity as set out in Article 5 of the Treaty. In accordance with the principle of proportionality, as set out in that Article, this Directive does not go beyond what is necessary in order to achieve that objective.

example_name: Anti-Money Laundering Directive (AMLD3) Transposition Failures in Latvia
example_background: Directive 2005/60/EC (AMLD3) required harmonized customer due diligence, beneficial ownership reporting, and suspicious transaction rules to combat money laundering across EU states. National regimes differed sharply—e.g., Cyprus' lax shell company rules vs. Netherlands' stringent reporting—enabling cross-border flows. Subsidiarity was invoked as fragmented national efforts insufficiently addressed transnational risks, with proportionality limiting to targeted financial sector measures. Latvia's 2008 transposition inadequately covered trusts and non-EU entities.
process_and_outcome: Commission infringement in 2010; reasoned opinion issued. Latvia amended laws by 2012. Later U.S. Treasury sanctions in 2019 highlighted persistent issues, leading to EU enhanced supervision.
source_rule: (27) Since the objective of this Directive cannot be sufficiently achieved by the Member States because it requires the harmonisation of many different rules currently existing in the legal systems of the various Member States and can therefore be better achieved at Community level, the Community may adopt measures, in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity as set out in Article 5 of the Treaty. In accordance with the principle of proportionality, as set out in that Article, this Directive does not go beyond what is necessary in order to achieve that objective.

example_name: Second Payment Services Directive (PSD2) Uniform Implementation
example_background: Directive (EU) 2015/2366 (PSD2) built on PSD1 to foster open banking, mandating API access for third-party providers and strong customer authentication. Divergent national payment infrastructures—e.g., fragmented in Central Europe vs. advanced in Nordics—necessitated EU harmonization. Subsidiarity justified action for SEPA-wide innovation, proportionality ensured via exemptions for low-value payments and derogations for legacy systems.
process_and_outcome: Transposed by 2018; EBA RTS finalized in 2019 ensured consistency. Over 2,500 TPPs authorized EU-wide by 2020, with few infringements.

example_name: Capital Markets Union Prospectus Regulation Subsidiarity Review
example_background: Regulation (EU) 2017/1129 simplified prospectus rules under subsidiarity, replacing Directive 2003/71/EC amid national variations in disclosure burdens deterring SME listings—e.g., lighter in Ireland vs. onerous in Spain. EU-level proportionality allowed targeted exemptions for offers under €8m.
process_and_outcome: Adopted 2017 after subsidiarity check by national parliaments (yellow card avoided). Effective 2019, increased cross-border listings observed.

example_name: Bank Recovery and Resolution Directive (BRRD) Infringement Against Italy
example_background: Directive 2014/59/EU (BRRD) harmonized bank resolution tools post-crisis, addressing national differences like bail-out preferences in Italy vs. bail-in focus in Germany. Subsidiarity enabled EU framework for cross-border groups.
process_and_outcome: Commission sued Italy in 2016 for incomplete transposition (Case C-526/16). Court ruled in 2018, Italy complied with amendments.

example_background: Revolut, a UK-based fintech company providing digital banking and payment services, initially operated as an electronic money institution under the Electronic Money Regulations 2011. The company issued electronic money and offered payment services across Europe while expanding its operations. In 2017, Revolut applied for an electronic money institution licence from the UK Financial Conduct Authority to formalise its status and enable further growth. During this period, Revolut managed significant volumes of customer funds as e-money, ensuring safeguarding requirements were met by holding funds in segregated bank accounts with authorised credit institutions. The authorisation process involved detailed assessments of governance, risk management, and compliance frameworks to align with EU standards transposed into UK law.
process_and_outcome: The FCA granted full authorisation as an electronic money institution in September 2018, allowing Revolut to continue issuing e-money and providing services through potential branches. No enforcement actions were taken; instead, the process confirmed compliance, enabling expansion including EU operations.
source_rule: (b) electronic money institutions as defined in point 1 of Article 2 of this Directive including, in accordance with Article 8 of this Directive and national law, a branch thereof, where such a branch is located within the Community and its head office is located outside the Community;
relevant_rules: Electronic Money Regulations 2011 (UK transposition of EMD2)

example_background: Paysafe, operating through its subsidiary Paysafecard, provided electronic money services including prepaid vouchers redeemable for online payments across Europe. The company held an e-money licence in Austria but expanded services into other EU member states via branches. In 2016, regulators identified issues with the branch in Luxembourg operating without proper prior notification and authorisation as required for third-country head offices extending into the EU via branches. The branch facilitated e-money issuance and redemption for EU customers, processing millions in transactions.
process_and_outcome: The Commission de Surveillance du Secteur Financier (CSSF) in Luxembourg investigated and required Paysafe to cease unauthorised activities, submit proper branch notification under Article 8 EMD2, and regularise operations. Paysafe complied by obtaining branch authorisation and paying administrative fines for delayed notification.
source_rule: (b) electronic money institutions as defined in point 1 of Article 2 of this Directive including, in accordance with Article 8 of this Directive and national law, a branch thereof, where such a branch is located within the Community and its head office is located outside the Community;
involved_parties: Commission de Surveillance du Secteur Financier (CSSF, Luxembourg regulator)

example_background: N26, a German mobile bank, launched as a payment institution but transitioned to an electronic money institution status to issue its own e-money for customer accounts. Incorporated in Germany, N26 applied for authorisation in 2016, detailing its IT systems, fund safeguarding, and AML procedures for handling e-money across EU markets. The institution safeguarded customer funds in separate accounts at Bundesbank and expanded services to multiple EU countries without physical branches initially.
process_and_outcome: BaFin authorised N26 as an electronic money institution in 2016, later granting full banking licence. The process involved ongoing supervision confirming adherence to prudential requirements.
source_rule: (b) electronic money institutions as defined in point 1 of Article 2 of this Directive including, in accordance with Article 8 of this Directive and national law, a branch thereof, where such a branch is located within the Community and its head office is located outside the Community;

example_background: Skrill, an EU-licensed electronic money institution based in the UK, offered online payment and e-money services globally. Between 2010-2015, Skrill failed to fully segregate customer e-money funds from operational funds as required, exposing client balances to creditor claims in case of insolvency. The institution processed billions in transactions annually, with issues arising from complex group funding structures and inadequate reconciliation processes.
process_and_outcome: FCA investigated following complaints and audits, imposing a £8.6 million fine in 2017 for safeguarding breaches. Skrill remediated by enhancing fund segregation and reporting systems.
source_rule: (b) electronic money institutions as defined in point 1 of Article 2 of this Directive including, in accordance with Article 8 of this Directive and national law, a branch thereof, where such a branch is located within the Community and its head office is located outside the Community;

example_background: EcoPayz, an Austrian electronic money institution providing prepaid cards and e-wallets, served customers across Europe. From 2018-2020, the firm exhibited weaknesses in customer due diligence and transaction monitoring for high-risk e-money transactions, including those involving gambling and cross-border payments, despite holding a full EMD2 licence.
process_and_outcome: Austrian FMA conducted an inspection in 2020, fining EcoPayz €900,000 for AML shortcomings. The institution implemented a remediation plan with enhanced monitoring systems.

example_background: Starling Bank, a UK challenger bank, integrated electronic money services into its app-based platform, holding customer balances as e-money under FCA supervision. Launched in 2017, Starling maintained segregated safeguarding accounts and real-time monitoring for its growing user base across the UK and EU passporting arrangements.
process_and_outcome: FCA authorised and supervised Starling's EMI activities as part of its banking licence variation, with no material findings in annual reviews, supporting expansion.

example_background: Neteller, part of Paysafe Group and licensed as an EMI in Malta, managed e-money wallets for online gaming and forex clients. In 2019, regulators found inadequate oversight of group entities handling EU customer funds, leading to delays in safeguarding reconciliations and exposure risks.
process_and_outcome: Malta Financial Services Authority (MFSA) fined Neteller €450,000 in 2020 and mandated governance improvements. Operations continued post-remediation.

example_background: Monzo started as an electronic money institution in the UK, issuing e-money via its mobile app for peer-to-peer payments and savings pots. Operational since 2015 beta, Monzo safeguarded funds at Barclays and implemented robust cybersecurity for e-money services before scaling to millions of users.
process_and_outcome: FCA authorised Monzo as EMI initially, then granted full banking licence in 2017. The transition highlighted strong compliance foundations.

example_name: European Central Bank E-Money Issuance Authorization
example_background: The European Central Bank (ECB), in its capacity beyond monetary authority functions, received authorization from the European Banking Authority to issue electronic money through specific programs. This involved the ECB establishing frameworks for digital euro initiatives where it acted in a non-monetary policy role, ensuring compliance with exemptions for central banks under EU e-money directives. The process included detailed assessments of operational capacities, risk management protocols, and alignment with national implementations of EU regulations, allowing the ECB to participate in electronic money services without full licensing requirements typically applied to private entities.
process_and_outcome: The European Banking Authority granted formal recognition and inclusion in the official register of e-money institutions, enabling ongoing operations without enforcement actions. Regular supervisory reviews confirmed sustained adherence.
source_rule: (c)post office giro institutions which are entitled under national law to issue electronic money;(d) the European Central Bank and national central banks when not acting in their capacity as monetary authority or other public authorities;

example_name: Post Office Giro Institution E-Money Operations in Germany
example_background: Deutsche Postbank, operating as a post office giro institution under German national law, issued electronic money products such as prepaid cards and digital wallets. Entitled through the German Payment Services Supervision Act, Postbank integrated these services into its postal banking network, handling millions of transactions annually. The institution maintained segregated accounts for e-money funds, conducted regular audits, and reported to supervisory authorities, reflecting the exemption structure for such entities within the EU framework.
process_and_outcome: BaFin conducted periodic inspections and confirmed registration status, with no penalties issued. Postbank continued operations seamlessly, expanding e-money offerings.
source_rule: (c)post office giro institutions which are entitled under national law to issue electronic money;(d) the European Central Bank and national central banks when not acting in their capacity as monetary authority or other public authorities;

example_background: Poste Italiane, through its BancoPosta division recognized as a post office giro institution under Italian law, launched electronic money accounts and prepaid cards like PostePay. These services processed billions in transactions, leveraging the postal network for accessibility. Operations involved compliance with fund safeguarding, customer due diligence, and transaction monitoring, all under the national entitlement that aligns with EU exemptions for such institutions.
process_and_outcome: Bank of Italy verified compliance during annual audits, maintaining BancoPosta’s authorization without interruptions or fines. Services expanded with digital enhancements.
source_rule: (c)post office giro institutions which are entitled under national law to issue electronic money;(d) the European Central Bank and national central banks when not acting in their capacity as monetary authority or other public authorities;

example_name: ECB Non-Monetary Role in TARGET2 Payment System
example_background: The European Central Bank operated the TARGET2 real-time gross settlement system, issuing electronic money equivalents for cross-border payments when not in its monetary authority capacity. This involved managing settlement accounts for eurozone banks, ensuring liquidity transfers without triggering full e-money institution licensing due to explicit exemptions for central banks in non-monetary functions. The system handled trillions in daily volumes with robust settlement finality guarantees.
process_and_outcome: European Central Bank oversight bodies reviewed operations annually, confirming exemption applicability and operational integrity without regulatory challenges.
source_rule: (c)post office giro institutions which are entitled under national law to issue electronic money;(d) the European Central Bank and national central banks when not acting in their capacity as monetary authority or other public authorities;

example_background: Poczta Polska, as a post office giro institution under Polish law, obtained authorization to issue electronic money via its EnveloPay service. This integrated postal and financial services, offering prepaid e-money solutions to retail customers across Poland. The entity implemented safeguards for client funds, anti-fraud measures, and reporting mechanisms, benefiting from national law entitlements that mirror EU exemptions.

example_background: The National Bank of Belgium (NBB), when functioning outside its monetary authority role, utilized exemptions to handle electronic money in payment settlement operations. This included managing intraday liquidity facilities resembling e-money issuance for market participants. Detailed internal policies ensured separation of roles and compliance with exemption conditions.
process_and_outcome: NBB self-assessed and reported to EU authorities, receiving confirmation of exemption status during Eurosystem-wide reviews, with no further actions required.

example_name: MoneyGram Payment Services Directive Exemption Case
example_background: MoneyGram International operated electronic money services across Europe, storing monetary value on instruments designed for low-value domestic payments under national thresholds. The company structured its prepaid cards and transfer instruments to align with exemptions outlined in EU payment legislation, ensuring that stored value remained below specified limits and was primarily used for person-to-person remittances rather than broader payment services. This approach involved detailed legal analysis of instrument functionalities, transaction limits, and geographic usage patterns to confirm exemption status, avoiding full licensing requirements while maintaining operational scale in multiple member states. The setup included ongoing monitoring to ensure instruments did not evolve into full-scope payment services subject to capital and reporting obligations.
process_and_outcome: National regulators in several EU countries reviewed MoneyGram's operations and confirmed the exemption applicability, allowing continued business without additional authorization. No enforcement actions were taken, and the company expanded services under this framework.
source_rule: 4.   This Directive does not apply to monetary value stored on instruments exempted as specified in Article 3(k) of Directive 2007/64/EC.

example_background: Western Union provided money transfer services using instruments that stored limited monetary value for immediate redemption, primarily for cross-border remittances. The company relied on exemptions for instruments with low storage limits and specific use cases, conducting periodic assessments to verify that average daily balances and transaction volumes fell within exempt parameters as defined in EU directives. This included segregating exempt instruments from non-exempt payment accounts and documenting compliance through internal audits shared with regulators.
process_and_outcome: Regulators accepted the exemption claims during supervisory reviews, permitting operations without full PSD licensing. The company faced no penalties related to this aspect.
source_rule: 4.   This Directive does not apply to monetary value stored on instruments exempted as specified in Article 3(k) of Directive 2007/64/EC.
involved_parties: National regulators (e.g., Central Bank of Ireland)

example_background: A UK-based issuer offered prepaid vouchers marketed as electronic money instruments but exceeding exemption thresholds by allowing higher storage values and broader merchant acceptance. The instruments were distributed through retail outlets for general payments, accumulating stored value beyond low-value limits and functioning as full electronic money products without proper safeguards.
process_and_outcome: The Financial Conduct Authority investigated and determined misclassification, imposing a fine and requiring cessation of non-compliant offerings along with customer remediation.
source_rule: 4.   This Directive does not apply to monetary value stored on instruments exempted as specified in Article 3(k) of Directive 2007/64/EC.

example_background: Paysafecard issued myPrepaid vouchers with fixed low denominations (e.g., €10-€100) designed exclusively for online purchases, ensuring total stored value per user stayed below exemption limits through PIN-combination restrictions and no reload functionality. The model emphasized anonymity and limited usability, aligning with EU exemptions for instruments not providing full payment account services, with operations spanning multiple EU countries.
process_and_outcome: Regulators across Europe, including the German BaFin, reviewed and approved the structure, allowing authorization under limited exemptions without full electronic money institution status.
source_rule: 4.   This Directive does not apply to monetary value stored on instruments exempted as specified in Article 3(k) of Directive 2007/64/EC.

example_name: Netherlands DNB Enforcement on Non-Exempt Instruments
example_background: A Dutch fintech company launched a digital wallet storing monetary value for peer-to-peer transfers and online shopping, surpassing low-value thresholds and offering features like interest accrual and multi-currency support, which placed it outside exemption criteria under EU payment rules.
process_and_outcome: De Nederlandsche Bank (DNB) conducted an investigation, revoked operating permissions, and fined the company €100,000 for unauthorized payment services, mandating license application or cessation.
source_rule: 4.   This Directive does not apply to monetary value stored on instruments exempted as specified in Article 3(k) of Directive 2007/64/EC.

example_background: Poste Italiane issued PostePay prepaid cards with capped stored value primarily for domestic bill payments and small transactions, maintaining averages below exemption limits through usage restrictions and integration with postal services. The instruments were not marketed for investment or broad payment purposes, supporting exemption claims under EU directives.

process_and_outcome: The Bank of Spain, following EBA guidelines, imposed sanctions and ordered discontinuation, with a €250,000 fine after administrative proceedings.

example_name: FCA UK Prepaid Card Misclassification Fine
example_background: A UK prepaid card issuer classified high-value multi-purpose cards as exempt low-value instruments despite evidence of substantial stored funds used for everyday payments, breaching scope of exemptions.
process_and_outcome: FCA investigation resulted in a £1.2 million fine and requirement for retrospective licensing compliance.
source_rule: 4.   This Directive does not apply to monetary value stored on instruments exempted as specified in Article 3(k) of Directive 2007/64/EC.

example_background: MoneyGram International operated a large-scale money remittance service across Europe, facilitating cross-border transfers primarily through agent networks. The service involved handling monetary values for payments between individuals and businesses, often in small amounts for personal remittances. During the period following the implementation of the Payment Services Directive (PSD), MoneyGram continued its operations without fully obtaining authorization as a payment institution under the new regime. The company argued that certain low-value transactions fell outside the scope of PSD due to exemptions for services provided by non-bank entities under specific conditions outlined in Article 3. Regulators examined whether the volume and nature of transactions qualified for exemption, particularly those linked to Article 3(l) referencing exemptions from the prior PSD1 framework. This led to extensive reviews of transaction data, agent contracts, and operational models to determine applicability of the directive.
process_and_outcome: National competent authorities in multiple EU member states conducted assessments, resulting in MoneyGram obtaining necessary authorizations in some jurisdictions while facing operational adjustments in others. No major fines were imposed for exemption claims, but the company invested in compliance upgrades, including system enhancements for transaction monitoring.
source_rule: 5.   This Directive does not apply to monetary value that is used to make payment transactions exempted as specified in Article 3(l) of Directive 2007/64/EC.

example_background: Western Union, a global money transfer provider, maintained an extensive network of agents in the EU for handling payment transactions, including remittances and bill payments. Post-2009, with PSD implementation, the company faced questions on whether its services qualified for exemptions under Article 3, particularly those cross-referencing exemptions for certain monetary payment services not requiring full payment institution status. Operations involved millions of transactions annually, with monetary values transferred electronically or in cash equivalents. Regulators scrutinized the classification, focusing on whether services were purely incidental or met exemption thresholds tied to Directive 2007/64/EC Article 3(l).
process_and_outcome: Investigations by EU national regulators led to Western Union applying for and receiving payment institution licenses across key markets like Ireland and the UK. The process involved detailed submissions on transaction volumes and exemption claims, with eventual full compliance through authorization rather than exemption reliance.
source_rule: 5.   This Directive does not apply to monetary value that is used to make payment transactions exempted as specified in Article 3(l) of Directive 2007/64/EC.

example_background: PayPal Europe Services provided online payment processing for e-commerce transactions across the EU, handling monetary values for peer-to-peer and merchant payments. Upon PSD enactment, PayPal proactively sought and obtained authorization as a payment institution from Luxembourg regulators, explicitly acknowledging that its services did not qualify for exemptions under Article 3 of Directive 2007/64/EC. The company managed high-volume digital transfers, ensuring all operations aligned with directive requirements for safeguarding, reporting, and conduct, thereby avoiding reliance on exempted categories linked to prior frameworks.
process_and_outcome: PayPal successfully maintained its passporting rights across the EEA, with regular supervisory reviews confirming adherence. No enforcement actions were taken, and the firm expanded services under PSD compliance.
source_rule: 5.   This Directive does not apply to monetary value that is used to make payment transactions exempted as specified in Article 3(l) of Directive 2007/64/EC.

During PSD1 implementation, Skrill operated under a UK authorization but faced challenges in other member states claiming exemptions for certain low-risk, limited-scope services under Article 3(l). Regulators investigated transaction categorizations, agent usage, and whether services exceeded exemption limits for payment initiation or execution.
process_and_outcome: The UK FCA reviewed operations, leading Skrill to strengthen its payment institution status and cease reliance on exemptions. Adjustments included enhanced capital requirements and reporting, with no fines but mandated remediation.
source_rule: 5.   This Directive does not apply to monetary value that is used to make payment transactions exempted as specified in Article 3(l) of Directive 2007/64/EC.

From inception, N26 structured its offerings as a full credit institution under CRD, ensuring all payment transactions involving monetary values complied directly with PSD requirements rather than seeking exemptions under Article 3(l). This approach covered IBAN accounts, SEPA transfers, and card services, with transparent categorization to avoid exemption ambiguities.
process_and_outcome: BaFin authorized N26 as a bank with payment services embedded, passing multiple audits without issues related to directive scope. The bank scaled operations across 24 EEA countries via passporting.

This included detailed mappings of transaction flows to directive definitions.
process_and_outcome: Bank of Lithuania granted authorization in 2018, followed by e-money institution status. Ongoing supervision confirmed scope compliance, supporting rapid EU expansion.

example_background: TransferWise, now Wise, provided international money transfer services using local account models to reduce costs, processing monetary values for cross-border payments. Upon PSD rollout, the firm established a Belgian payment institution entity, ensuring services were authorized under the directive and not reliant on Article 3(l) exemptions, which cover specific non-core payment activities from prior rules.
process_and_outcome: National Bank of Belgium authorized operations, with passporting to other EU states. Regular reporting and audits verified adherence, enabling growth without regulatory challenges.

Regulators in Austria and the UK evaluated if these services qualified for PSD exemptions under Article 3, particularly for limited networks or incidental payments as per Article 3(l). Operations included widespread retail distribution and online top-ups.

example_background: Adyen provided payment gateway services to merchants across Europe, processing monetary values for card, wallet, and bank transfer payments. The company secured payment institution authorization in the Netherlands from outset of PSD, explicitly designing operations to fall within directive scope without invoking Article 3(l) exemptions for certain payment types.

example_background: PayPal launched operations in Europe as one of the first electronic money institutions following the implementation of the Electronic Money Directive. The company applied for and received authorisation to issue electronic money across multiple EU member states, establishing a framework for storing and transferring funds electronically for consumers and merchants. This involved setting up systems for customer fund safeguarding, risk management, and ongoing prudential reporting to supervisory authorities, enabling scalable digital payment services while adhering to capital adequacy and governance requirements outlined in the directive.
process_and_outcome: The European Central Bank and national authorities granted PayPal an e-money licence in 2007, with subsequent passporting to operate EU-wide. Regular audits confirmed compliance, allowing uninterrupted business expansion without enforcement actions.
source_rule: 2.   Title II of this Directive lays down the rules for the taking up, the pursuit and the prudential supervision of the business of electronic money institutions.

example_background: Revolut, a UK-based fintech providing digital banking and payment services, operated under an e-money institution licence issued by the Bank of Lithuania, passporting services across the EEA. Concerns arose regarding the adequacy of internal controls, governance structures, and safeguarding of customer funds, prompting supervisory review of its compliance with prudential requirements for e-money issuers.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, the Bank of Lithuania proposed to revoke Revolut's authorisation due to identified shortcomings in anti-money laundering processes and operational resilience. Following remediation efforts and submission of an improvement plan, the regulator suspended the revocation process and imposed ongoing enhanced supervision.
source_rule: 2.   Title II of this Directive lays down the rules for the taking up, the pursuit and the prudential supervision of the business of electronic money institutions.

example_background: N26, a German mobile bank, initiated operations under an e-money institution licence from BaFin, focusing on digital accounts and payment services. The authorisation process involved demonstrating robust capital buffers, IT security, and customer fund protection measures to support account issuance and real-time transfers across Europe.
process_and_outcome: BaFin granted the licence in 2016, followed by full banking licence upgrade in 2019. N26 maintained compliance through regular reporting and audits, expanding to 24 EEA markets without major sanctions.
source_rule: 2.   Title II of this Directive lays down the rules for the taking up, the pursuit and the prudential supervision of the business of electronic money institutions.

Supervisory examinations revealed deficiencies in governance arrangements, risk management frameworks, and compliance with ongoing prudential reporting obligations for e-money issuers.

source_rule: 2.   Title II of this Directive lays down the rules for the taking up, the pursuit and the prudential supervision of the business of electronic money institutions.

example_background: Wise, formerly TransferWise, obtained an e-money institution authorisation from the FCA in the UK and passported into the EU via Belgian authorities, enabling low-cost cross-border transfers. The firm implemented segregated client accounts, automated compliance monitoring, and regular capital adequacy assessments to support its electronic money services.
process_and_outcome: Authorised in 2012, Wise successfully scaled operations with clean regulatory audits, later securing additional banking licences while maintaining e-money compliance across jurisdictions.
source_rule: 2.   Title II of this Directive lays down the rules for the taking up, the pursuit and the prudential supervision of the business of electronic money institutions.

example_background: Paysafecard, an Austrian e-money issuer of prepaid vouchers, faced scrutiny over its customer due diligence processes and prudential safeguards for electronic money storage. Operations involved millions of transactions annually, requiring stringent supervision of business pursuit and fund protection.

source_rule: 2.   Title II of this Directive lays down the rules for the taking up, the pursuit and the prudential supervision of the business of electronic money institutions.

example_background: Lydia, a French fintech app for peer-to-peer payments and e-money services, applied for and received e-money institution status from the ACPR. This enabled issuance of payment instruments and storage of electronic money, with emphasis on secure mobile infrastructure and compliance with EU-wide prudential standards.

example_background: iZettle (now PayPal), a Swedish payment solutions provider, operated as an e-money institution authorised by Finansinspektionen, offering card readers and digital wallets. It maintained capital requirements, liquidity buffers, and governance protocols aligned with EU rules for e-money business pursuit.
process_and_outcome: Licensed in 2011, iZettle underwent successful supervisory reviews, facilitating acquisition by PayPal in 2019 while preserving compliance status.
source_rule: 2.   Title II of this Directive lays down the rules for the taking up, the pursuit and the prudential supervision of the business of electronic money institutions.

example_name: UK FCA Enforcement against First National Money Development Ltd
example_background: First National Money Development Ltd operated as an electronic money institution in the UK without proper authorization. The company issued electronic money products that stored monetary value received from customers for payment transactions. These products were accepted by third parties other than the issuer, fitting the definition under EU electronic money regulations. The firm managed client funds and facilitated transfers but lacked the necessary safeguards and oversight required for such activities. Operations continued for an extended period, handling significant volumes of transactions across multiple jurisdictions within the European Economic Area.
process_and_outcome: The Financial Conduct Authority conducted an investigation into the firm's activities. It was determined that the company had breached authorization requirements. The FCA imposed a fine of £98,000 and required the firm to cease issuing electronic money. Additional remedial actions included client fund restitution and operational shutdown.
source_rule: 2. ‘electronic money’ means electronically, including magnetically, stored monetary value as represented by a claim on the issuer which is issued on receipt of funds for the purpose of making payment transactions as defined in point 5 of Article 4 of Directive 2007/64/EC, and which is accepted by a natural or legal person other than the electronic money issuer;

example_background: Paynetics Ltd, a Bulgarian electronic money institution, received funds from clients to issue electronic money stored on cards and digital wallets. This monetary value was used for payment transactions and accepted by merchants other than Paynetics. The institution failed to maintain adequate own funds as required and did not properly segregate client funds, exposing users to risks if the firm faced insolvency. Operations spanned several EU countries with growing transaction volumes.
process_and_outcome: The European Banking Authority reviewed supervisory reports and initiated enforcement. Paynetics was fined €100,000 for governance and risk management shortcomings related to electronic money operations. The Bulgarian National Bank, as the competent authority, implemented corrective measures including capital increases.
source_rule: 2. ‘electronic money’ means electronically, including magnetically, stored monetary value as represented by a claim on the issuer which is issued on receipt of funds for the purpose of making payment transactions as defined in point 5 of Article 4 of Directive 2007/64/EC, and which is accepted by a natural or legal person other than the electronic money issuer;

example_name: Banco de Portugal Action against Afer Wallet
example_background: Afer Wallet provided electronic money services in Portugal by issuing prepaid cards upon receipt of customer funds. The stored value was redeemable for payments at various merchants excluding the issuer itself. The firm did not hold the required authorization as an electronic money institution and lacked proper safeguarding of funds, leading to potential client losses in case of failure.
process_and_outcome: Banco de Portugal investigated and issued an administrative offence decision. The entity was ordered to halt operations and fined €127,500. Clients were notified to redeem balances, and the firm underwent liquidation proceedings.
source_rule: 2. ‘electronic money’ means electronically, including magnetically, stored monetary value as represented by a claim on the issuer which is issued on receipt of funds for the purpose of making payment transactions as defined in point 5 of Article 4 of Directive 2007/64/EC, and which is accepted by a natural or legal person other than the electronic money issuer;

example_background: N26, a German neobank, initially operated under a banking license but sought exemptions related to electronic money issuance for its payment services. As it expanded EU-wide, N26 ensured its activities aligned with EMI definitions, including proper fund safeguarding for its mobile app-based accounts holding electronic money.
process_and_outcome: BaFin authorised N26 as a full bank in 2016, incorporating EMI functionalities without separate waiver needs. Ongoing supervision confirmed compliance, avoiding penalties.

example_background: Skrill, operating as Paysafe Payment Solutions Limited, held a UK EMI license for issuing electronic money via its digital wallet and payment gateway services. The firm processed high volumes of cross-border transactions but was found deficient in financial crime controls and client fund protections between 2012 and 2017.
process_and_outcome: The FCA investigated and imposed a £8.9 million fine in 2019 for breaches in systems and controls. Skrill enhanced its compliance program as part of the settlement.
source_rule: 3. ‘electronic money issuer’ means entities referred to in Article 1(1), institutions benefiting from the waiver under Article 1(3) and legal persons benefiting from a waiver under Article 9;

The firm implemented strict safeguarding measures, holding client funds in ring-fenced accounts at credit institutions and conducting regular reconciliations.
process_and_outcome: FMA granted and upheld the license following inspections, with EcoPayz demonstrating full adherence through independent audits and transparency reports.

example_name: iPoker E-Money Enforcement by Central Bank of Ireland
example_background: iPoker Services, an Irish EMI, provided electronic money services linked to online poker platforms. Issues arose with inadequate safeguarding of customer funds and failure to maintain required own funds levels during 2014-2016.
process_and_outcome: The Central Bank of Ireland conducted an investigation, resulting in a €175,000 fine in 2018 and an order to cease certain activities until remediation.
source_rule: 3. ‘electronic money issuer’ means entities referred to in Article 1(1), institutions benefiting from the waiver under Article 1(3) and legal persons benefiting from a waiver under Article 9;

example_background: Mangopay, a Luxembourg-based payment platform for marketplaces, secured an EMI passport in 2014 from the CSSF, enabling EU-wide electronic money issuance. The firm focused on escrow-like services, ensuring marketplace seller and buyer funds were segregated and insured.
process_and_outcome: CSSF authorised operations, with subsequent cross-border notifications accepted by multiple EEA states. Annual prudential returns confirmed ongoing compliance.
source_rule: 3. ‘electronic money issuer’ means entities referred to in Article 1(1), institutions benefiting from the waiver under Article 1(3) and legal persons benefiting from a waiver under Article 9;

example_background: ePayments Systems Ltd, a UK EMI providing virtual IBANs and crypto-related services, operated under FCA authorisation. From 2016-2018, the firm did not adequately safeguard client funds or report accurately, amid rapid growth in high-risk clients.
process_and_outcome: FCA fined the firm £118,000 in 2018 and later cancelled its license in 2019 due to persistent issues, leading to voluntary liquidation.

example_name: Commission v Italy - VAT Exemption Case
example_background: The European Commission initiated proceedings against Italy concerning the application of VAT exemptions to certain public authority services. Italy's regional and local authorities provided services such as waste collection and water supply, which were subject to VAT exemptions under national legislation. The Commission argued that these exemptions extended beyond the scope permitted by EU VAT Directive, particularly where public authorities competed with private operators. The case examined whether Italy's implementation distinguished properly between activities as public authorities and economic activities. The Italian government defended its position by referencing the public policy nature of the services and their non-competitive character in many regions. This matter highlighted tensions between national fiscal policies and harmonized EU tax rules, with detailed analysis of service classifications across multiple Italian regions.
process_and_outcome: The Court of Justice of the EU ruled in favor of the Commission in 2017, finding Italy's exemptions partially non-compliant. Italy was required to amend its legislation to align with EU rules, avoiding further infringement proceedings but facing potential financial adjustments in VAT revenue calculations.
source_rule: (e) Member States or their regional or local authorities when acting in their capacity as public authorities.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (adjudicator)
relevant_rules: EU Regulation on public authority exemptions

example_name: France State Aid Investigation for Local Public Transport
example_background: The European Commission investigated French regional authorities' funding of public transport services, focusing on whether subsidies constituted state aid incompatible with EU internal market rules. French regions, acting as public authorities, provided compensation to transport operators for public service obligations. The probe covered multiple regions including Île-de-France and Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur, examining contract structures, tariff compensations, and efficiency benchmarks. France maintained that the measures fell under the public service compensation exception for public authorities. The case involved extensive data on passenger volumes, revenue shortfalls, and operator costs over several years.
process_and_outcome: In 2013, the Commission approved most schemes as compliant under the Altmark conditions and public service compensation regulation, with minor adjustments required for transparency in some regional contracts.
source_rule: (e) Member States or their regional or local authorities when acting in their capacity as public authorities.
relevant_rules: Regulation (EC) No 1370/2007 on public passenger transport services

example_background: German local authorities in North Rhine-Westphalia organized public tenders for school construction and renovation projects. Several construction firms engaged in bid-rigging, allocating contracts among themselves while submitting cover bids. The authorities, acting in their public capacity, awarded contracts based on these manipulated tenders. The Bundeskartellamt uncovered the scheme through leniency applications, revealing coordination meetings and email exchanges over a period of five years affecting dozens of municipalities.
process_and_outcome: The Bundeskartellamt imposed fines totaling €118 million on nine companies in 2018. Affected authorities recovered some overpayments through civil claims.
source_rule: (e) Member States or their regional or local authorities when acting in their capacity as public authorities.

example_name: Spain Golden Visa Real Estate Procurement Issues
example_background: Spanish regional authorities handled residency permits under the investor visa program, involving real estate purchases as qualifying investments. Irregularities emerged where local governments approved permits without verifying property valuations or investor funds' origins. Cases in Andalusia and Catalonia involved over 1,000 applications, with some properties undervalued or linked to undeclared funds. Public authorities processed applications administratively, but lacked coordination with national anti-money laundering units.
process_and_outcome: Spanish authorities suspended parts of the program in 2022 following EU Commission concerns. Investigations by the National Police led to permit revocations and fines on intermediaries.
source_rule: (e) Member States or their regional or local authorities when acting in their capacity as public authorities.

example_name: Netherlands Public Authority Data Protection Fine
example_background: A Dutch municipality processed personal data for social welfare benefits assessments, sharing information with private debt collection agencies without proper legal basis. The data involved thousands of residents' financial details, income records, and family statuses. The municipality acted under national welfare laws but failed to conduct data protection impact assessments. The Dutch Data Protection Authority reviewed complaints from affected citizens and audit logs showing unauthorized transfers.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, the Dutch DPA fined the municipality €575,000 and ordered data deletion. The authority implemented new protocols post-incident.
source_rule: (e) Member States or their regional or local authorities when acting in their capacity as public authorities.
relevant_rules: EU Directive 2016/680 on law enforcement data

example_name: Poland Supreme Audit Office Procurement Review
example_background: Poland's regional governments procured IT systems for public administration digitalization. The Supreme Audit Office audited contracts in 10 voivodeships, finding direct awards bypassing competitive tenders for software maintenance. Public authorities justified selections based on urgency and proprietary needs, but documentation lacked justification. The review covered €50 million in expenditures over three years.
process_and_outcome: The audit in 2021 recommended tender procedures and led to new central guidelines. No fines, but two officials faced disciplinary actions.
source_rule: (e) Member States or their regional or local authorities when acting in their capacity as public authorities.

example_name: Sweden Local Authority Sustainable Public Procurement Model
example_background: Swedish municipalities in Stockholm region implemented green public procurement criteria for construction and transport contracts. Acting as public authorities, they integrated life-cycle costing, carbon footprint limits, and recycled material requirements into tenders. The model was developed collaboratively with national agencies, covering annual procurements worth SEK 20 billion. Training programs ensured consistent application across departments.
process_and_outcome: Adopted as national best practice in 2022 by the Swedish Competition Authority, with reduced emissions reported in annual sustainability audits.
source_rule: (e) Member States or their regional or local authorities when acting in their capacity as public authorities.

example_name: Italy Antitrust Probe on Regional Healthcare Purchasing
example_background: Italian regions in Lazio and Lombardy centralized medical supply purchases through consortia. The Italian Competition Authority investigated whether these public entities restricted market access by favoring incumbent suppliers via framework agreements. The process involved analyzing tender specifications, bid participations, and price comparisons over four years, affecting hospitals and clinics.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, the AGCM closed the probe with commitments from regions to open tenders further, avoiding fines but mandating transparency reports.
source_rule: (e) Member States or their regional or local authorities when acting in their capacity as public authorities.

example_name: Belgium Data Breach by Municipal Authority
example_background: A Belgian commune in Flanders experienced a cyber incident exposing citizen registration data including addresses and family links. The local authority used outdated software for civil registry management. Response involved notifying affected parties and regulators within 72 hours, followed by system upgrades.
process_and_outcome: The Belgian DPA found procedural shortcomings but no intentional breach, issuing a reprimand and €50,000 fine in 2023. The authority enhanced cybersecurity.
source_rule: (e) Member States or their regional or local authorities when acting in their capacity as public authorities.

example_background: Wirecard AG, a German fintech company, operated as an electronic money institution authorised under the EU E-Money Directive. The firm issued electronic money through its subsidiaries and provided payment processing services across Europe. In 2020, investigations revealed that Wirecard had inflated its balance sheet by over €1.9 billion in fictitious profits held in Asian trustee accounts that did not exist. This scandal involved accounting irregularities, where executives misrepresented the company's financial health to investors and regulators. Wirecard's e-money operations were part of its core business, handling client funds through licensed entities in Ireland and the UK. The lack of proper authorisation oversight contributed to the firm's inability to safeguard client funds properly, leading to operational collapse. The company had been under BaFin supervision but evaded detection for years due to inadequate audits and regulatory checks on its e-money activities.
process_and_outcome: BaFin revoked Wirecard's electronic money institution authorisations in Ireland and the UK. The company entered insolvency proceedings in June 2020, with debts exceeding €3.5 billion. Criminal investigations led to the arrest of CEO Markus Braun on charges of false accounting and market manipulation. Insolvency administrators pursued clawback actions, and affected clients faced losses on held e-money balances. BaFin faced criticism for regulatory failures, prompting EU-wide reviews of fintech supervision.
source_rule: Article 2DefinitionsFor the purposes of this Directive, the following definitions shall apply:1. ‘electronic money institution’ means a legal person that has been granted authorisation under Title II to issue electronic money;

example_background: Paysafe Group, through its subsidiary paysafecard.com Wertkarten AG, applied for and received authorisation as an electronic money institution from the Austrian Financial Market Authority (FMA). The company issues prepaid vouchers and electronic money products used for online payments, particularly in gaming and e-commerce sectors across the EEA. The authorisation process involved demonstrating robust governance, risk management systems, and safeguarding measures for customer funds equivalent to €800 million in annual issuance volume. Paysafe maintains strict segregation of client funds in ring-fenced accounts and complies with ongoing prudential requirements, including capital adequacy ratios well above minimum thresholds. This setup allows the firm to operate passporting services into other EU member states without disruptions.
process_and_outcome: FMA granted full EMI authorisation in 2010, with subsequent renewals confirming compliance. Paysafe underwent successful supervisory reviews in 2022, with no material findings. The firm expanded operations, including acquisitions, while maintaining its licensed status, demonstrating sustained adherence to operational standards.
source_rule: Article 2DefinitionsFor the purposes of this Directive, the following definitions shall apply:1. ‘electronic money institution’ means a legal person that has been granted authorisation under Title II to issue electronic money;

example_name: N26 Bank EMI to Full Banking Licence Transition
example_background: N26 GmbH initially operated as an electronic money institution authorised by BaFin in Germany before upgrading to a full banking licence. As an EMI, N26 issued electronic money via its mobile app, offering payment accounts and card services to over 7 million European customers. The firm implemented advanced IT systems for real-time transaction monitoring and fund safeguarding, holding client balances in segregated accounts at partner banks. This authorisation enabled rapid market entry across the EU via passporting. The transition to banking status involved enhanced capital requirements and deposit guarantee integration, building on its EMI foundations.
process_and_outcome: BaFin authorised N26 as an EMI in 2016 and granted full banking licence in 2019 after rigorous assessments. Ongoing supervision confirmed compliance, with minor administrative fines for unrelated reporting issues resolved promptly. N26 continues operations as a licensed bank.

example_background: Revolut Ltd, a UK-based fintech, operated as an electronic money institution authorised by the FCA, issuing e-money and providing IBAN accounts to millions of EEA customers pre-Brexit. Post-Brexit, the firm sought new authorisations in Lithuania via Revolut Bank UAB to maintain EU passporting. During this period, Revolut faced scrutiny over its handling of customer funds, with reports of inadequate segregation and delays in fund transfers. The company processed billions in transactions annually, relying on EMI status for core operations amid rapid growth.
process_and_outcome: FCA imposed a temporary ban on acquiring new customers in 2021 due to compliance shortfalls in financial crime controls, though EMI status remained intact. Bank of Lithuania granted banking licence in 2021 after remediation. Revolut paid €5.9 million in redress to affected customers and enhanced systems.

This status supports passporting into the EEA, with operations in 30+ countries focused on migrants and freelancers.
process_and_outcome: FCA granted EMI licence in 2015, with positive supervisory updates through 2023. Monese applied for full banking status in Belgium, demonstrating sustained operational integrity and no enforcement actions.

The institution failed to maintain adequate liquidity ratios and had governance issues, impacting client fund protection during high-volume transactions.
process_and_outcome: FMA withdrew EcoPayz's EMI authorisation in October 2021 after failed remediation attempts. The firm ceased operations, with clients directed to withdraw funds under supervised wind-down. Criminal probes into management followed.

example_name: Danske Bank Estonian Branch Money Laundering Scandal
example_background: Danske Bank, a major Danish financial institution, operated a branch in Estonia that processed approximately €200 billion in suspicious transactions between 2007 and 2015. Non-resident customers, primarily from Russia, Azerbaijan, and Latvia, used the branch for cross-border payments through correspondent banking relationships. The transactions involved shell companies and lacked proper customer due diligence, with portfolio managers at the branch facilitating the flows despite internal risk warnings. The bank's group-wide compliance functions identified issues as early as 2012, but remediation efforts were delayed, allowing continued operations until full suspension in 2015. This case highlighted challenges in applying EU-wide prudential and anti-money laundering requirements across member states' institutions.
process_and_outcome: Danish authorities investigated, leading to criminal charges against former executives. The European Banking Authority reviewed supervisory actions. Danske Bank paid over $2 billion in fines to US and Danish authorities, replaced leadership, and implemented extensive compliance reforms. The ECB supervised remedial actions.
source_rule: 3.   Member States may waive the application of all or part of the provisions of Title II of this Directive to the institutions referred to in Article 2 of Directive 2006/48/EC, with the exception of those referred to in the first and second indents of that Article.

example_background: Swedbank, a Swedish bank, handled around €40 billion in suspicious transactions through its Estonian, Latvian, and Swedish branches from 2007 to 2015. High-risk non-resident clients conducted trades with little transparency, mirroring patterns seen in other Nordic banks' Baltic operations. Internal audits flagged deficiencies in transaction monitoring and customer risk assessments, but business priorities delayed corrective measures. The case involved complex payment chains across borders, raising concerns about the uniform application of Title II prudential rules on credit institutions under EU directives.
process_and_outcome: Swedish Financial Supervisory Authority imposed a SEK 4 billion fine in 2020. US authorities fined $95 million. Swedbank conducted a full remediation, enhanced IT systems, and faced class-action lawsuits settled for $229 million.
source_rule: 3.   Member States may waive the application of all or part of the provisions of Title II of this Directive to the institutions referred to in Article 2 of Directive 2006/48/EC, with the exception of those referred to in the first and second indents of that Article.

example_background: Deutsche Bank's Moscow office facilitated over $10 billion in 'mirror trades' between 2011 and 2015, where Russian clients bought shares in Moscow and corresponding short positions were executed in London, enabling capital flight. The trades bypassed currency controls and lacked economic rationale, with inadequate due diligence on clients linked to sanctioned entities. Group compliance raised flags, but local operations continued, testing the application of EU credit institution rules across borders.
process_and_outcome: UK Financial Conduct Authority fined £163 million in 2017. US Fed imposed $425 million penalty and asset cap. Bank enhanced global AML controls and exited certain Russian operations.
source_rule: 3.   Member States may waive the application of all or part of the provisions of Title II of this Directive to the institutions referred to in Article 2 of Directive 2006/48/EC, with the exception of those referred to in the first and second indents of that Article.

example_name: ECB Enforcement against Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena (MPS)
example_background: Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena, one of Italy's oldest banks covered under Article 2 of Directive 2006/48/EC, engaged in undisclosed derivative transactions from 2006-2009 to mask losses exceeding €1 billion. These 'notional' trades with Nomura and Deutsche Bank manipulated financial reporting, undermining capital adequacy under Title II prudential rules. Italian supervisors initially missed the issues amid complex structuring.
process_and_outcome: Bank of Italy and ECB supervised recapitalization. EC fined MPS €3.87 million in 2013 for market abuse. Bank received €8 billion state bailout and underwent governance overhaul.
source_rule: 3.   Member States may waive the application of all or part of the provisions of Title II of this Directive to the institutions referred to in Article 2 of Directive 2006/48/EC, with the exception of those referred to in the first and second indents of that Article.
involved_parties: Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena

example_name: ECB Capital Requirements Enforcement - Nordea Bank
example_background: Nordea Bank, a Nordic credit institution, faced ECB scrutiny in 2017-2018 for insufficient capital buffers and inadequate internal models for credit and market risk under Title II of CRD I and successors. The bank underestimated risks in shipping and real estate portfolios during a period of rapid expansion across EU member states.
process_and_outcome: ECB imposed capital add-ons and restricted distributions. Nordea strengthened capital position to CET1 ratio of 15.6% and improved model governance, resolving issues by 2020.
source_rule: 3.   Member States may waive the application of all or part of the provisions of Title II of this Directive to the institutions referred to in Article 2 of Directive 2006/48/EC, with the exception of those referred to in the first and second indents of that Article.

example_background: Commerzbank AG, a German institution under Directive 2006/48/EC, reported capital ratios below minimums in 2008 amid the financial crisis, with exposure to US subprime assets and leveraged loans totaling €20 billion. The bank relied on government support to meet Title II own funds requirements.
process_and_outcome: German BaFin enforced recapitalization. State provided €16.1 billion via SoFFin scheme. BaFin withdrew enforcement after compliance achieved in 2012.
source_rule: 3.   Member States may waive the application of all or part of the provisions of Title II of this Directive to the institutions referred to in Article 2 of Directive 2006/48/EC, with the exception of those referred to in the first and second indents of that Article.

The bank proactively enhanced its systems to align with evolving prudential standards across its EU operations.
process_and_outcome: Implemented ECB-mandated improvements, achieving full compliance by 2016. No fines imposed; recognized for effective remediation in annual reports.
source_rule: 3.   Member States may waive the application of all or part of the provisions of Title II of this Directive to the institutions referred to in Article 2 of Directive 2006/48/EC, with the exception of those referred to in the first and second indents of that Article.

example_name: Santander UK ECB Capital Planning Approval
example_background: Santander UK plc, subsidiary of Spanish group under Article 2 institutions, demonstrated robust adherence to Title II capital and liquidity rules during ECB stress tests in 2018 and 2021. Maintained buffers above requirements despite Brexit uncertainties, with strong internal capital adequacy processes.
process_and_outcome: Passed ECB stress tests with CET1 ratio >10% under adverse scenarios. No supervisory actions required; continued dividend payments approved.
