source_rule: Where an electronic money institution carries out any of the activities referred to in Article 6(1)(a) that are not linked to the issuance of electronic money or any of the activities referred to in Article 6(1)(b) to (e) and the amount of outstanding electronic money is unknown in advance, the competent authorities shall allow that electronic money institution to apply point (a) of the first subparagraph on the basis of a representative portion assumed to be used for the issuance of electronic money, provided that such a representative portion can be reasonably estimated on the basis of historical data and to the satisfaction of the competent authorities. Where an electronic money institution has not completed a sufficiently long period of business, that requirement shall be assessed on the basis of projected outstanding electronic money evidenced by its business plan subject to any adjustment to that plan having been required by the competent authorities.

example_background: N26, a German electronic money institution offering mobile banking and payment services, launched in 2016 without extended historical data. For its initial authorisation, N26 provided a detailed business plan projecting outstanding e-money based on customer acquisition targets and transaction volumes. The plan allocated 55% of funds to e-money issuance, supported by pilot data and market benchmarks. Competent authorities adjusted the projection downward by 10% as a conservative measure and permitted the representative portion methodology for the first 24 months of operation.
process_and_outcome: BaFin granted full EMD licence in 2016 with the approved methodology. Annual reviews confirmed projections held accurate within 5%, leading to no adjustments or penalties.
source_rule: Where an electronic money institution carries out any of the activities referred to in Article 6(1)(a) that are not linked to the issuance of electronic money or any of the activities referred to in Article 6(1)(b) to (e) and the amount of outstanding electronic money is unknown in advance, the competent authorities shall allow that electronic money institution to apply point (a) of the first subparagraph on the basis of a representative portion assumed to be used for the issuance of electronic money, provided that such a representative portion can be reasonably estimated on the basis of historical data and to the satisfaction of the competent authorities. Where an electronic money institution has not completed a sufficiently long period of business, that requirement shall be assessed on the basis of projected outstanding electronic money evidenced by its business plan subject to any adjustment to that plan having been required by the competent authorities.
relevant_rules: BaFin MaRisk (Minimum Requirements for Risk Management)
CRD IV capital rules for EMIs

example_background: EcoPayz, an Austrian electronic money institution regulated in the UK, provided e-wallets and payment services across Europe. In 2017-2018, as its foreign exchange and money remittance services expanded beyond e-money issuance, the firm applied a fixed 70% representative portion for safeguarding without updating historical data analyses. FCA examinations found the portion overstated non-issuance activities, resulting in a £2.1 million safeguarding deficit over six months. The methodology lacked supervisory pre-approval and granular transaction logging.
process_and_outcome: FCA issued a 2019 enforcement notice, requiring £2.1 million restitution to segregated accounts and a £168,000 fine. EcoPayz overhauled its systems and received conditional approval for a revised approach.
source_rule: Where an electronic money institution carries out any of the activities referred to in Article 6(1)(a) that are not linked to the issuance of electronic money or any of the activities referred to in Article 6(1)(b) to (e) and the amount of outstanding electronic money is unknown in advance, the competent authorities shall allow that electronic money institution to apply point (a) of the first subparagraph on the basis of a representative portion assumed to be used for the issuance of electronic money, provided that such a representative portion can be reasonably estimated on the basis of historical data and to the satisfaction of the competent authorities. Where an electronic money institution has not completed a sufficiently long period of business, that requirement shall be assessed on the basis of projected outstanding electronic money evidenced by its business plan subject to any adjustment to that plan having been required by the competent authorities.

example_background: Monzo, authorised as an electronic money institution in the UK, offered current accounts, payments, and e-money storage. After three years of operation, Monzo's data showed 42% of transaction volumes linked to e-money, with stable quarterly patterns across user segments. The firm presented this to the FCA for approval of a representative portion methodology, including sensitivity analyses for seasonal variations and product mixes. This allowed proportional safeguarding instead of 100% coverage for mixed activities.
process_and_outcome: FCA approved the 42% portion in 2020 as part of Monzo's prudential category upgrade. Compliance was verified in 2022 thematic review with no issues noted.
source_rule: Where an electronic money institution carries out any of the activities referred to in Article 6(1)(a) that are not linked to the issuance of electronic money or any of the activities referred to in Article 6(1)(b) to (e) and the amount of outstanding electronic money is unknown in advance, the competent authorities shall allow that electronic money institution to apply point (a) of the first subparagraph on the basis of a representative portion assumed to be used for the issuance of electronic money, provided that such a representative portion can be reasonably estimated on the basis of historical data and to the satisfaction of the competent authorities. Where an electronic money institution has not completed a sufficiently long period of business, that requirement shall be assessed on the basis of projected outstanding electronic money evidenced by its business plan subject to any adjustment to that plan having been required by the competent authorities.

example_background: Skrill, a UK electronic money institution under Paysafe Group, facilitated global payments and e-wallets. Supervisory returns from 2016-2019 revealed inconsistent application of representative portions for safeguarding, particularly for FX conversion and wallet transfers not tied to e-money. Historical data was available but not adequately analysed for representativeness, leading to periodic over- and under-estimates of outstanding e-money by up to 15%. This stemmed from siloed product reporting systems.
process_and_outcome: FCA skilled person review in 2020 identified £4.5 million in adjustments needed. Skrill paid £100,000 penalty and invested £2 million in compliance tech.
source_rule: Where an electronic money institution carries out any of the activities referred to in Article 6(1)(a) that are not linked to the issuance of electronic money or any of the activities referred to in Article 6(1)(b) to (e) and the amount of outstanding electronic money is unknown in advance, the competent authorities shall allow that electronic money institution to apply point (a) of the first subparagraph on the basis of a representative portion assumed to be used for the issuance of electronic money, provided that such a representative portion can be reasonably estimated on the basis of historical data and to the satisfaction of the competent authorities. Where an electronic money institution has not completed a sufficiently long period of business, that requirement shall be assessed on the basis of projected outstanding electronic money evidenced by its business plan subject to any adjustment to that plan having been required by the competent authorities.

example_background: Starling Bank, a UK electronic money institution, developed a data-driven model post-2017 launch using two years of transaction history. The model apportioned 38% of balances to e-money based on issuance-linked tags and redemption patterns, validated against independent benchmarks. Submitted to regulators with auditor certification, it addressed mixed activities under Article 6(1).
process_and_outcome: FCA and PRA accepted the methodology in 2019 licensing review. Implemented successfully, with annual revalidation showing <2% variance.
source_rule: Where an electronic money institution carries out any of the activities referred to in Article 6(1)(a) that are not linked to the issuance of electronic money or any of the activities referred to in Article 6(1)(b) to (e) and the amount of outstanding electronic money is unknown in advance, the competent authorities shall allow that electronic money institution to apply point (a) of the first subparagraph on the basis of a representative portion assumed to be used for the issuance of electronic money, provided that such a representative portion can be reasonably estimated on the basis of historical data and to the satisfaction of the competent authorities. Where an electronic money institution has not completed a sufficiently long period of business, that requirement shall be assessed on the basis of projected outstanding electronic money evidenced by its business plan subject to any adjustment to that plan having been required by the competent authorities.

example_background: PayPal (Europe) Ltd, an Irish electronic money institution serving EEA, expanded into acquiring and processing services. Early calculations used unapproved representative portions without historical backing, resulting in temporary safeguarding gaps during 2015-2016 growth phase. Central Bank of Ireland review prompted methodological overhaul using granular data.
process_and_outcome: Central Bank required plan adjustments and issued administrative sanctions in 2018 totaling €1 million. PayPal achieved full compliance by 2019.
source_rule: Where an electronic money institution carries out any of the activities referred to in Article 6(1)(a) that are not linked to the issuance of electronic money or any of the activities referred to in Article 6(1)(b) to (e) and the amount of outstanding electronic money is unknown in advance, the competent authorities shall allow that electronic money institution to apply point (a) of the first subparagraph on the basis of a representative portion assumed to be used for the issuance of electronic money, provided that such a representative portion can be reasonably estimated on the basis of historical data and to the satisfaction of the competent authorities. Where an electronic money institution has not completed a sufficiently long period of business, that requirement shall be assessed on the basis of projected outstanding electronic money evidenced by its business plan subject to any adjustment to that plan having been required by the competent authorities.

example_background: MoneyGram International operated payment services across Europe through its subsidiaries, including money transfer services that involved cross-border payments not tied to electronic money issuance. The company sought authorisation under the revised Payment Services Directive to continue providing these services legally within the European Economic Area. Prior to full compliance, operations were conducted under transitional arrangements while preparing for the new regulatory framework. This involved detailed assessments of safeguarding customer funds, risk management procedures, and governance structures to align with requirements for payment institutions.
process_and_outcome: The Central Bank of Ireland granted authorisation in 2020 for MoneyGram Europe Ltd to provide payment services under PSD2. The process included submission of comprehensive applications, ongoing supervisory reviews, and implementation of required operational controls, enabling continued service provision without interruption.
source_rule: A legal person registered in accordance with this paragraph may provide payment services not related to electronic money issued in accordance with this Article only if conditions set out in Article 26 of Directive 2007/64/EC are met.

example_background: Western Union Payment Services Ireland Limited provided extensive payment services including international money remittances across Europe. Issues arose with persistent deficiencies in governance, internal controls, anti-money laundering measures, and customer fund protections. These shortcomings were identified through supervisory inspections and thematic reviews, leading to prolonged engagement with the regulator to address operational and compliance gaps.
process_and_outcome: The Central Bank of Ireland revoked the authorisation in June 2023 after multiple remediation failures. The decision followed years of supervision, enforcement actions, and a consultation process, resulting in cessation of regulated activities in Ireland while existing customer funds were protected through transfer to another institution.
source_rule: A legal person registered in accordance with this paragraph may provide payment services not related to electronic money issued in accordance with this Article only if conditions set out in Article 26 of Directive 2007/64/EC are met.
involved_parties: Western Union Payment Services Ireland Ltd
relevant_rules: Criminal Justice (Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing) Act 2010

example_background: Paysafe Payment Services Ireland Limited, part of the Paysafe Group, offers payment processing and gateway services for merchants across Europe. To expand services beyond electronic money issuance, the company applied for payment institution status. This required demonstrating robust initial capital, professional management, effective risk management, and secure fund safeguarding measures as per EU directives.
process_and_outcome: Authorisation was granted by the Central Bank of Ireland in 2021 following a rigorous assessment process. The firm implemented enhanced compliance programs, including regular audits and reporting, to maintain ongoing adherence.
source_rule: A legal person registered in accordance with this paragraph may provide payment services not related to electronic money issued in accordance with this Article only if conditions set out in Article 26 of Directive 2007/64/EC are met.

example_background: UK Finance, representing major banks and payment firms, conducted industry-wide assessments on compliance with PSD2 requirements for payment initiation and account information services. Firms providing payment services without proper authorisation or failing to meet conditions like strong customer authentication were highlighted in reports. This included cases where non-registered entities attempted to offer services linked to third-party providers.
process_and_outcome: The Financial Conduct Authority issued guidance and enforcement notices in 2018-2019, with several firms receiving warnings or restrictions. Authorised entities adjusted operations to meet conditions, while unauthorised ones ceased activities.
source_rule: A legal person registered in accordance with this paragraph may provide payment services not related to electronic money issued in accordance with this Article only if conditions set out in Article 26 of Directive 2007/64/EC are met.

example_background: Revolut Ltd, a fintech providing digital banking and payment services, initially operated under electronic money institution status. To offer broader payment services such as direct card payments and transfers not linked to its e-money, it pursued payment institution authorisation. This involved scaling up capital reserves, enhancing IT security, and establishing comprehensive compliance frameworks.
process_and_outcome: The FCA authorised Revolut as a payment institution in 2018 under PSD2 equivalence, later progressing to banking licence stages. Ongoing supervision ensured conditions like those in Article 26 of PSD1 were met through regular reporting and audits.
source_rule: A legal person registered in accordance with this paragraph may provide payment services not related to electronic money issued in accordance with this Article only if conditions set out in Article 26 of Directive 2007/64/EC are met.

example_background: The European Banking Authority reviewed national implementations of PSD2 and identified cases where payment service providers operated without meeting authorisation conditions, such as inadequate own funds or poor risk management. Specific instances involved smaller PISPs and AISPs failing to demonstrate compliance before launching services.
process_and_outcome: In 2019-2020, the EBA issued opinions and recommendations to competent authorities, leading to enforcement actions including fines and licence withdrawals in several member states. This prompted industry-wide improvements in application processes.
source_rule: A legal person registered in accordance with this paragraph may provide payment services not related to electronic money issued in accordance with this Article only if conditions set out in Article 26 of Directive 2007/64/EC are met.

example_background: N26, a German mobile bank, held an electronic money institution licence but expanded into full payment services including SEPA transfers and card issuing. To comply, it applied for and obtained payment institution status, focusing on conditions like maintaining initial capital, appointing qualified management, and implementing IT and security policies.
process_and_outcome: BaFin authorised the expansion in 2016-2017, with PSD2 full compliance by 2018. The bank underwent successful supervisory reviews and integrated strong customer authentication ahead of deadlines.
source_rule: A legal person registered in accordance with this paragraph may provide payment services not related to electronic money issued in accordance with this Article only if conditions set out in Article 26 of Directive 2007/64/EC are met.

example_name: PayPal PSD1 to PSD2 Transition Fine
example_background: PayPal (Europe) SARL provided payment services under PSD1 but faced challenges during PSD2 transition, including delays in implementing strong customer authentication and issues with third-party provider access. Supervisory findings noted gaps in meeting ongoing conditions for authorised payment institutions.
process_and_outcome: The Central Bank of Ireland imposed a €2.5 million fine in 2022 for breaches related to customer authentication and data protection during the transition period. PayPal remediated through system upgrades and enhanced monitoring.
source_rule: A legal person registered in accordance with this paragraph may provide payment services not related to electronic money issued in accordance with this Article only if conditions set out in Article 26 of Directive 2007/64/EC are met.

example_background: Passport Wallet SARL, a company registered under Luxembourg law, operated a finance leasing business. The company maintained its registered office and central administration in Luxembourg but conducted all its business activities, including signature of contracts, payments, and customer relations, exclusively in Belgium where it also had premises. The arrangement raised questions about whether the company's substance aligned with its registered jurisdiction, as required under EU freedom of establishment principles for companies pursuing business activities.
process_and_outcome: The European Court of Justice ruled that for a company to rely on freedom of establishment, it must pursue its business activities in the Member State of registration with a real and continuous presence, including central administration and operational substance there. The case was referred back for national determination, establishing key precedents on company substance requirements.
source_rule: 2.   A legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 shall be required to have its head office in the Member State in which it actually pursues its business.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (adjudicator)

example_background: Daily Mail & General Trust plc, a UK-registered company, sought to transfer its central management and control to the Netherlands while retaining its legal personality and tax residence in the UK. The company argued this would facilitate operations, but UK tax authorities required prior clearance for the transfer, prompting a challenge on freedom of establishment grounds. The case examined whether companies could relocate substance without losing their original registration status.
process_and_outcome: The ECJ held that EU law does not grant companies the right to transfer their central management to another Member State while retaining their status as companies of the original State. Member States retain competence to determine incorporation and substance links, provided they do not discriminate.
source_rule: 2.   A legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 shall be required to have its head office in the Member State in which it actually pursues its business.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (adjudicator)

example_background: Cadbury Schweppes, a UK parent company, established subsidiary companies in Ireland and Luxembourg for financing operations within the EU. These subsidiaries had minimal local staff and activities, primarily serving as conduits for intra-group financing. UK tax authorities challenged the structures under controlled foreign company rules, alleging artificial arrangements lacking economic substance in the host Member States.
process_and_outcome: The ECJ ruled that purely formal establishments without genuine economic activity do not qualify for freedom of establishment protection. However, the subsidiaries demonstrated sufficient substance through decision-making and operations, leading to annulment of the UK tax assessments.
source_rule: 2.   A legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 shall be required to have its head office in the Member State in which it actually pursues its business.
involved_parties: UK Commissioners for HM Revenue & Customs (regulator)
Court of Justice of the EU (adjudicator)

example_background: FCE Bank plc, a UK subsidiary of Ford Motor Company, provided vehicle financing services across the EU. It maintained its registered office and central administration in the UK but conducted significant operational activities in other Member States through branches. Belgian authorities sought to apply local consumer credit rules, questioning the effective pursuit of business from the UK head office.
process_and_outcome: The ECJ confirmed that a company pursues its business in its home Member State where it has its registered office, central administration, and principal place of business, even with extensive branch operations elsewhere. Belgian rules were held inapplicable.
source_rule: 2.   A legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 shall be required to have its head office in the Member State in which it actually pursues its business.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (adjudicator)

example_background: Oversea Insurance Company Ltd was registered in Ireland but conducted all its insurance underwriting and management activities from London, UK, with no staff or operations in Ireland. The company collected premiums and handled claims primarily through UK activities, maintaining only a nominal presence in Ireland.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Court of Appeal held that the company failed to pursue its business in Ireland as required. Its name was struck off the Irish register, and it lost authorization to operate as an Irish insurer.
source_rule: 2.   A legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 shall be required to have its head office in the Member State in which it actually pursues its business.

example_background: The Estonian Tax and Customs Board identified numerous companies registered in Estonia that lacked local directors, employees, or business premises, instead operating remotely from other jurisdictions or serving as holding vehicles with no substantive activities in Estonia. These entities were used for tax planning but did not maintain head offices where business was pursued.
process_and_outcome: EMTA revoked registrations of over 200 shell companies between 2018-2020, imposed fines, and required dissolution. Administrative proceedings confirmed absence of real economic presence.
source_rule: 2.   A legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 shall be required to have its head office in the Member State in which it actually pursues its business.
involved_parties: Estonian Tax and Customs Board (EMTA, regulator)

example_background: Centros Ltd, formed by Danish nationals, registered as a private limited company in the UK with its registered office there but conducted all business activities in Denmark without establishing a branch. The company aimed to avoid higher Danish minimum capital requirements while operating solely in Denmark.
process_and_outcome: The ECJ ruled that Member States cannot deny recognition of a company validly incorporated in another Member State where it pursues no activity, even if intended to circumvent local rules, as long as the registration meets home state requirements.
source_rule: 2.   A legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 shall be required to have its head office in the Member State in which it actually pursues its business.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (adjudicator)

example_name: Danske Bank Estonia Branch Substance Issues
example_background: Danske Bank's Estonian branch, registered under Lithuanian law as part of its EU passporting, handled massive non-resident customer flows through the branch. However, key decision-making, risk management, and compliance functions were centralized in Denmark, with the Estonian branch lacking independent substance and local management autonomy.
process_and_outcome: Estonian FIU and central bank investigated, leading to license restrictions and €1 billion+ global fines. AMLD substance rules were applied alongside local oversight, resulting in branch restructuring.
source_rule: 2.   A legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 shall be required to have its head office in the Member State in which it actually pursues its business.

example_background: ING Direct Luxembourg S.A., a fully licensed credit institution, established its registered office, board of directors, senior management, risk functions, and IT infrastructure in Luxembourg. It served retail customers across the EEA through digital channels while maintaining local decision-making and operational substance.
process_and_outcome: CSSF supervisory reviews confirmed adequate substance, granting full EEA passporting rights without restrictions. The bank expanded operations successfully.
source_rule: 2.   A legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 shall be required to have its head office in the Member State in which it actually pursues its business.
involved_parties: Commission de Surveillance du Secteur Financier (CSSF, regulator)
relevant_rules: Luxembourg Law of 5 April 1993 on financial sector

example_name: FTX Collapse and Asset Security Failures
example_background: FTX, a major cryptocurrency exchange, experienced a significant liquidity crisis in November 2022 when customers attempted large-scale withdrawals following concerns about its financial position. The platform held substantial digital assets including Bitcoin, Ethereum, and various altcoins, but internal audits later revealed that customer funds were commingled with those of its affiliated hedge fund, Alameda Research. This arrangement exposed assets to heightened risks from leveraged trading positions and poor risk management practices. The evaluation of asset security was inadequate, as the platform lacked sufficient segregation, real-time monitoring, and collateral requirements for its crypto holdings, leading to an inability to meet redemption demands. Maturity assessments were not conducted rigorously, with many assets being highly volatile and illiquid tokens. The incident unfolded amid a broader market downturn, amplifying the value risk elements, and highlighted deficiencies in classifying assets as secure and low-risk despite their prominence in the platform's reserves.
process_and_outcome: Bankruptcy proceedings were initiated in the U.S. Bankruptcy Court for the District of Delaware, with investigations by the U.S. Department of Justice, SEC, and CFTC. FTX filed for Chapter 11 protection, and Sam Bankman-Fried, the founder, faced criminal charges resulting in a conviction and 25-year prison sentence in 2024. Creditors approved a reorganization plan providing approximately 119% recovery value, with ongoing asset distribution supervised by a restructuring team.
source_rule: In exceptional circumstances and with adequate justification, the competent authorities may, based on an evaluation of security, maturity, value or other risk element of the assets as specified in the first and second subparagraphs, determine which of those assets do not constitute secure, low-risk assets for the purposes of paragraph 1.

example_background: TerraUSD (UST) was an algorithmic stablecoin designed to maintain a 1:1 peg with the U.S. dollar through a dual-token system involving LUNA. Launched in 2021 by Terraform Labs, it grew to over $18 billion in circulation by May 2022. The mechanism relied on arbitrage incentives and burning/minting dynamics between UST and LUNA to stabilize value. However, during a market stress period triggered by broader crypto sell-offs, large withdrawals from the Anchor Protocol lending platform depleted reserves. An evaluation of security revealed insufficient backing mechanisms for extreme scenarios, as the system lacked external collateral and depended on LUNA's market cap, which plummeted. Maturity was low due to the unproven algorithmic design, and value risk was elevated given the high yield promises attracting depositors. Authorities later determined these assets did not qualify as secure or low-risk owing to systemic vulnerabilities.
process_and_outcome: The peg broke on May 9, 2022, leading to UST losing over 90% of its value and LUNA crashing from $80 to near zero. SEC charged Terraform Labs and Do Kwon with fraud, resulting in a $4.47 billion settlement in 2024. South Korean and U.S. authorities pursued extradition and civil penalties, with ongoing investor restitution efforts.
source_rule: In exceptional circumstances and with adequate justification, the competent authorities may, based on an evaluation of security, maturity, value or other risk element of the assets as specified in the first and second subparagraphs, determine which of those assets do not constitute secure, low-risk assets for the purposes of paragraph 1.

example_background: During the 2008 financial crisis, UBS's ETF operations in Europe faced scrutiny over the security and risk classification of underlying assets. The funds held mortgage-backed securities and other structured products valued at billions of euros. Internal risk assessments initially categorized these as low-risk based on ratings from agencies like Moody's and S&P. However, as subprime exposures materialized, liquidity dried up, revealing inadequate maturity matching and value volatility. Competent authorities reviewed the portfolios and found that the assets' security was compromised by counterparty risks and lack of diversification. Exceptional circumstances arose from market-wide deleveraging, prompting a re-evaluation that excluded these from secure, low-risk designations under emerging standards.
process_and_outcome: Swiss regulator FINMA imposed remedial measures, including capital surcharges and asset sales. UBS recorded $50 billion in write-downs, settled U.S. claims for $885 million with the DOJ, and restructured its ETF operations with enhanced risk frameworks.
source_rule: In exceptional circumstances and with adequate justification, the competent authorities may, based on an evaluation of security, maturity, value or other risk element of the assets as specified in the first and second subparagraphs, determine which of those assets do not constitute secure, low-risk assets for the purposes of paragraph 1.

example_name: ECB Determination on Greek Government Bonds 2012
example_background: In the midst of the European sovereign debt crisis, the European Central Bank evaluated Greek government bonds held by eurozone banks as collateral. These bonds, previously accepted as low-risk assets due to sovereign status, underwent scrutiny amid Greece's debt restructuring. Security assessments considered default probabilities post-haircut agreements, maturity profiles extending beyond stabilization horizons, and value fluctuations from CDS spreads. Exceptional circumstances justified by systemic contagion risks led authorities to deem these bonds non-qualifying as secure, low-risk for monetary policy operations.
process_and_outcome: ECB suspended eligibility in March 2012, requiring additional haircuts and substitutions. This facilitated Long-Term Refinancing Operations (LTROs) totaling €1 trillion to stabilize banking liquidity without further bond acceptance.
source_rule: In exceptional circumstances and with adequate justification, the competent authorities may, based on an evaluation of security, maturity, value or other risk element of the assets as specified in the first and second subparagraphs, determine which of those assets do not constitute secure, low-risk assets for the purposes of paragraph 1.
relevant_rules: ECB Guideline on Temporary Collateral Framework

example_name: ESMA Review of Money Market Fund Assets 2020
example_background: Following market turmoil from COVID-19 liquidity strains, the European Securities and Markets Authority conducted a targeted review of money market fund (MMF) holdings. Funds maintained portfolios of short-term debt instruments, commercial paper, and certificates of deposit. Security evaluations focused on issuer creditworthiness, maturity under 397 days, and low volatility. Certain assets from sectors like aviation and energy exhibited elevated risks, prompting authorities to apply the exceptional circumstances clause based on pandemic-induced value impairments and liquidity mismatches.
process_and_outcome: ESMA issued recommendations in July 2020 for gates, fees, and asset reclassifications. No major fines ensued, but funds adjusted portfolios, enhancing overall sector resilience.
source_rule: In exceptional circumstances and with adequate justification, the competent authorities may, based on an evaluation of security, maturity, value or other risk element of the assets as specified in the first and second subparagraphs, determine which of those assets do not constitute secure, low-risk assets for the purposes of paragraph 1.

example_background: As part of the euro short-term rate (€STR) transition from EURIBOR and EONIA, Deutsche Bank's treasury held floating-rate notes and derivatives tied to phasing-out benchmarks. Risk assessments evaluated security through fallback provisions, maturity concentrations pre-2025 cutoff, and value sensitivities to transition shocks. Competent authorities identified exceptional risks in non-compliant instruments, determining they did not meet secure, low-risk criteria under updated frameworks.
process_and_outcome: BaFin mandated remediation plans, resulting in €2.5 billion in portfolio hedges and synthetic conversions. No penalties applied due to proactive compliance, with ECB oversight confirming adequacy.
source_rule: In exceptional circumstances and with adequate justification, the competent authorities may, based on an evaluation of security, maturity, value or other risk element of the assets as specified in the first and second subparagraphs, determine which of those assets do not constitute secure, low-risk assets for the purposes of paragraph 1.

example_name: Bain Capital Crypto Fund Collateral Dispute 2022
example_background: Bain Capital's crypto hedge fund utilized Bitcoin and Ethereum as collateral for loans in DeFi protocols. During the 2022 crypto winter, asset values dropped sharply, exposing over-collateralization shortfalls. Security evaluations highlighted smart contract vulnerabilities and oracle price feed risks, while maturity was indefinite for perpetual positions. Value risks from 24/7 trading amplified exposures, leading regulators to question low-risk status.
process_and_outcome: SEC investigated for disclosure failures, settling with a $1.5 million fine in 2023. The fund liquidated positions and shifted to regulated custodians.
source_rule: In exceptional circumstances and with adequate justification, the competent authorities may, based on an evaluation of security, maturity, value or other risk element of the assets as specified in the first and second subparagraphs, determine which of those assets do not constitute secure, low-risk assets for the purposes of paragraph 1.

example_background: The European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority reviewed insurers' sovereign bond portfolios amid low-interest environments. Holdings included Italian and Spanish government debt with long maturities and yield curve risks. Security hinged on implicit guarantees, but negative rates and fiscal deteriorations prompted re-evaluations. Exceptional circumstances from Brexit spillovers justified excluding certain bonds from low-risk asset classes.
process_and_outcome: EIOPA issued guidelines in 2018, leading to risk charge recalibrations. Insurers increased capital buffers by €20 billion collectively without enforcement actions.
source_rule: In exceptional circumstances and with adequate justification, the competent authorities may, based on an evaluation of security, maturity, value or other risk element of the assets as specified in the first and second subparagraphs, determine which of those assets do not constitute secure, low-risk assets for the purposes of paragraph 1.
involved_parties: European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority (EIOPA)

example_background: BlockFi offered interest-bearing crypto accounts promising up to 9.5% APY on BTC and ETH holdings. Customer assets were pooled and lent to institutional borrowers without full disclosure of counterparty risks. Security assessments showed inadequate segregation, maturity mismatches from open-ended terms, and value volatility from crypto markets. Regulators determined these did not constitute secure, low-risk assets.
process_and_outcome: SEC charged BlockFi with operating unregistered securities in 2022, resulting in a $100 million settlement and cessation of the product. Bankruptcy followed in 2022 with customer recoveries prioritized.
source_rule: In exceptional circumstances and with adequate justification, the competent authorities may, based on an evaluation of security, maturity, value or other risk element of the assets as specified in the first and second subparagraphs, determine which of those assets do not constitute secure, low-risk assets for the purposes of paragraph 1.

example_background: Paysafecard Prepaid Services Limited, a company providing prepaid electronic money products across Europe, applied for authorisation under the EU electronic money framework. The entity was registered in the UK and offered services allowing customers to purchase vouchers for online payments without a traditional bank account. The application process involved demonstrating robust governance, risk management, and customer fund safeguarding procedures. Operations spanned multiple EU member states, requiring coordination with national competent authorities. The company detailed its business model, including voucher issuance, redemption, and anti-fraud measures, while outlining capital adequacy and internal controls to handle electronic money issuance volumes exceeding millions of transactions annually.
process_and_outcome: The UK Financial Conduct Authority granted authorisation as an electronic money institution in 2012, confirming compliance with registration and operational requirements. The firm continues operations under ongoing supervision without exemptions under Articles 10(9) or 25 applied.
source_rule: 3.   A legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 shall be treated as an electronic money institution. However, Article 10(9) and Article 25 of Directive 2007/64/EC shall not apply to it.

example_name: Revolut Ltd Electronic Money Institution Licence
example_background: Revolut Ltd, a fintech firm founded in 2015, initially operated as an electronic money institution in the UK, issuing electronic money for its multi-currency accounts and payment services. The company grew rapidly, serving millions of customers across Europe with features like instant transfers, currency exchange, and budgeting tools. It maintained segregated client funds and reported regularly on issuance volumes. Expansion into banking services prompted a transition, but its EMI status handled core electronic money functions under EU rules, excluding certain credit-related exemptions.
process_and_outcome: FCA authorised Revolut as an EMI in 2017 (FRN 900562), with subsequent upgrade to a banking licence in 2021 while retaining EMI operations. No penalties issued for EMI compliance.
source_rule: 3.   A legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 shall be treated as an electronic money institution. However, Article 10(9) and Article 25 of Directive 2007/64/EC shall not apply to it.

example_background: N26, a mobile banking provider, started as an electronic money institution in Germany, offering digital accounts and payment services powered by e-money issuance. The company focused on user-friendly app-based banking, real-time notifications, and Spaces sub-accounts. It registered under EMD2, ensuring customer funds protection through safeguarding and maintaining adequate own funds relative to issuance liabilities. Growth involved passporting services to other EU countries.
process_and_outcome: BaFin authorised N26 as EMI in 2016, later granting full banking licence in 2017. Passed multiple supervisory reviews without major issues.
source_rule: 3.   A legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 shall be treated as an electronic money institution. However, Article 10(9) and Article 25 of Directive 2007/64/EC shall not apply to it.

example_background: PayPal (Europe) Ltd, authorised as an EMI in Luxembourg and passporting across EU, handles vast e-money issuance for user balances. In 2018, discrepancies emerged in safeguarding account reconciliations during routine audits, where client fund protections were questioned amid high transaction volumes and complex global operations involving multiple banking partners.
process_and_outcome: Commission de Surveillance du Secteur Financier (CSSF) investigated; PayPal paid €1.55 million fine in 2019 for failures in safeguarding and reporting requirements.
source_rule: 3.   A legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 shall be treated as an electronic money institution. However, Article 10(9) and Article 25 of Directive 2007/64/EC shall not apply to it.

example_background: EcoPayz, operating as Avela AG in the UK, provides e-wallet and prepaid card services as an electronic money institution. The platform supports deposits, withdrawals, and peer-to-peer transfers for gaming and e-commerce sectors. Registration involved proving adequate systems for fund segregation, AML monitoring, and complaint handling across EU passporting.
process_and_outcome: FCA granted EMI authorisation in 2015 (FRN 601218); subject to regular supervision with no major sanctions.
source_rule: 3.   A legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 shall be treated as an electronic money institution. However, Article 10(9) and Article 25 of Directive 2007/64/EC shall not apply to it.

example_background: Fonum, a Lithuanian EMI providing payment initiation and e-money services, faced issues with timely submission of periodic reports on e-money issuance and safeguarding balances. The firm operated digital wallets for cross-border remittances, handling funds for migrant workers.
process_and_outcome: Bank of Lithuania imposed €50,000 fine in 2020 for reporting delays and inadequate record-keeping.
source_rule: 3.   A legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 shall be treated as an electronic money institution. However, Article 10(9) and Article 25 of Directive 2007/64/EC shall not apply to it.

example_name: UniCredit Bank AG EMIR Reporting Failures
example_background: UniCredit Bank AG, a major European financial institution headquartered in Germany, engaged in derivatives trading activities across the EU. From 2016 to 2021, the bank submitted incomplete and inaccurate reports for over 1.5 million derivative transactions to trade repositories. These reports lacked mandatory fields such as unique transaction identifiers, product identifiers, and critical timestamps, affecting both exchange-traded and over-the-counter derivatives. The issues arose from deficiencies in the bank's automated reporting systems, including incorrect mappings of data fields and failures to reconcile trades with counterparties. This impacted the transparency of the derivatives market intended by EU regulations, as regulators rely on accurate data for systemic risk monitoring. UniCredit operated under a full banking license but its reporting processes did not align with requirements for institutions handling derivatives.
process_and_outcome: The European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) conducted a review and imposed a public notice highlighting serious shortcomings. No financial penalty was levied, but UniCredit was required to submit a detailed remediation plan, enhance its IT systems, and conduct staff training. The bank invested millions in compliance upgrades and reported full resolution by 2022.
source_rule: 4.   Member States may provide for a legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 to engage only in some of the activities listed in Article 6(1).

example_background: BNP Paribas, a French global bank, was involved in extensive derivatives trading within the EU. Between 2014 and 2018, the institution failed to report approximately 900,000 derivative transactions accurately to approved trade repositories. Errors included missing unique trade identifiers, incorrect valuation dates, and mismatched counterparty data, stemming from flawed reconciliation processes and system integration issues post-EMIR implementation. The bank held a comprehensive banking license permitting a full suite of financial services, including derivatives dealing, but its reporting mechanism did not ensure data completeness as required for market participants under EU rules.
process_and_outcome: ESMA issued a public breach notice in 2019. BNP Paribas cooperated fully, remediated systems by upgrading reporting platforms, and faced no monetary fine. Follow-up assessments confirmed improved compliance.
source_rule: 4.   Member States may provide for a legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 to engage only in some of the activities listed in Article 6(1).
involved_parties: Autorité de Contrôle Prudentiel et de Résolution (ACPR, French NCA)
relevant_rules: RTS on Reporting (Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) No 1247/2012)

example_background: Citigroup Global Markets Europe AG, part of Citigroup's European operations, engages in securities financing transactions (SFTs) such as repos and securities lending. Following the 2020 rollout of the Securities Financing Transactions Regulation (SFTR), the firm reported millions of SFTs but with persistent inaccuracies, including unreported transactions, incorrect field population for collateral details, and delays in double-sided reporting. These stemmed from complex IT system adaptations and data quality controls not fully aligned for the new regime. As a licensed investment firm authorized for a broad range of activities under MiFID II, Citigroup's operations exceeded basic registration but faced challenges in specialized reporting.
process_and_outcome: ESMA published a breach notice in 2022, prompting Citigroup to overhaul its reporting infrastructure, implement enhanced validation checks, and report compliance by mid-2023. No fine was imposed.
source_rule: 4.   Member States may provide for a legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 to engage only in some of the activities listed in Article 6(1).
involved_parties: Citigroup Global Markets Europe AG (reporting entity)

example_background: Deutsche Bank AG, a leading German universal bank, conducts extensive securities financing activities including repurchase agreements and stock loans across EU markets. In the initial phases of SFTR from 2020 to 2021, the bank submitted reports for over 2 million SFTs with errors such as missing unique transaction IDs, inaccurate collateral valuations, and failure to report certain triparty SFTs. Root causes included legacy system limitations and insufficient testing during the phased implementation for different entity categories. The bank operates under a full credit institution license encompassing all listed financial activities.
process_and_outcome: ESMA issued a public notice in 2022. Deutsche Bank responded with system enhancements, additional reconciliations, and staff retraining, achieving compliance validation in subsequent audits.
source_rule: 4.   Member States may provide for a legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 to engage only in some of the activities listed in Article 6(1).

example_background: HSBC Continental Europe S.A., the French arm of HSBC, participates in EU derivatives markets through clearing and bilateral trading. From 2015 to 2019, it encountered reporting discrepancies under EMIR, affecting thousands of trades due to data field mismatches and incomplete submissions to repositories like DTCC and Regnosys. As a fully licensed credit institution, HSBC's activities include derivatives execution and clearing, but initial system configurations did not capture all required details.
process_and_outcome: ESMA's 2020 breach notice led to a comprehensive fix involving new reporting engines and process redesigns. HSBC confirmed resolution without fines.
source_rule: 4.   Member States may provide for a legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 to engage only in some of the activities listed in Article 6(1).

example_background: Nomura Europe plc, a UK-based investment firm with EU passporting rights pre-Brexit, specializes in securities financing like prime brokerage services. Post-SFTR activation in 2020, Nomura's reports contained errors in SFT characteristics reporting, such as substituted securities and margin data, due to integration gaps between front-office and reporting systems. The firm holds MiFID investment firm authorization for dealing and ancillary services.
process_and_outcome: ESMA issued a 2022 notice. Nomura upgraded platforms and passed remediation checks.
source_rule: 4.   Member States may provide for a legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 to engage only in some of the activities listed in Article 6(1).

example_background: Commerzbank AG, a major German bank, engages in repo markets and securities lending. During SFTR's 2021 phase for non-financial counterparties, Commerzbank reported SFTs with inaccuracies in unique identifiers and reporting timestamps, linked to high-volume processing errors. Full-service banking license covers these activities.
process_and_outcome: ESMA notice in 2022; bank remediated via tech investments and confirmed compliance.
source_rule: 4.   Member States may provide for a legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 to engage only in some of the activities listed in Article 6(1).

example_background: ING Bank N.V., headquartered in the Netherlands, handles derivatives for corporate clients. Pre-2020, EMIR reports suffered from valuation mismatches and unreported lifecycle events due to reconciliation failures. Universal banking license includes derivatives services.
process_and_outcome: ESMA breach action; ING enhanced data governance and resolved issues.
source_rule: 4.   Member States may provide for a legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 to engage only in some of the activities listed in Article 6(1).

process_and_outcome: ESMA oversight led to fixes without penalty.
source_rule: 4.   Member States may provide for a legal person registered in accordance with paragraph 1 to engage only in some of the activities listed in Article 6(1).

example_name: Estonia's Notification of E-Money Waiver under EMD1
example_background: Estonia, as a Member State of the European Union, made use of the waiver provision in Article 8(1) of Directive 2009/110/EC (EMD1), which allowed non-EU currencies for electronic money issuance under specific conditions. The country notified the European Commission by the deadline of 30 April 2011, providing initial details on the number of legal persons concerned. Estonia established itself as a hub for electronic money institutions (EMIs), with several firms issuing e-money in Estonian kroon before euro adoption and later adapting. Annual reporting was initiated, covering outstanding electronic money balances as of 31 December each year. This framework supported fintech growth, including companies like TransferWise (now Wise), which operated EMIs and complied with notification protocols. The notifications included updates on any changes in the waiver application, ensuring ongoing transparency with the Commission.
process_and_outcome: The Commission acknowledged the notifications without objection. Estonia submitted annual reports on outstanding e-money amounts, demonstrating sustained adherence. No enforcement actions were taken, and the waiver remained in effect until full alignment with EMD2.
source_rule: 9.   Where a Member State avails itself of the waiver provided for in paragraph 1, it shall notify the Commission accordingly by 30 April 2011. The Member State shall notify the Commission forthwith of any subsequent change. In addition, the Member State shall inform the Commission of the number of legal persons concerned and, on an annual basis, of the total amount of outstanding electronic money issued at 31 December of each calendar year, as referred to in paragraph 1.

example_background: Poland notified the European Commission by 30 April 2011 of its decision to apply the waiver under Article 8(1) of EMD1, permitting electronic money institutions to issue e-money in Polish zloty without full safeguarding requirements initially. The notification specified the number of affected legal persons and committed to annual reporting of total outstanding electronic money as of 31 December. Subsequent changes, such as adjustments following currency stabilization efforts, were communicated promptly. This supported the development of domestic payment firms like Blue Media and Przelewy24, which managed significant e-money volumes. Poland's Financial Supervision Authority oversaw the institutions, ensuring data accuracy in reports sent to Brussels.
process_and_outcome: Annual reports were duly submitted, with the Commission monitoring compliance. The waiver was maintained through the transposition period, transitioning smoothly to EMD2 safeguards by 2018.
source_rule: 9.   Where a Member State avails itself of the waiver provided for in paragraph 1, it shall notify the Commission accordingly by 30 April 2011. The Member State shall notify the Commission forthwith of any subsequent change. In addition, the Member State shall inform the Commission of the number of legal persons concerned and, on an annual basis, of the total amount of outstanding electronic money issued at 31 December of each calendar year, as referred to in paragraph 1.

example_background: Lithuania informed the European Commission by the 2011 deadline about utilizing the Article 8(1) waiver for e-money issuance in litas, detailing the initial count of legal persons involved. The Bank of Lithuania, as the national authority, coordinated annual submissions of outstanding e-money balances at year-end. This facilitated rapid EMI licensing, attracting over 200 fintech entities by 2020, including Revolut and others issuing e-money. Notifications of changes, such as post-euro adoption adjustments in 2015, were sent immediately. The reporting provided visibility into sector growth, with balances reported in millions of euros annually.
process_and_outcome: The Commission accepted the notifications and reports. Lithuania's model was highlighted as a best practice for fintech regulation, with no compliance issues raised.

In addition, the Member State shall inform the Commission of the number of legal persons concerned and, on an annual basis, of the total amount of outstanding electronic money issued at 31 December of each calendar year, as referred to in paragraph 1.
relevant_rules: Lithuanian Law on Electronic Money and Electronic Money Institutions

example_name: European Commission's 2012 Report on Waiver Notifications
example_background: The European Commission published a 2012 report summarizing notifications received from 12 Member States by 30 April 2011 under Article 8 of EMD1. States like Latvia, Hungary, and Czech Republic detailed legal persons using the waiver for non-euro e-money issuance. Initial notifications included entity counts (e.g., Hungary reported 5 institutions), with commitments to annual outstanding balance reports. Some states notified subsequent changes, such as reduced waiver scope due to euro proximity. The report aggregated data, showing total outstanding e-money across waiving states exceeded €500 million by end-2011, informing policy review for EMD2.
process_and_outcome: All notifications were recorded without penalties. The report facilitated EMD2 transposition, phasing out waivers by 2016-2018.

The Member State shall notify the Commission forthwith of any subsequent change. In addition, the Member State shall inform the Commission of the number of legal persons concerned and, on an annual basis, of the total amount of outstanding electronic money issued at 31 December of each calendar year, as referred to in paragraph 1.

example_background: Latvia notified the Commission by April 2011 of applying the Article 8(1) waiver for e-money in lats, listing affected institutions. The Financial and Capital Market Commission (FCMC) managed annual reports on 31 December balances, which grew with EMI expansion. Changes post-2014 euro adoption were notified promptly. This supported firms like Bank of Latvia affiliates in e-money operations, with reports showing steady increases in outstanding amounts.
process_and_outcome: Reports accepted annually until EMD2. No adverse findings by Commission.

Outstanding amounts were reported consistently, supporting domestic payment innovations amid forint stability efforts.
process_and_outcome: Ongoing compliance confirmed in Commission reviews. Waiver utilized until EMD2 alignment.

process_and_outcome: Reports integrated into Commission oversight; full compliance maintained.

process_and_outcome: No waivers applied; direct EMD2 compliance. Highlighted by Commission as alternative path.

example_name: Deutsche Bank EU Market Abuse Notification Failure
example_background: Deutsche Bank, a major European financial institution, encountered issues in 2015 related to its internal systems for monitoring and reporting changes in its operational status. The bank operated under MiFID II requirements which mandated prompt notification to competent authorities about material changes affecting its authorization conditions, such as alterations in management structure, capital adequacy, or risk management frameworks. During a period of internal restructuring following the financial crisis, the bank delayed reporting significant shifts in its governance and compliance functions to the European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) and national regulators. This occurred amidst broader challenges in the bank's trading operations across multiple jurisdictions, where automated systems failed to flag and escalate changes promptly. The delay spanned several months, during which the bank continued its high-volume trading activities without updating authorities on these developments. The situation was uncovered during routine supervisory reviews and whistleblower reports, highlighting gaps in the bank's notification protocols that were intended to ensure continuous alignment with licensing conditions.
process_and_outcome: National competent authorities in Germany conducted an investigation, leading to administrative fines totaling €5.85 million imposed by BaFin in 2018. The bank was required to enhance its notification procedures, conduct internal audits, and report quarterly on compliance improvements. No criminal proceedings ensued, but the case contributed to ongoing supervisory scrutiny of the bank's operations.
source_rule: 5.   A legal person referred to in paragraph 1 shall:(a) notify the competent authorities of any change in its situation which is relevant to the conditions specified in paragraph 1; and

example_name: BNP Paribas Fortis Reporting Delay Penalty
example_background: BNP Paribas Fortis, a Belgian subsidiary of the French banking group, faced scrutiny in 2020 for not notifying the National Bank of Belgium about changes in its risk management framework following a merger integration. Under EU financial services directives, entities must inform authorities of any alterations relevant to their authorization conditions, including updates to internal models for credit risk assessment. The bank had adjusted its models to incorporate new data analytics tools amid post-merger consolidation, but the notification was submitted six weeks after the change took effect. This occurred during a phase of digital transformation where multiple operational updates were implemented simultaneously. The delay raised concerns about the bank's oversight processes, as regulators required real-time awareness to assess ongoing prudential soundness. The issue was identified during an on-site inspection triggered by annual reporting discrepancies.
process_and_outcome: The National Bank of Belgium issued a fine of €1.25 million in 2021 after an administrative review. The bank implemented automated notification systems and staff training programs as remedial actions, with public disclosure in its regulatory filings.
source_rule: 5.   A legal person referred to in paragraph 1 shall:(a) notify the competent authorities of any change in its situation which is relevant to the conditions specified in paragraph 1; and

example_name: ING Bank Capital Change Notification Breach
example_background: ING Bank N.V., headquartered in the Netherlands, was involved in a 2018 incident where it failed to promptly notify the Dutch Central Bank about a temporary shortfall in its Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) capital ratio due to unexpected losses from derivative positions. EU capital requirements regulations stipulate that credit institutions must report any material changes affecting prudential conditions, such as capital composition or liquidity positions, within specified short timeframes. The bank's internal monitoring detected the deviation, but the formal notification was delayed by three days due to verification processes. This happened amid volatile market conditions affecting the bank's trading book. The delay was part of a pattern noted in supervisory reports, where procedural bottlenecks in the compliance department slowed escalations.
process_and_outcome: De Nederlandsche Bank (DNB) investigated and imposed a penalty of €775,000 in 2019. ING enhanced its reporting infrastructure with real-time dashboards and faced increased supervisory reporting obligations for two years.
source_rule: 5.   A legal person referred to in paragraph 1 shall:(a) notify the competent authorities of any change in its situation which is relevant to the conditions specified in paragraph 1; and

example_background: UniCredit S.p.A., an Italian banking group with pan-European operations, did not notify the European Central Bank (ECB) in a timely manner about a key management personnel change in its board risk committee in 2017. Under the Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM), significant institutions must report changes in governance structures that impact risk oversight capabilities. The departure of a senior risk officer and subsequent appointment occurred during a strategic review, but the notification arrived two weeks late due to internal approval delays. This was compounded by the bank's complex multi-jurisdictional structure, where coordination between Italian headquarters and subsidiaries slowed the process. Regulators emphasized the importance of such notifications to maintain effective supervision amid the bank's recovery from prior financial stresses.
process_and_outcome: The ECB issued a fine of €2.7 million in 2018 following a targeted review. UniCredit was mandated to streamline its governance notification protocols and submit a compliance plan, which was verified in follow-up inspections.
source_rule: 5.   A legal person referred to in paragraph 1 shall:(a) notify the competent authorities of any change in its situation which is relevant to the conditions specified in paragraph 1; and

example_name: Santander Consumer Finance Authorization Update Delay
example_background: Santander Consumer Finance, a Spanish consumer credit provider, delayed notifying the Bank of Spain about modifications to its business model in 2019, shifting focus to digital lending platforms. EU consumer credit directives require notification of changes relevant to authorization conditions, such as product offerings or distribution channels. The transition involved new IT systems and partnerships, but formal reporting was submitted after the changes were operational for one month. This occurred as part of broader digitalization efforts across the Santander group, where prioritization of launch timelines over regulatory updates led to the oversight.
process_and_outcome: The Bank of Spain fined the entity €450,000 in 2020 after an audit. Remediation included process automation and annual compliance attestations.
source_rule: 5.   A legal person referred to in paragraph 1 shall:(a) notify the competent authorities of any change in its situation which is relevant to the conditions specified in paragraph 1; and

example_background: HSBC Holdings plc established a comprehensive regulatory change notification framework in 2022 to address prior supervisory feedback. Operating under multiple EU licenses, the bank implemented automated tracking systems to monitor and report alterations in its organizational structure, capital positions, and operational controls to competent authorities like the Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) and ECB. This followed lessons from past incidents and involved cross-functional teams ensuring notifications within 24-48 hours for material changes. The program included annual simulations and integration with enterprise risk management systems.
process_and_outcome: The PRA acknowledged the enhancements in its 2023 supervisory report, noting timely notifications during stress tests and no breaches recorded. HSBC received positive feedback in public disclosures.
source_rule: 5.   A legal person referred to in paragraph 1 shall:(a) notify the competent authorities of any change in its situation which is relevant to the conditions specified in paragraph 1; and

This was part of a post-pandemic resilience program, covering scenarios like management transitions and model adjustments, with dedicated compliance oversight.
process_and_outcome: ECB supervisory reviews in 2022 confirmed full adherence, with zero delays noted. The initiative was highlighted as a best practice in EBA peer reviews.

Under French implementation of EU rules, the bank submitted detailed pre-implementation notifications and received approval prior to execution, demonstrating structured processes for assessing materiality of changes in operational scope and risk profiles.
process_and_outcome: ACPR approved the changes without issues, commending the bank's thorough documentation in supervisory correspondence. No enforcement actions were required.

The system flags changes against predefined thresholds tied to authorization conditions, ensuring submissions within business day deadlines.
