example_background: Paysafecard, an electronic money issuer operating prepaid voucher services across Europe, maintained average outstanding electronic money balances as part of its operations under EU e-money licensing. The company processed millions in transactions annually through its network of distribution points and online platforms. During routine supervisory reviews, authorities examined the firm's record-keeping and reporting mechanisms for outstanding e-money balances, which represent funds loaded onto vouchers but not yet redeemed. Paysafecard had systems in place to track these balances in real-time, but discrepancies emerged in how data was aggregated and formatted for submission. The firm relied on third-party processors for transaction data, leading to delays in reconciliation processes. Over a two-year period, submissions were prepared but not always transmitted on the exact dates mandated by national authorities, with some reports delayed by up to 45 days due to internal approval workflows and IT integration issues. This occurred amid growing scrutiny on e-money firms to ensure transparency in safeguarding customer funds equivalent to outstanding balances. Paysafecard held an e-money license from the UK's Financial Conduct Authority and equivalents in other member states, subjecting it to harmonized EU reporting obligations.
process_and_outcome: The Financial Conduct Authority conducted an investigation following data validation checks, identifying three instances of late reporting between 2018 and 2020. Paysafecard was issued a formal warning and required to pay a financial penalty of £100,000. The company enhanced its reporting automation and underwent an independent audit, submitting corrected historical data and committing to quarterly compliance attestations.
source_rule: (b) at least annually, on date specified by the competent authorities, report on the average outstanding electronic money.
relevant_rules: EBA Guidelines on reporting for e-money institutions

example_background: Revolut, a UK-based fintech holding an e-money license, manages significant volumes of electronic money through its app-based accounts serving over 20 million customers. Outstanding electronic money balances fluctuate daily due to user top-ups, spending, and transfers. The firm implemented dedicated compliance teams to monitor and calculate average outstanding balances monthly, with annual aggregation aligned to FCA-specified dates. In preparation for its 2021 annual report, Revolut conducted internal simulations of reporting processes, integrating data from core banking systems and reconciliation tools. This followed earlier supervisory engagements where regulators emphasized timely and accurate submissions to support macroprudential oversight. Revolut's balances are safeguarded in segregated accounts, and reporting ensures authorities can verify adequacy. The process involved cross-verification with external auditors and submission via standardized templates 30 days prior to the deadline.
process_and_outcome: FCA review confirmed full adherence, with no discrepancies noted. Revolut received supervisory commendation in its annual assessment, avoiding any enforcement action and qualifying for streamlined reporting in subsequent years.
source_rule: (b) at least annually, on date specified by the competent authorities, report on the average outstanding electronic money.
relevant_rules: FCA SUP 16 reporting rules for e-money

example_background: Wirecard, a German payment processor with e-money licenses in multiple EU states, handled billions in electronic money transactions through its cards and wallets. Outstanding balances were a key metric, representing unredeemed funds in client accounts. The company used complex trust structures in Asia for balance management, complicating accurate average calculations. Annual reporting to BaFin required submission by March 31 each year, detailing prior-year averages. Internal audits from 2017-2019 revealed underreporting of balances by up to 20% due to unrecorded third-party liabilities and valuation errors in foreign subsidiaries. This tied into broader accounting issues where balances were allegedly inflated or hidden. Wirecard's compliance function struggled with data consolidation across 14 countries, leading to inconsistent methodologies for averaging outstanding amounts.
process_and_outcome: BaFin investigations uncovered reporting inaccuracies alongside fraud. Wirecard faced €13 million in fines for supervisory violations, filed for insolvency in 2020, and executives were prosecuted. Regulators mandated enhanced reporting for surviving entities.
source_rule: (b) at least annually, on date specified by the competent authorities, report on the average outstanding electronic money.

example_background: N26, a German mobile bank with an e-money institution license, reports average outstanding electronic money balances annually to BaFin on the stipulated date of April 15. Serving 7 million EU customers, N26 tracks balances via real-time ledger systems, computing monthly averages rolled up yearly. In 2022, the firm automated reporting through API integrations with regulatory portals, ensuring data accuracy to the euro. This practice emerged after early licensing conditions emphasized robust reporting. N26's compliance program includes daily balance reconciliations and stress-testing of reporting outputs, with board-level oversight. Submissions include detailed breakdowns by currency and jurisdiction, exceeding basic requirements.
process_and_outcome: BaFin validated the 2022 submission without issues, noting it as exemplary. N26 was granted approval for banking license expansion, partly crediting strong e-money compliance.
source_rule: (b) at least annually, on date specified by the competent authorities, report on the average outstanding electronic money.

example_background: EcoPayz, an Austrian e-money firm offering wallets and prepaid cards, managed €500 million in outstanding electronic money annually. Regulators required annual reporting of average balances by June 30 to the FMA. Between 2016 and 2018, EcoPayz submitted reports late or with incomplete data due to manual processes and staff turnover in compliance. Balances were calculated using end-of-month snapshots rather than true daily averages, understating figures by 5-10%. This coincided with AML control weaknesses, where unreported balances masked suspicious transaction patterns. The firm expanded rapidly across Europe, straining its reporting infrastructure.
process_and_outcome: FMA imposed a €450,000 fine after inspection and ordered system upgrades. EcoPayz remediated by hiring external consultants and filing amended reports for prior years.
source_rule: (b) at least annually, on date specified by the competent authorities, report on the average outstanding electronic money.

example_background: Skrill (Paysafe), a global e-money issuer with EU licenses, reports average outstanding electronic money balances annually to the FCA and other authorities on November 15. Handling high-volume forex and gaming payments, Skrill maintains precise tracking via Oracle-based systems, averaging daily outstanding amounts over 365 days. In 2023, the firm conducted mock submissions and peer reviews, ensuring compliance amid regulatory changes post-Brexit. Reporting includes sensitivity analyses on balance fluctuations and safeguarding confirmations, demonstrating proactive alignment with EU standards.
process_and_outcome: FCA thematic review praised Skrill's processes, with no findings. The firm continued operations without restrictions.
source_rule: (b) at least annually, on date specified by the competent authorities, report on the average outstanding electronic money.

example_background: iDEAL, operated by Currence under Dutch e-money authorization, facilitates bank transfers with associated outstanding balances. DNB mandates annual reporting by May 1. In 2019, incomplete data submission occurred due to integration failures with member bank feeds, affecting average balance accuracy for €2 billion in processed volume. The system relied on batch processing, causing delays in finalizing year-end averages.
process_and_outcome: De Nederlandsche Bank issued a €100,000 penalty and remediation plan. iDEAL upgraded to real-time reporting, passing follow-up audits.
source_rule: (b) at least annually, on date specified by the competent authorities, report on the average outstanding electronic money.

example_background: Monzo, holding a UK e-money license alongside banking aspirations, computes and reports average outstanding balances annually on FCA's February 28 deadline. With 5 million users, Monzo uses machine learning for balance predictions and daily averaging. Its 2022 report was submitted early with full audit trails, reflecting investments in compliance tech post-launch.
process_and_outcome: FCA approved without comment, supporting Monzo's full banking license.
source_rule: (b) at least annually, on date specified by the competent authorities, report on the average outstanding electronic money.

example_background: PayPal's Luxembourg e-money entity reports average outstanding balances annually to CSSF by April 30. In 2017-2018, submissions contained calculation errors from legacy systems, overstating averages by 3% due to inclusion of non-e-money liabilities. This affected €10 billion in balances amid rapid growth.
process_and_outcome: CSSF fined €600,000 following examination, requiring process recertification.
source_rule: (b) at least annually, on date specified by the competent authorities, report on the average outstanding electronic money.
involved_parties: Commission de Surveillance du Secteur Financier (CSSF, regulator)

example_background: Swedbank's operations in Sweden, Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania handled €40 billion in suspicious payments from 2007 to 2015, linked to the same networks involved in the Danske Bank case. Transactions involved high-risk non-resident clients, shell companies, and mirrored suspicious flows across borders. The bank's transaction monitoring systems did not flag or investigate anomalies adequately, and customer risk assessments were outdated or incomplete. Whistleblower reports and media investigations in 2019 brought the issues to light, revealing persistent control weaknesses despite regulatory warnings.
process_and_outcome: Swedish and Baltic regulators imposed fines totaling over SEK 4 billion ($380 million) in 2020. Swedbank overhauled its board, paid $225 million to U.S. authorities, and faced class-action lawsuits.
source_rule: 8.   This Article shall not apply in respect of the provisions of Directive 2005/60/EC or national anti-money-laundering provisions.

example_background: HSBC Private Bank (Suisse) SA maintained relationships with politically exposed persons (PEPs) and high-risk clients without adequate due diligence from 2000 to 2015. The bank processed multimillion-euro deposits from sources including sanctioned entities and failed to monitor ongoing transactions or update risk profiles. Specific cases involved funds from African PEPs and Eastern European clients routed through complex structures. Internal compliance functions were under-resourced, and training was insufficient, leading to repeated oversights.
process_and_outcome: Swiss FINMA ordered a CHF 35 million fine and disgorgement of profits in 2019, along with a public reprimand and remediation requirements. HSBC enhanced its global AML program.
source_rule: 8.   This Article shall not apply in respect of the provisions of Directive 2005/60/EC or national anti-money-laundering provisions.

example_background: Standard Chartered Bank failed to maintain adequate AML controls across its global network, particularly in Hong Kong and UAE branches, allowing $1.1 trillion in transactions to pass without proper screening between 2009 and 2017. Issues included deficient customer onboarding, transaction monitoring alerts dismissed without review, and failures to report suspicious activities promptly. The bank's systems did not effectively identify high-risk clients or jurisdictions, contributing to prolonged exposure.
process_and_outcome: U.S. FinCEN and NYDFS imposed $1.1 billion in penalties in 2019. The bank invested $2 billion in compliance fixes and faced ongoing monitoring.
source_rule: 8.   This Article shall not apply in respect of the provisions of Directive 2005/60/EC or national anti-money-laundering provisions.

example_name: BNP Paribas Sanctions and AML Issues
example_background: BNP Paribas processed over $8.8 billion in transactions for Sudanese, Iranian, and other sanctioned entities between 2002 and 2012, bypassing U.S. sanctions through deceptive practices. While primarily a sanctions case, it involved parallel AML shortcomings, such as inadequate due diligence on intermediary banks and failure to scrutinize high-value cross-border payments. The bank's compliance team overlooked red flags in payment instructions.
process_and_outcome: U.S. authorities imposed a record $8.9 billion penalty in 2014, including guilty pleas. BNP enhanced its compliance infrastructure globally.
source_rule: 8.   This Article shall not apply in respect of the provisions of Directive 2005/60/EC or national anti-money-laundering provisions.

example_name: Credit Suisse Panama Papers Related AML Probe
example_background: Credit Suisse faced scrutiny following the 2016 Panama Papers leak, which revealed the bank's role in setting up over 1,000 offshore entities for clients between 2007 and 2015. The bank conducted insufficient due diligence on beneficial owners, accepted funds from unknown sources, and did not monitor post-onboarding activity effectively. Cases involved tax evasion risks and potential laundering through trusts and foundations in Panama and other jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: Swiss FINMA fined Credit Suisse CHF 13 million in 2018 for AML breaches. The bank paid additional settlements and reformed its private banking practices.
source_rule: 8.   This Article shall not apply in respect of the provisions of Directive 2005/60/EC or national anti-money-laundering provisions.

example_background: German regulator BaFin identified serious AML shortcomings at Commerzbank in 2019, including inadequate transaction monitoring, poor customer risk classification, and delays in filing suspicious transaction reports. Over several years, the bank's systems generated millions of unaddressed alerts, with particular issues in correspondent banking and high-risk client segments. Corrective actions were initiated but deemed insufficient by regulators.
process_and_outcome: BaFin imposed a €1.5 million fine and required a comprehensive remediation plan with independent audits. Commerzbank invested hundreds of millions in system upgrades.
source_rule: 8.   This Article shall not apply in respect of the provisions of Directive 2005/60/EC or national anti-money-laundering provisions.

example_name: UK FCA Enforcement against EML Payments UK Ltd
example_background: EML Payments UK Ltd, an electronic money institution authorized under the UK's Electronic Money Regulations, underwent a significant change in its ownership structure in 2021 when Quadrant Private Equity acquired a majority stake. This change altered the control framework and required re-assessment of the firm's compliance with authorization conditions, including prudential requirements and ongoing suitability of controllers. The firm initially notified the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) of the change but proceeded with operations without promptly obtaining updated approvals or confirmations that all original authorization conditions under Article 3 equivalents continued to be met. Over a period of several months, EML continued issuing electronic money through its programs while internal audits revealed gaps in safeguarding client funds and risk management controls. The situation escalated when the FCA conducted a supervisory review in early 2022, identifying that the post-change conditions no longer fully aligned with required standards, prompting a need for formal re-authorization or variation. EML's delay in seeking this within the prescribed timeframe led to operational restrictions.
process_and_outcome: The FCA issued a Final Notice in 2023 prohibiting EML from issuing new electronic money and requiring a full application for continued authorization. The firm was ordered to wind down unauthorized activities, implement enhanced compliance measures, and faced ongoing supervision. No monetary penalty was imposed at that stage, but restrictions remained until compliance was restored.
source_rule: 6.   Member States shall take the necessary steps to ensure that where the conditions set out in paragraphs 1, 2 and 4 are no longer met, the legal person concerned shall seek authorisation within 30 calendar days in accordance with Article 3. Any such person that has not sought authorisation within that period shall be prohibited, in accordance with Article 10, from issuing electronic money.
involved_parties: EML Payments UK Ltd (electronic money institution)
relevant_rules: FCA Handbook on Authorisation and Supervision

example_name: Central Bank of Ireland Action against WebBanking E-Money Firm
example_background: An Ireland-based electronic money institution experienced a merger with a non-EU entity in 2020, which resulted in changes to its governance and capital structure. The merger meant that key conditions for its existing authorization, such as maintaining sufficient own funds and effective risk controls as per Article 3 of the relevant directive, were impacted. The firm continued issuing prepaid cards and electronic money transfer services for over 45 days post-merger without submitting a formal application for authorization update or variation. During this period, it processed transactions worth millions of euros, with internal reports later showing temporary shortfalls in safeguarding arrangements. The Central Bank of Ireland initiated a thematic review of e-money firms in mid-2021, which flagged the case due to the prolonged operation without updated permissions.
process_and_outcome: The regulator imposed a €150,000 administrative sanction, required cessation of new issuances until authorization was granted, and mandated a remediation plan including independent audits. The firm eventually obtained approval after 90 days and enhanced its compliance framework.

example_name: FCA Ban on P27 Digital EMI for Controller Change Failure
example_background: P27 Digital Limited, holding EMI authorization in the UK, saw a change in its ultimate beneficial owners in late 2022, which affected the suitability criteria originally assessed under its authorization. The firm notified the FCA but did not seek formal approval or re-authorization within the required period after conditions ceased to be met, continuing to issue electronic money for corporate payment solutions. This occurred amid broader market pressures on fintechs, and internal compliance reviews indicated deviations in ongoing prudential safeguards. The FCA's intervention followed a routine controller notification review process.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the FCA prohibited P27 Digital from issuing electronic money under Article 10 equivalents, required customer fund transfers to authorized entities, and imposed supervisory conditions. The firm ceased operations and entered administration.

example_name: Deutsche Bank PPI Mis-selling Remediation Compliance
example_background: Deutsche Bank AG in the UK, operating under e-money permissions for certain payment products, faced changes in its internal control framework following a 2019 group-wide restructuring. This impacted conditions related to consumer protection and fund safeguarding. The bank proactively engaged with regulators within 30 days of identifying the shifts, submitting detailed applications to maintain authorization status while implementing remediation for past payment protection insurance (PPI) issues intertwined with e-money products.
process_and_outcome: The FCA approved the updated authorization without interruption, noting the timely submission and robust remediation plans. The bank paid over £1 billion in PPI redress but maintained e-money operations seamlessly.

example_name: Revolut Ltd Timely Authorisation Renewal Post-Growth Changes
example_background: Revolut, a prominent UK and EU electronic money institution, experienced rapid scaling and changes in its business model in 2021-2022, including shifts in risk exposure and controller interests that affected original authorization conditions. Upon internal detection that certain prudential and operational conditions under Article 3 were strained, the firm promptly filed for authorization variation within 25 days, detailing enhanced safeguarding and AML controls while continuing limited operations under supervision.
process_and_outcome: The FCA granted the variation with additional conditions, allowing uninterrupted service. This was part of Revolut's successful transition to full banking license application.

example_name: Dutch AFM Fine on Adyen for Delayed Authorisation Update
example_background: Adyen N.V., a Dutch payment institution with e-money services, underwent an acquisition of a subsidiary in 2020 that changed its group structure and risk profile. This meant original authorization conditions for e-money issuance, including capital adequacy, were no longer fully satisfied. Adyen notified the Dutch Authority for the Financial Markets (AFM) but delayed the formal re-authorization request beyond 35 days, continuing to issue e-money across Europe during this time.
process_and_outcome: AFM imposed a €100,000 fine in 2022, required immediate application submission, and enhanced reporting. Adyen complied subsequently without further sanctions.

This altered conditions like geographical scope and fund safeguarding linkages. PayPal identified the impacts early and sought authorization updates from the Central Bank of Ireland within 20 days, implementing parallel UK permissions.
process_and_outcome: Authorizations were granted without disruption, enabling continued e-money issuance across jurisdictions with affirmed compliance.

process_and_outcome: BaFin issued a public warning and temporary prohibition in 2023, followed by a €50,000 fine. N26 rectified and resumed after approval.

example_name: Wise (TransferWise) Proactive Authorisation Amid Ownership Shift
example_background: Wise Payments Limited, UK EMI, navigated a 2021 IPO preparation that changed controller dynamics and business scale, impacting authorization conditions. The company submitted preemptive applications to FCA within 15 days of change identification, bolstering compliance programs.
process_and_outcome: FCA approved variations promptly, supporting Wise's growth to multi-jurisdictional banking aspirations without penalties.

example_background: From 2013 to 2016, Credit Suisse arranged $2 billion in loans disguised as investments in tuna fishing and security services for Mozambique government entities. The funds were used for undisclosed purposes, with bribes paid to officials. The bank failed to perform proper due diligence on the transactions, ignoring red flags like inflated project costs and lack of transparency. Compliance monitoring did not flag the off-balance-sheet loans or related derivatives structured to hide risks.
process_and_outcome: Swiss regulator FINMA investigated in 2019, finding serious breaches. The US SEC and DOJ imposed $475 million in penalties in 2021. Credit Suisse paid over $1 billion total, exited emerging markets financing, and strengthened client onboarding controls.
source_rule: 7.   Member States shall ensure that their competent authorities are sufficiently empowered to verify continued compliance with the requirements laid down in this Article.

example_background: Between 2007 and 2015, Danske Bank's Estonian branch processed €200 billion in suspicious transactions through non-resident accounts, primarily from Russia, Azerbaijan, and Latvia. The branch had weak customer due diligence, allowing shell companies to move funds with minimal verification. IT systems failed to detect patterns, and compliance reports were ignored by management. Aggregator accounts funneled funds that bypassed standard monitoring.
process_and_outcome: Danish FSA and US DOJ investigated from 2017. In 2018, the bank reported the issue voluntarily. Fines exceeded €1 billion by 2022, including $2 billion to US authorities. Danske overhauled its AML framework and exited the non-resident portfolio.
source_rule: 7.   Member States shall ensure that their competent authorities are sufficiently empowered to verify continued compliance with the requirements laid down in this Article.

example_background: HSBC's Swiss private bank handled accounts for high-risk clients from 2000 to 2015 without adequate due diligence. It facilitated tax evasion by US clients through undeclared accounts and structures like bearer shares. Compliance functions did not monitor cross-border flows or verify source of funds, despite regulatory warnings. The bank relied on introducers without independent checks.
process_and_outcome: Swiss FINMA ordered a 2014 audit revealing issues, leading to a public warning. In 2021, HSBC Switzerland was fined CHF 30 million. The group enhanced global AML systems as part of broader US and UK settlements totaling $1.9 billion.
source_rule: 7.   Member States shall ensure that their competent authorities are sufficiently empowered to verify continued compliance with the requirements laid down in this Article.

example_background: Swedbank's Baltic branches processed €40 billion in suspicious transactions from 2007 to 2015, linked to the same networks as Danske. Transaction monitoring systems were outdated, missing high-velocity transfers between Estonia, Sweden, and Denmark. Compliance staff turnover and inadequate training contributed to failures in reporting suspicious activities to authorities.
process_and_outcome: Swedish FSA fined the bank SEK 4 billion in 2020 following investigations prompted by media reports. Estonian and Latvian regulators also imposed penalties. Swedbank invested €500 million in compliance upgrades.
source_rule: 7.   Member States shall ensure that their competent authorities are sufficiently empowered to verify continued compliance with the requirements laid down in this Article.

example_background: Standard Chartered processed $1 trillion in transactions from 2008 to 2013, including for sanctioned Iranian banks, without proper screening. The bank's global network mirrored high-risk payments, stripping identifying data. Compliance programs existed but were under-resourced, failing to adapt to changing sanctions lists.
process_and_outcome: New York DFS imposed a $300 million fine in 2019 for AML and sanctions failures. The bank paid additional penalties to FinCEN and enhanced monitoring tools.
source_rule: 7.   Member States shall ensure that their competent authorities are sufficiently empowered to verify continued compliance with the requirements laid down in this Article.
involved_parties: New York Department of Financial Services

example_background: Commerzbank lacked adequate systems to monitor trading activities for market abuse from 2012 to 2017. The firm relied on manual reviews instead of automated surveillance, missing manipulative behaviors in equity and bond markets across its German and international desks.
process_and_outcome: BaFin and FCA fined the bank €3.5 million in 2021 after joint inspections. Commerzbank implemented automated post-trade controls compliant with MiFID II requirements.
source_rule: 7.   Member States shall ensure that their competent authorities are sufficiently empowered to verify continued compliance with the requirements laid down in this Article.

example_background: Nordea's Danish bank division processed suspicious transactions worth DKK 150 billion from 2009 to 2018 without sufficient checks. Monitoring systems did not cover all account types, and suspicious activity reports were delayed or incomplete.
process_and_outcome: Danish FSA imposed a DKK 4.3 billion fine in 2022 following a multi-year probe. Nordea invested in AI-driven monitoring and staff training.
source_rule: 7.   Member States shall ensure that their competent authorities are sufficiently empowered to verify continued compliance with the requirements laid down in this Article.
relevant_rules: Danish Act on Measures to Prevent Money Laundering

example_background: Revolut Trading Ltd, a UK-based electronic money institution, faced scrutiny over its practices in issuing and safeguarding electronic money. Between 2016 and 2019, the firm held customer funds in a pooled account at a third-party bank without ensuring immediate issuance at par value upon receipt of funds. The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) identified that Revolut issued e-money tokens but failed to promptly convert received funds into electronic money at face value, leading to delays and discrepancies in customer balances. This occurred during a period of rapid growth for Revolut, as it expanded its customer base across Europe while transitioning from a payments institution to an e-money issuer. The issue stemmed from operational processes that did not align with safeguarding and issuance requirements, affecting a large number of retail customers who expected their deposits to be immediately represented as electronic money.
process_and_outcome: The FCA conducted an investigation and imposed a financial penalty of £167,164 on Revolut Trading Ltd in September 2020. The firm was required to appoint skilled persons to review its safeguarding arrangements and implement remedial measures to ensure future compliance. No customer compensation was ordered, as no direct losses were identified.
source_rule: Article 11Issuance and redeemability 1.   Member States shall ensure that electronic money issuers issue electronic money at par value on the receipt of funds.

example_background: Wirecard AG, through its UK subsidiary Wirecard Card Solutions Ltd, operated as an electronic money institution issuing payment cards across Europe. From 2015 to 2019, the firm received funds from customers for e-money issuance but did not always ensure that electronic money was issued at par value immediately upon receipt. Investigations revealed that customer funds were held in segregated accounts, but there were instances where the issuance process was delayed due to internal reconciliation issues, particularly during high-volume periods. This contributed to broader concerns about Wirecard's financial reporting and operational controls, which unraveled in a major accounting scandal affecting its entire group. The delays meant that customers' deposited funds were not instantaneously converted to e-money at face value, exposing them to risks during the interim period.
process_and_outcome: The FCA investigated and imposed a £1.098 million fine on Wirecard Card Solutions Ltd in April 2020 for failures in safeguarding and issuance processes. The parent company's collapse led to insolvency proceedings in Germany, with criminal investigations into fraud. The UK subsidiary entered administration.
source_rule: Article 11Issuance and redeemability 1.   Member States shall ensure that electronic money issuers issue electronic money at par value on the receipt of funds.

example_background: Paysafe Financial Services Ltd, an e-money issuer authorized in the UK, underwent a review of its issuance and redeemability processes. The FCA examined how the firm handled customer funds received for prepaid cards and digital wallets. In certain scenarios, particularly involving international fund transfers, there were processing delays that prevented immediate issuance of electronic money at par value. This was identified during routine supervisory visits between 2018 and 2020, where it was found that the firm's systems did not always trigger par value issuance upon fund confirmation, leading to temporary mismatches between customer deposits and e-money balances. Paysafe had grown through acquisitions and was servicing millions of transactions annually.
process_and_outcome: The FCA issued a Final Notice in 2021 imposing a penalty of £166,250. Paysafe was required to enhance its systems and conduct a past business review. The firm settled early, reducing the fine.
source_rule: Article 11Issuance and redeemability 1.   Member States shall ensure that electronic money issuers issue electronic money at par value on the receipt of funds.
relevant_rules: FCA Handbook (SYSC) on systems and controls

example_name: FCA Approval of N26 E-Money Operations
example_background: N26, a German neobank, sought and obtained authorization as an electronic money institution from the FCA for its UK operations in 2018. Upon receiving customer funds via bank transfers or card payments, N26 implemented automated processes to issue electronic money at par value instantly. The firm's technology platform ensured that upon fund receipt, e-money was credited to user accounts at face value without delay, complying with operational standards during its expansion phase. This was part of N26's broader compliance framework as it scaled across Europe under PSD2 and EMD2 frameworks.
process_and_outcome: The FCA authorized N26 Bank GmbH as an e-money institution following a thorough assessment, with no enforcement actions taken. Ongoing supervision confirmed adherence to issuance requirements through 2023.
source_rule: Article 11Issuance and redeemability 1.   Member States shall ensure that electronic money issuers issue electronic money at par value on the receipt of funds.

example_background: Monzo Bank Ltd, authorized as both a bank and e-money institution in the UK, processes customer deposits by issuing electronic money at par value upon receipt. Customers funding their accounts via Faster Payments or debit cards see immediate crediting at face value. Monzo's core banking system integrates fund confirmation with e-money issuance, ensuring no delays even during peak loads. This practice has been consistent since its e-money authorization in 2017, supporting its growth to millions of users.
process_and_outcome: FCA supervisory reviews from 2018-2022 affirmed compliance with no issues raised on issuance processes. Monzo received full banking license in 2019, building on its e-money compliance record.
source_rule: Article 11Issuance and redeemability 1.   Member States shall ensure that electronic money issuers issue electronic money at par value on the receipt of funds.

example_background: Starling Bank, a UK challenger bank with e-money permissions, receives funds from customers and issues electronic money at par value through real-time processing. Its payment rails ensure that upon settlement of incoming transfers, e-money is issued without intermediary holds. This has been operational since 2016, handling billions in transactions annually while maintaining precise balance equivalence.
process_and_outcome: FCA granted full banking authorization in 2019 after verifying e-money processes. No enforcement actions or findings related to issuance delays reported through 2023.
source_rule: Article 11Issuance and redeemability 1.   Member States shall ensure that electronic money issuers issue electronic money at par value on the receipt of funds.

example_background: EcoPayz UK Ltd, an e-money institution providing digital wallets, was reviewed for its handling of customer fund receipts. From 2017 to 2019, the firm experienced system limitations that occasionally delayed the issuance of electronic money at par value after funds were received, particularly for cross-border payments where settlement took additional time. This resulted in customer funds remaining as 'pending' before conversion to e-money, affecting transparency and risk exposure.
process_and_outcome: FCA fined EcoPayz £218,225 in 2020 and required system upgrades. The firm undertook a remediation program to align processes with requirements.
source_rule: Article 11Issuance and redeemability 1.   Member States shall ensure that electronic money issuers issue electronic money at par value on the receipt of funds.

example_background: PayPal (Europe) S.à r.l. et Cie, S.C.A., an Luxembourg-based e-money institution passporting services across the EU, issues electronic money at par value upon receipt of funds into user balances. The platform's infrastructure processes deposits from cards, bank transfers, and P2P instantly, crediting e-money at face value. This has been standard since its e-money authorization in 2007, serving hundreds of millions of users.
process_and_outcome: Central Bank of Ireland and other EU supervisors have conducted reviews with no material findings on issuance. PayPal maintains annual attestations confirming compliance.
source_rule: Article 11Issuance and redeemability 1.   Member States shall ensure that electronic money issuers issue electronic money at par value on the receipt of funds.

example_background: Skrill Ltd (formerly Moneybookers), a UK e-money issuer, managed customer funds for online wallets and payment services. FCA reviews from 2016-2018 found that the firm did not consistently issue e-money at par value immediately upon fund receipt due to manual reconciliation processes in its back-office systems, leading to delays of up to several days for certain transactions.
process_and_outcome: FCA imposed a £1.023 million penalty in 2019. Skrill invested in automated systems and completed a customer notification exercise.
source_rule: Article 11Issuance and redeemability 1.   Member States shall ensure that electronic money issuers issue electronic money at par value on the receipt of funds.

example_name: UK FCA Action Against eMoney Licensee for Unauthorized Activities
example_background: In 2022, the UK Financial Conduct Authority identified a firm authorized as an electronic money institution that engaged in activities beyond its permitted scope under the Electronic Money Regulations. The firm, which had been granted a limited e-money license, began offering additional payment services and holding client funds in ways not covered by its authorization. This occurred after the firm expanded its operations without seeking necessary approvals or variations to its license. The case highlighted ongoing monitoring challenges post-Brexit in the UK's e-money sector, where firms must strictly adhere to defined permissions. The firm's activities involved processing transactions for high-risk clients, leading to supervisory concerns over consumer protection and market integrity.
process_and_outcome: The FCA conducted a thematic review and imposed restrictions on the firm, requiring it to cease unauthorized activities. A final notice was issued with a financial penalty of £107,000 and additional remediation costs. The firm appealed but settled, implementing compliance enhancements.
source_rule: TITLE IIIISSUANCE AND REDEEMABILITY OF ELECTRONIC MONEYArticle 10Prohibition from issuing electronic money Without prejudice to Article 18, Member States shall prohibit natural or legal persons who are not electronic money issuers from issuing electronic money.
involved_parties: FCA-authorized e-money firm (unnamed in public notice)

example_name: Central Bank of Ireland Sanctions Non-Authorized Crypto E-Money Offering
example_background: A cryptocurrency platform operating in Ireland in 2021 advertised and distributed digital tokens presented as electronic money redeemable for fiat currency. The platform was not registered as an electronic money issuer with the Central Bank of Ireland nor did it hold an equivalent authorization under EU frameworks. The platform targeted retail investors with promises of stability and instant redemption, processing millions in transactions through unapproved channels. This case emerged during heightened scrutiny of crypto-assets mimicking e-money products amid the implementation of new EU rules.
process_and_outcome: The Central Bank issued a public statement and administrative sanctions, ordering cessation of activities and imposing fines totaling €200,000. The platform wound down Irish operations and faced cross-border coordination with other EU regulators.

The firm issues electronic money through its app-based accounts, ensuring all issuances are backed by segregated client funds and redeemable on demand. Throughout its growth to millions of users, Revolut maintained strict adherence to authorization requirements, undergoing regular audits and reporting to demonstrate operational integrity in handling e-money.
process_and_outcome: Revolut successfully renewed its licenses and expanded services under supervision, with no major enforcement actions related to issuance prohibitions. It served as a model for scalable e-money operations.

example_name: FCA Enforcement Against Unauthorized Fintech E-Money Issuance
example_background: A fintech startup in 2019 launched a prepaid card product backed by digital tokens it claimed qualified as electronic money. Operating without an e-money institution license, the company processed payments for UK consumers and held funds in non-segregated accounts. The product was marketed aggressively online, attracting thousands of users before regulatory detection during a routine market sweep.
process_and_outcome: The FCA intervened with an injunction, froze assets, and banned the directors from financial services. A £500,000 penalty was levied, and the firm entered liquidation.

example_background: German neobank N26 secured an electronic money institution license from BaFin in 2016, enabling it to issue e-money across the EU via passporting. The bank integrated e-money functionalities into its mobile app, ensuring all issuances complied with safeguarding and redemption rules while scaling to over 7 million customers by 2023. Regular supervisory reviews confirmed proper fund segregation and reporting.
process_and_outcome: N26 progressed to full banking license application, with e-money operations cited positively in regulatory assessments. No issuance-related sanctions were imposed.
source_rule: TITLE IIIISSUANCE AND REDEEMABILITY OF ELECTRONIC MONEYArticle 10Prohibition from issuing electronic money Without prejudice to Article 18, Member States shall prohibit natural or legal persons who are not electronic money issuers from issuing electronic money.

example_name: Dutch AFM Fine on Non-Licensed Payment Firm for E-Money
example_background: In 2020, the Dutch Authority for the Financial Markets (AFM) investigated a payment services provider that issued stored-value cards functioning as electronic money without the required EMI authorization. The firm had been operating since 2018, reloading cards with fiat equivalents for online purchases, handling €10 million in volume without prudential safeguards.
process_and_outcome: AFM imposed a €220,000 administrative fine and mandated cessation. The firm appealed unsuccessfully and restructured under supervision.

example_background: Wise, formerly TransferWise, operates as an electronic money institution licensed by the FCA since 2012 and passporting across Europe. It issues e-money for multi-currency accounts, maintaining 100% safeguarding of client balances in separate bank accounts and providing daily reconciliations. This approach supported its growth to 12 million customers while meeting redemption obligations.
process_and_outcome: FCA supervisory visits resulted in clean reports, with Wise recognized for robust compliance frameworks. It expanded services without issuance violations.

example_name: CySEC Enforcement Against Unlicensed Crypto E-Money in Cyprus
example_background: A Cyprus-based crypto exchange in 2022 offered stablecoins as electronic money redeemable 1:1 for euros without CySEC authorization as an EMI. The platform onboarded EU clients and processed high volumes, blurring lines between crypto and regulated e-money amid MiCA developments.
process_and_outcome: CySEC issued a €150,000 fine, client fund recovery orders, and a permanent ban on Cyprus operations. Cross-EU notifications followed.

example_name: Monzo E-Money to Banking License Transition Compliance
example_background: UK challenger bank Monzo started as an e-money institution under FCA authorization in 2015, issuing e-money for its current accounts. It adhered to EMI rules on fund protection and redeemability while pursuing a full banking license, demonstrating scalable compliance during rapid user growth to 8 million.
process_and_outcome: Monzo obtained full banking license in 2019, with e-money phase-out managed seamlessly under dual oversight, earning regulatory commendations.
source_rule: TITLE IIIISSUANCE AND REDEEMABILITY OF ELECTRONIC MONEYArticle 10Prohibition from issuing electronic money Without prejudice to Article 18, Member States shall prohibit natural or legal persons who are not electronic money issuers from issuing electronic money.

example_name: E-Money Ireland Redemption Delays (Central Bank of Ireland Enforcement)
example_background: E-Money Ireland Limited, an authorized electronic money institution in Ireland, faced issues in processing redemption requests from electronic money holders. Between 2019 and 2021, the firm experienced operational delays in redeeming funds at par value upon holder requests, attributed to inadequate liquidity management and system limitations. Customers reported waits of up to several weeks for redemptions, affecting hundreds of accounts with total values exceeding €500,000. The Central Bank of Ireland identified these shortcomings during routine supervisory reviews, noting that the institution failed to maintain sufficient safeguards to ensure immediate access to funds as expected under EU electronic money directives. This led to customer complaints filed with the Financial Services and Pensions Ombudsman, highlighting disruptions in access to monetary value held in e-money wallets.
process_and_outcome: The Central Bank conducted an investigation, issued a reprimand, and imposed an administrative fine of €105,000 in 2022. E-Money Ireland was required to implement enhanced liquidity controls, upgrade IT systems, and compensate affected customers with interest on delayed redemptions totaling €25,000.
source_rule: 2.   Member States shall ensure that, upon request by the electronic money holder, electronic money issuers redeem, at any moment and at par value, the monetary value of the electronic money held.

example_background: Paysafecard, a leading prepaid e-money voucher provider operating across multiple EU member states, established robust redemption processes for its electronic money products. Since obtaining e-money institution authorization in 2014, the company maintained dedicated customer service channels and automated systems allowing holders to redeem voucher balances at par value instantly via linked bank accounts or cash at partner locations. Annual audits confirmed 99.9% redemption success rates within 24 hours, with liquidity reserves held in segregated accounts exceeding outstanding e-money liabilities by 120%. This approach supported millions of transactions yearly without notable delays, serving as a model during industry consultations with the European Banking Authority.
process_and_outcome: Regulatory reviews by national authorities in Germany and the UK affirmed full adherence, resulting in no enforcement actions and public recognition in EBA reports on e-money supervision.
source_rule: 2.   Member States shall ensure that, upon request by the electronic money holder, electronic money issuers redeem, at any moment and at par value, the monetary value of the electronic money held.

example_background: Wirex Limited, a UK-based e-money institution offering crypto-linked e-money wallets, encountered challenges in 2020-2021 when a surge in redemption requests during market volatility overwhelmed its processing capabilities. Holders reported delays of 10-30 days for par value redemptions totaling over £10 million, due to integration issues between fiat and crypto ledgers and insufficient real-time liquidity monitoring. The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) received over 200 complaints, prompting a supervisory intervention. Wirex had safeguards but lacked stress-testing for high-volume scenarios, impacting trust in its multi-currency e-money products used by 1.5 million customers.
process_and_outcome: FCA investigation led to a voluntary variation of permission in 2021, requiring system upgrades and a £1.3 million redress scheme for affected customers. No fine was imposed but ongoing monitoring was mandated.
source_rule: 2.   Member States shall ensure that, upon request by the electronic money holder, electronic money issuers redeem, at any moment and at par value, the monetary value of the electronic money held.

example_name: N26 Bank E-Money Redemption Practices (BaFin Review)
example_background: N26, a German neobank holding an EU e-money license, implemented seamless redemption mechanisms for its electronic money accounts since 2016. Customers could request redemptions at par value via app instantly to SEPA transfers, with 100% completion within T+1 day. The firm maintained liquidity ratios above 150% of float, using real-time dashboards for monitoring. BaFin's 2022 thematic review praised N26's processes amid examinations of 50+ institutions, noting zero complaints on redemptions out of 500,000+ accounts.
process_and_outcome: Positive supervisory findings in BaFin report, contributing to N26's banking license upgrade in 2022 without remediation requirements.

Root causes included outdated batch processing and understaffed verification teams. The Bank of Lithuania noted non-compliance with prompt redemption obligations despite adequate overall liquidity.

example_background: Wells Fargo employees created millions of unauthorized accounts and credit cards to meet aggressive sales targets, leading to customers incurring fees for products they did not request or authorize. This practice involved opening deposit accounts, credit cards, and adding services like overdraft protection without customer consent, resulting in unexpected fees deducted from customer balances. The bank's internal sales incentive structure pressured staff to engage in these activities over several years, affecting a large number of retail banking customers across the United States.
process_and_outcome: The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB), Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC), and other regulators conducted investigations, leading to a $3 billion settlement in 2020. Wells Fargo was fined $1.95 billion by the CFPB and $1.1 billion by the OCC, clawed back executive compensation, and faced an asset cap limiting growth until compliance improvements were demonstrated.

example_background: Revolut Ltd, a UK electronic money institution, provided prepaid accounts and electronic money services to millions of customers under contracts without fixed termination dates, allowing redemption requests at any time. In 2019, the firm identified delays in processing customer redemption requests, with some taking over six months due to operational bottlenecks in verifying and transferring safeguarded funds. This affected customers seeking to withdraw balances before any potential contract end, exposing them to prolonged access restrictions. The firm's safeguarding arrangements involved ring-fenced accounts, but reconciliation processes failed to handle peak redemption volumes promptly, leading to a backlog of over 5,000 requests.
process_and_outcome: FCA investigation followed customer complaints, resulting in a £5.2 million fine in 2021 for failures in timely redemption processing and systems controls. Revolut remediated affected customers with compensation and upgraded its IT infrastructure for faster payouts.
source_rule: (a)where redemption is requested before the termination of the contract;(b) where the contract provides for a termination date and the electronic money holder terminates the contract before that date; or

example_background: Metro Bank, holding an electronic money institution authorization, offered current accounts functioning as e-money products with no fixed termination dates, permitting customer redemptions at will. From 2019 to 2022, the bank experienced governance and control lapses, including inadequate oversight of its e-money safeguarding and redemption processes. Customer funds were not always promptly available upon request due to integration issues between banking and e-money operations, and there were instances where redemption requests before operational 'termination' events were delayed amid the bank's broader financial distress.
process_and_outcome: FCA revoked Metro Bank's EMI permissions in June 2022 after supervisory review, citing unfit management and persistent breaches. The bank appealed but ceased EMI activities, focusing on core banking while remediating systems.
source_rule: (a)where redemption is requested before the termination of the contract;(b) where the contract provides for a termination date and the electronic money holder terminates the contract before that date; or

example_background: Wirecard, licensed as an electronic money institution in the UK via its subsidiary Wirecard Card Solutions, managed customer e-money balances under open-ended contracts allowing anytime redemption. By 2020, it emerged that €1.9 billion in purported trustee assets safeguarding e-money were fictitious, meaning customer funds were not properly segregated and at risk if redemption was requested prior to the firm's collapse. Contracts did not specify termination dates, but the firm's insolvency proceedings highlighted unprotected early withdrawals amid accounting fraud.
process_and_outcome: BaFin and FCA froze operations; UK customers' funds were ring-fenced via FSCS up to £85k. Wirecard filed for insolvency, with criminal probes ongoing; executives faced charges. EBA issued sector-wide warnings.
source_rule: (a)where redemption is requested before the termination of the contract;(b) where the contract provides for a termination date and the electronic money holder terminates the contract before that date; or

example_background: Starling Bank, a UK electronic money institution, maintains customer e-money accounts with flexible redemption options before any contract termination. The firm implemented robust daily reconciliations of segregated accounts holding customer funds equivalent to issued e-money, using independent trustee oversight. Even during high-volume redemption periods, such as account closures, funds were disbursed within T+1 days, demonstrating operational resilience tested in annual FCA audits.
process_and_outcome: FCA supervisory reviews in 2022-2023 confirmed full compliance, with no findings on redemption delays or safeguarding shortfalls. The bank published transparency reports affirming adherence.
source_rule: (a)where redemption is requested before the termination of the contract;(b) where the contract provides for a termination date and the electronic money holder terminates the contract before that date; or

Safeguarding pools exceed liabilities by design, with monthly attestations to regulators.
process_and_outcome: FCA innovation hub review praised processes in 2021, leading to expanded permissions without conditions related to redemptions or safeguarding.

example_background: Revolut, a UK-based fintech company operating as an electronic money institution, faced scrutiny over its practices related to customer fund redemptions following account closures or contract terminations. Between 2019 and 2021, Revolut applied fixed administrative fees for redemption requests processed after certain time periods, including instances exceeding one year from termination. Customers reported difficulties in accessing their safeguarded funds, with fees deducted that were not clearly tied to documented costs. The process involved automated systems charging standard amounts regardless of individual circumstances, leading to complaints aggregated through consumer protection channels. This occurred amid Revolut's rapid growth in the EU market, handling millions of e-money accounts under its EMI license.
process_and_outcome: The UK's Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) investigated following customer complaints and a thematic review of EMI redemption practices. In 2022, Revolut was fined £64,000 and ordered to refund affected customers approximately £250,000 in overcharged fees. The firm also committed to revising its fee structures and enhancing transparency in redemption policies.
source_rule: (c)where redemption is requested more than one year after the date of termination of the contract. Any such fee shall be proportionate and commensurate with the actual costs incurred by the electronic money issuer.

example_background: Paysafecard, a prepaid e-money product issuer operating across Europe, implemented policies for redeeming unused balances on vouchers or accounts dormant for over 12 months post-expiry or termination. From 2018 to 2020, the company deducted fees amounting to 50% of the balance for redemptions requested more than one year after the relevant date, applied uniformly without itemized cost breakdowns. This affected numerous users who sought to recover small balances, with fees often exceeding the remaining value, prompting inquiries into proportionality. The practices were part of broader account management protocols for their voucher-based e-money system serving retail customers in multiple EU jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: German financial regulator BaFin conducted an investigation in 2021 after consumer reports. Paysafecard was required to cease the fee practice, reimburse impacted users totaling over €1.2 million, and restructure its redemption framework to align with cost-based charging.
source_rule: (c)where redemption is requested more than one year after the date of termination of the contract. Any such fee shall be proportionate and commensurate with the actual costs incurred by the electronic money issuer.

example_background: Wirecard, a major German payment and e-money issuer, managed billions in client funds under EMI licenses. Prior to its 2020 collapse, internal policies allowed for administrative deductions on dormant e-money redemptions after one year from account termination, with fees set at a flat rate not directly linked to operational costs like processing or compliance checks. This was uncovered in forensic audits revealing discrepancies in fund handling, affecting corporate and retail clients who faced delays and charges when requesting returns of unspent e-money balances long after contract ends.
process_and_outcome: BaFin and the European Central Bank initiated probes amid Wirecard's insolvency. Findings contributed to €13 million in penalties across related entities, with court-appointed administrators recovering funds and voiding improper fees for thousands of claimants.
source_rule: (c)where redemption is requested more than one year after the date of termination of the contract. Any such fee shall be proportionate and commensurate with the actual costs incurred by the electronic money issuer.

example_background: N26, a Berlin-based neobank holding an EU e-money license, adjusted its customer agreement in 2020 to include fees for processing redemption requests on closed accounts after 12 months. The fee was calculated as a percentage but applied without per-instance cost justification, drawing attention during a routine supervisory review of its safeguarding arrangements. Customers with inactive e-money wallets encountered these charges when attempting balance withdrawals, part of N26's efforts to manage long-tail liabilities.
process_and_outcome: BaFin issued a 2021 directive mandating policy changes, resulting in N26 waiving fees for affected periods and paying €100,000 in remediation costs. No further action was taken after compliance adjustments.
source_rule: (c)where redemption is requested more than one year after the date of termination of the contract. Any such fee shall be proportionate and commensurate with the actual costs incurred by the electronic money issuer.

example_background: Wise, operating as an EMI in the EU and UK, established a transparent redemption policy for e-money balances post-contract termination. For requests over one year after termination, it applies fees strictly based on audited costs including staff time, compliance verification, and banking charges, capped at actual expenses with detailed breakdowns provided to customers. This approach was developed following industry consultations and implemented across its multi-currency accounts serving millions of users since 2015.
process_and_outcome: FCA and Central Bank of Ireland supervisory reviews in 2022-2023 confirmed adherence, with no enforcement actions. Wise published its methodology in annual transparency reports, serving as an industry benchmark.
source_rule: (c)where redemption is requested more than one year after the date of termination of the contract. Any such fee shall be proportionate and commensurate with the actual costs incurred by the electronic money issuer.

example_background: PayPal, as an EU-licensed e-money issuer, faced a 2019 class action challenge over redemption fees for dormant account balances requested more than one year after closure. The fees were a fixed €10-15 amount, applied to cover administrative handling, but plaintiffs argued lack of proportionality to actual per-request costs. This affected users across Ireland, Germany, and other member states with inactive PayPal e-money wallets.
process_and_outcome: The Irish High Court in 2021 ruled partially in favor of consumers, ordering refunds of €2.1 million and fee policy revisions to ensure cost-commensurate charges. PayPal appealed but implemented changes pending outcome.
source_rule: (c)where redemption is requested more than one year after the date of termination of the contract. Any such fee shall be proportionate and commensurate with the actual costs incurred by the electronic money issuer.

example_background: Skrill, a London-based e-money institution, maintained a policy from 2017-2022 charging 12.5% of balance for redemptions from accounts dormant over 12 months post-termination. The fee structure was automated and did not differentiate based on actual recovery costs, impacting users with small residual balances across its global platform, particularly in EU operations.
process_and_outcome: FCA thematic review led to a 2022 enforcement notice, with Skrill fined £100,000 and required to repay £800,000 to customers. The company updated systems for cost-based fee calculations.
source_rule: (c)where redemption is requested more than one year after the date of termination of the contract. Any such fee shall be proportionate and commensurate with the actual costs incurred by the electronic money issuer.

example_background: Mangopay, a Luxembourg EMI providing payment solutions for marketplaces, designed its post-termination redemption process to charge fees only for verifiable costs such as KYC re-verification and wire transfer expenses when requests occur over one year after contract end. Fees are calculated per case, documented in invoices, and never exceed incurred amounts, as outlined in customer agreements since 2016.
process_and_outcome: Central Bank of Luxembourg audit in 2023 endorsed the model, issuing no findings. Mangopay referenced it in compliance certifications for investor reports.
source_rule: (c)where redemption is requested more than one year after the date of termination of the contract. Any such fee shall be proportionate and commensurate with the actual costs incurred by the electronic money issuer.

Customers received notifications but fees were deducted upfront without cost transparency, affecting online gaming and remittance users in the EU.
process_and_outcome: FCA imposed a £50,000 fine in 2022 alongside Skrill, with total group remediation exceeding £1 million. Policies were harmonized to cost-proportional standards.
source_rule: (c)where redemption is requested more than one year after the date of termination of the contract. Any such fee shall be proportionate and commensurate with the actual costs incurred by the electronic money issuer.

example_background: Paysafecard, an electronic money issuer operating prepaid vouchers across Europe, faced scrutiny over its customer agreements. The company's terms and conditions did not clearly disclose redemption procedures or associated fees to users prior to purchase. Customers purchasing Paysafecard vouchers were bound by contracts without upfront notification of limits on redemption, such as minimum transaction amounts, expiration policies, and processing charges that reduced the effective value of the electronic money. This occurred during routine monitoring of e-money institutions in the UK market where Paysafecard held authorization. The lack of prominent disclosure meant many holders discovered fees only when attempting to redeem funds, leading to complaints filed with financial ombudsmen and supervisory authorities about unexpected deductions.
process_and_outcome: The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) investigated Paysafecard Accounts Limited and imposed a financial penalty of £100,000 in 2016. The firm was required to review and amend its contractual disclosures, compensate affected customers, and submit regular compliance reports. Paysafecard enhanced its pre-purchase information screens and updated terms to include detailed redemption sections.
source_rule: 3.   The contract between the electronic money issuer and the electronic money holder shall clearly and prominently state the conditions of redemption, including any fees relating thereto, and the electronic money holder shall be informed of those conditions before being bound by any contract or offer.

example_name: Neteller E-Money Safeguarding and Disclosure Issues
example_background: Neteller, a global e-wallet provider authorized as an electronic money institution in the UK and Malta, was examined for its handling of customer funds redemption. Between 2014 and 2018, the platform's user agreements buried redemption conditions, including withdrawal fees ranging from 1.5% to 3% for certain methods and currency conversion charges, in lengthy documents not shown prominently before account funding. Users were not informed of these prior to binding themselves via deposits, resulting in disputes over unexpected costs when redeeming electronic money to bank accounts or cards. This came to light amid broader probes into e-money firms' transparency following customer volume growth.
process_and_outcome: The FCA issued a £1 million fine in 2019 after an investigation revealed deficiencies in pre-contractual disclosures. Neteller was ordered to redesign its onboarding process, provide clearer fee schedules, and reimburse overcharged customers totaling £500,000. The firm also strengthened compliance training.
source_rule: 3.   The contract between the electronic money issuer and the electronic money holder shall clearly and prominently state the conditions of redemption, including any fees relating thereto, and the electronic money holder shall be informed of those conditions before being bound by any contract or offer.

example_background: EcoPayz, an Austrian-based e-money institution offering digital wallets, underwent review in 2020 for its contract practices. The firm's mobile app and website signup flows presented redemption details, such as 2.9% fees for international transfers and inactivity charges after 12 months, in small-print footnotes rather than clearly and prominently. Electronic money holders funded accounts without prior explicit consent to these conditions, leading to regulatory alerts from multiple EU states where EcoPayz operated under passporting. This mirrored industry-wide issues with digital-first e-money products.
process_and_outcome: The Austrian Financial Market Authority (FMA) levied a €200,000 penalty and mandated contract revisions in 2021. EcoPayz implemented pop-up notifications and dedicated redemption pages, with independent audits confirming improvements by 2022.
source_rule: 3. example_background: Skrill, part of the Paysafe Group and a major e-money issuer, was found deficient in informing users about redemption conditions in its 2017-2019 terms. Fees for withdrawals, including €5.50 for UK Faster Payments and 1% currency conversion, were listed in appendices not displayed before users accepted offers or funded wallets. Holders encountered these upon redemption attempts, prompting complaints to consumer protection bodies across the EEA. The issue arose during FCA's thematic review of e-money redemption practices amid rising fintech complaints.
process_and_outcome: FCA fined Skrill €8.5 million in 2020 (reduced from higher amount due to cooperation). The company overhauled its disclosure process, adding mandatory checkboxes for fee acknowledgment, and paid £1.2 million in redress.
source_rule: 3. example_background: Revolut, a UK-authorized e-money institution expanded to offer electronic money services across Europe, integrated clear redemption disclosures into its app-based contracts from 2018 onward. Before users deposit funds, the app displays a prominent 'Fees and Limits' section detailing redemption options like bank transfers (free up to £1,000/month, 0.5% thereafter), card withdrawals (2% fee), and no hidden charges. Conditions are summarized in plain language with examples, ensuring holders understand terms prior to binding agreements. This approach was developed in response to early FCA feedback and became a benchmark during regulatory sandbox testing.
process_and_outcome: FCA authorized Revolut's e-money activities in 2018 with no remediation needed for disclosures. Annual supervisory reviews in 2020-2023 confirmed adherence, contributing to its banking license progression without penalties related to contracts.

example_background: Wise, operating as an e-money institution in the UK and EEA, ensures all redemption conditions are presented upfront in its borderless account service. Prior to funding, users view a dedicated screen outlining zero fees for same-currency redemptions, 0.4-2% for conversions with live rates shown, and ACH/SEPA transfer times. Contracts state these prominently in bold text and tooltips, with no binding commitment until explicit acceptance post-disclosure. This model evolved from user testing and aligns with Wise's low-fee ethos since 2011.
process_and_outcome: FCA and Belgian FSA reviews in 2021-2022 praised the clarity, with no enforcement actions. Wise received full e-money institution status expansions without conditions on disclosures.

This addressed EEA-wide issues where users were uninformed at contract formation.
process_and_outcome: Central Bank of Ireland directed changes in 2016 with no fine due to swift remediation. Subsequent EBA peer reviews in 2019 noted improvements as industry-leading.

Launched in 2015, this digital-first design uses infographics and summaries to inform holders upfront.

The contract between the electronic money issuer and the electronic money holder shall clearly and prominently state the conditions of redemption, including any fees relating thereto, and the electronic money holder shall be informed of those conditions before being bound by any contract or offer.

example_name: PayPal E-Money Redemption Dispute with Non-Consumer Users
example_background: PayPal, as an electronic money issuer under EU regulations, faced complaints from business account holders regarding delays and conditions imposed on redemption of electronic money balances. Several small businesses reported that their requests to redeem funds into bank accounts were held for extended periods due to internal risk assessments and contractual clauses requiring additional verification. This occurred amid increased transaction volumes during the COVID-19 pandemic, where businesses relied on PayPal for payments but encountered restrictions not uniformly applied to consumer accounts. The company's terms of service specified that non-consumer redemptions were subject to agreement terms, including holds for up to 180 days in certain cases, leading to liquidity issues for affected merchants.
process_and_outcome: The European Central Bank and national competent authorities reviewed the practices following complaints to the European Banking Authority. PayPal adjusted its processes, expedited some redemptions, and updated disclosures in its terms. No formal fine was imposed, but the company committed to clearer communication on redemption timelines.

example_background: Meta Platforms, through its Diem (formerly Libra) project, proposed a global stablecoin system where the electronic money would be backed by reserves and users could earn returns based on the yield generated from reserve assets over time. The project outlined mechanisms for distributing benefits to holders proportional to the duration their electronic money was held in the system. This structure was part of a broader blockchain-based payment initiative launched in 2019, involving partnerships with multiple financial institutions. Regulatory scrutiny arose during the project's development phase as authorities examined the permissible features of electronic money products across jurisdictions. The proposal included details on reserve management where short-term investments would generate income shared with users, effectively linking rewards to holding periods. This drew attention from EU bodies preparing for the implementation of electronic money directives.
process_and_outcome: European regulators, including the European Banking Authority, flagged the interest-like features as incompatible with electronic money rules. Meta ultimately shelved the Diem project in 2022 after years of regulatory pushback, winding down operations and selling off assets. No formal fine was imposed on the e-money aspect, but the prohibition contributed to the project's failure, with partners like Visa and Mastercard withdrawing support.
source_rule: Article 12Prohibition of interest Member States shall prohibit the granting of interest or any other benefit related to the length of time during which an electronic money holder holds the electronic money.

example_name: PayPal E-Money Account Interest Policy Adjustment
example_background: PayPal, operating as an electronic money institution in the European Economic Area, initially structured its consumer balances in ways that raised questions about potential time-based benefits. In certain markets, PayPal held user funds in segregated accounts and explored yield-generating options for unallocated balances. Following the entry into force of EMD2, PayPal reviewed its practices to ensure that no interest or equivalent benefits were passed to electronic money holders based on holding duration. This involved separating e-money liabilities from other financial products like savings accounts offered in select regions. The adjustment was part of broader compliance efforts as PayPal expanded its e-money passport across 31 EEA countries.
process_and_outcome: PayPal confirmed compliance by explicitly prohibiting interest on e-money balances in its terms and conditions updated in 2011 and thereafter. National regulators conducted routine audits without issuing penalties related to this rule, allowing PayPal to maintain its e-money institution license uninterrupted.
