example_name: BaFin Enforcement against Wirecard E-Money Operations
example_background: Wirecard AG, through subsidiaries like Wirecard Card Solutions, was authorized as an e-money institution in multiple EU jurisdictions, issuing prepaid cards and electronic money under the framework of Directive 2009/110/EC. From 2016 to 2020, investigations revealed that Wirecard had misrepresented its e-money issuance volumes and client fund safeguarding practices. The company reported billions in e-money liabilities but failed to maintain corresponding segregated safeguards for funds received for electronic money issuance. This occurred amid broader accounting irregularities where third-party acquisition balances, some tied to e-money partners in Asia, were fabricated. BaFin and other regulators monitored Wirecard's compliance with e-money rules post the 2011 directive repeal, but initial oversight relied on self-reported data that proved inaccurate.
process_and_outcome: BaFin withdrew Wirecard's licenses in June 2020 amid insolvency proceedings. Criminal investigations led to charges against executives, with fines and asset freezes. The ECB and national authorities coordinated to protect e-money holders, resulting in full reimbursement from safeguards where verifiable.

example_name: FCA Approval of Revolut E-Money to Banking License Transition
example_background: Revolut Ltd, originally authorized as an e-money institution by the FCA in 2017, issued electronic money and provided payment services across the EEA under Directive 2009/110/EC passporting. The company grew rapidly, handling millions of customer accounts with e-money balances. In 2021, Revolut applied for a full UK banking license while maintaining compliant e-money operations. During the application process, the FCA reviewed Revolut's e-money issuance practices, including fund safeguarding, transaction monitoring, and customer fund protection, confirming adherence to requirements established after the repeal of Directive 2000/46/EC.
process_and_outcome: Revolut received restricted banking authorization from the FCA in July 2024, allowing expansion while upholding e-money compliance. No issues were found in prior e-money operations, enabling seamless transition.

example_name: Central Bank of Ireland Fine on PayPal E-Money Safeguarding
example_background: PayPal Payments (EU) Limited, an Irish-authorized e-money institution since 2011, issues electronic money for its EU customers. In 2019, the Central Bank of Ireland's review found that PayPal's processes for safeguarding relevant funds—monies received in exchange for electronic money—did not fully comply with separation and insolvency-proofing requirements. Between 2015 and 2018, there were instances where fund reconciliation delays and inadequate trustee arrangements exposed safeguarded funds to operational risks. This was identified during thematic supervision on e-money institutions post-Directive 2009/110/EC implementation.
process_and_outcome: The Central Bank imposed a €6.5 million fine in 2021 after an enforcement investigation. PayPal settled without admitting liability, enhanced its safeguarding framework, and reported full remediation.

example_name: FCA Fine on Collinson Group for E-Money Breaches
example_background: Collinson Insurance Group Limited and Collinson International Limited, part of the Collinson Group authorized as e-money institutions, offered foreign exchange and prepaid card services involving electronic money issuance. From 2014 to 2018, the firms failed to maintain adequate policies for approving new agents distributing their e-money products, did not conduct sufficient due diligence, and lacked proper monitoring of agent transactions. This led to risks in the distribution chain for electronic money. The FCA's multi-year investigation uncovered these governance and control shortcomings in e-money operations.
process_and_outcome: The FCA fined the group £339,783 in 2021. The firms voluntarily surrendered their e-money permissions and ceased relevant activities, implementing corrective governance measures.

example_name: Starling Bank E-Money Compliance During Growth
example_background: Starling Bank, authorized as an e-money institution by the FCA in 2016 before obtaining a full banking license in 2019, issued electronic money through its mobile banking app to UK customers. The bank maintained segregated client money accounts, daily reconciliations, and robust IT systems for e-money issuance and redemption. During FCA supervision, Starling demonstrated adherence to safeguarding and reporting requirements under the post-2011 regulatory framework.
process_and_outcome: Starling successfully transitioned to a banking license in 2019 with no compliance findings against its e-money operations. It received positive supervisory feedback and expanded services.

example_name: Deutsche Bank E-Money Fine by BaFin
example_background: Deutsche Bank AG's payment subsidiary held e-money institution status and issued electronic money for corporate clients. In 2020, BaFin found that from 2016 to 2019, the bank did not properly identify and safeguard funds received specifically for e-money issuance, mixing them with other client monies. This breached segregation rules, with volumes in the hundreds of millions of euros annually. The issues were uncovered during an AML and payments-themed inspection.
process_and_outcome: BaFin imposed a €7.2 million fine in 2022. Deutsche Bank remediated systems, separated fund flows, and enhanced compliance training.

example_background: Monzo initially operated under an e-money institution license from the FCA starting in 2017, issuing electronic money for its digital banking customers. The firm implemented ring-fenced safeguarding accounts with daily attestations by independent trustees, real-time monitoring of e-money balances, and transparent reporting to regulators. This setup ensured funds were protected in line with Directive 2009/110/EC requirements during rapid customer onboarding.
process_and_outcome: Monzo obtained full banking authorization in October 2024 following clean supervisory reviews of its e-money compliance. No remedial actions were required.

example_background: Barings Bank, a British merchant bank established in 1762, operated through its Singapore office where trader Nick Leeson engaged in unauthorized derivatives trading on the Nikkei index futures. Leeson accumulated massive losses totaling over £800 million due to speculative positions that were hidden in a secret error account numbered 88888. The bank functioned as a credit institution under EU banking directives by receiving deposits and granting credits, but lacked proper internal controls over its trading activities. Leeson's positions initially generated profits, but subsequent market downturns, particularly after the Kobe earthquake in 1995, led to unsustainable losses exceeding the bank's capital base. The absence of segregation between trading and settlement functions at Barings Futures Singapore allowed the accumulation of undetected losses over several years.
process_and_outcome: The UK's Serious Fraud Office and Singapore authorities investigated. Barings Bank was declared insolvent on February 26, 1995, and sold to ING for £1. The Bank of England and Singapore regulators imposed no direct fines on the entity as it collapsed, but Nick Leeson was arrested, extradited to Singapore, convicted of fraud, and sentenced to 6.5 years in prison. The incident prompted global regulatory reforms in risk management for banks.

example_background: HSBC Holdings plc, a UK-headquartered global banking group operating as a credit institution with extensive deposit-taking and lending activities in Europe and worldwide, facilitated transactions for high-risk clients including Mexican drug cartels and sanctioned entities between 2000 and 2010. US investigators found deficiencies in HSBC's anti-money laundering controls, such as inadequate monitoring of $878 billion in bulk cash shipments from Mexico, suspicious wire transfers totaling $4.1 billion, and failures to apply enhanced due diligence to politically exposed persons. These issues persisted despite prior regulatory warnings, impacting the integrity of HSBC's core deposit and credit granting operations.
process_and_outcome: US DOJ deferred prosecution after HSBC paid $1.9 billion penalty in 2012 ($1.256B forfeiture, $665M civil fine), the largest then for a bank. HSBC implemented a five-year monitorship, overhauled AML systems, and exited high-risk markets.

example_background: Danske Bank A/S, Denmark's largest bank and an EU credit institution engaged in deposit-taking and lending, processed approximately €200 billion in suspicious transactions through its Estonian branch from 2007 to 2015. Non-resident clients, primarily from Russia, Latvia, and Azerbaijan, used shell companies to move funds via accounts opened with minimal due diligence. The branch lacked proper transaction monitoring systems despite internal warnings as early as 2013, allowing the flow of potentially illicit proceeds through the bank's core banking platform that handled public deposits and credits.
process_and_outcome: Danish FSA, US DOJ, and EU authorities investigated. Danske paid €470 million Danish fine in 2021, $2 billion US settlement in 2022, and faced €383 million EU AML fine. Executives resigned, and the bank sold the Estonian branch.
source_rule: (*1)   OJ L 267, 10.10.2009, p 7 ’."Article 20Amendments to Directive 2006/48/ECDirective 2006/48/EC is hereby amended as follows:1.Article 4 is amended as following:(a)point 1 is replaced by the following:‘1. “credit institution” means an undertaking the business of which is to receive deposits or other repayable funds from the public and to grant credits for its own account;’

example_background: Swedbank AB, a Swedish credit institution operating deposit and credit services in the EU, facilitated €40 billion in suspicious transactions through its Estonian, Latvian, and Swedish branches from 2007 to 2015. These payments, linked to high-risk non-resident clients and mirrored patterns seen at Danske Bank, involved inadequate customer verification and transaction monitoring in the Baltic operations where public deposits were managed alongside cross-border flows.
process_and_outcome: Swedish FSA, Estonian FIU, and US authorities investigated. Swedbank paid SEK 4 billion (€386 million) Swedish fine in 2020 and $3.4 million FinCEN penalty in 2022. Leadership changes occurred, and compliance investments exceeded SEK 10 billion.
source_rule: (*1)   OJ L 267, 10.10.2009, p 7 ’."Article 20Amendments to Directive 2006/48/ECDirective 2006/48/EC is hereby amended as follows:1.Article 4 is amended as following:(a)point 1 is replaced by the following:‘1. “credit institution” means an undertaking the business of which is to receive deposits or other repayable funds from the public and to grant credits for its own account;’

example_background: UBS AG, a Swiss bank with EU credit institution status through deposit-taking and lending arms, assisted over 17,000 US clients in evading taxes from 2002 to 2007 by smuggling account data on USB drives, structuring deposits to avoid reporting thresholds, and using offshore entities. This involved UBS's private banking division managing undeclared US client funds totaling billions, interfacing with its core EU deposit operations.
process_and_outcome: US DOJ investigated; UBS pleaded guilty in 2009 to conspiring to defraud the US, paying $780 million. The bank disclosed client names under court order and ceased US cross-border business.

example_name: European Commission Infringement against Belgium on CRD IV
example_background: Belgium faced scrutiny for delayed and incomplete transposition of CRD IV (Directive 2013/36/EU) into national law. The directive required Member States to adopt measures by 31 December 2013 with explicit references upon publication. Belgium's implementation through amendments to the Act of 25 April 2014 on the status and supervision of credit institutions occurred late, and initial drafts lacked clear references to the directive in official gazettes. This affected requirements for own funds, leverage ratios, and remuneration policies for financial institutions under Belgian jurisdiction.
process_and_outcome: In June 2015, the European Commission sent a reasoned opinion to Belgium under Article 258 TFEU for failure to notify full transposition. Belgium completed measures in 2016, leading to closure of the case without referral to the Court of Justice.
source_rule: They shall apply those measures from 30 April 2011. When Member States adopt those measures, they shall contain a reference to this Directive or be accompanied by such a reference on the occasion of their official publication. The methods of making such reference shall be laid down by the Member States.
relevant_rules: Treaty on the Functioning of the EU (Article 258)

example_name: Germany Transposition of Payment Services Directive (PSD2)
example_background: PSD2 (Directive (EU) 2015/2366) mandated transposition by 13 January 2018 with references in national measures. Germany implemented this via the Payment Services Supervision Act (ZAG) and amendments to the German Civil Code, published in the Federal Law Gazette (Bundesgesetzblatt) on 13 June 2017. Each legislative act included explicit mentions of PSD2, such as 'in implementation of Directive (EU) 2015/2366,' ensuring alignment with EU rules on payment initiation services, account information services, and strong customer authentication.
process_and_outcome: Transposition was notified to the Commission on time, avoiding infringement proceedings. BaFin issued implementing guidelines referencing PSD2.
source_rule: They shall apply those measures from 30 April 2011. When Member States adopt those measures, they shall contain a reference to this Directive or be accompanied by such a reference on the occasion of their official publication. The methods of making such reference shall be laid down by the Member States.

example_name: Italy Infringement Proceedings on Mortgage Credit Directive
example_background: The Mortgage Credit Directive (Directive 2014/17/EU) required transposition by 21 March 2016 with publication references. Italy's Legislative Decree No. 165 of 2016 was adopted late on 11 October 2016 and published without immediate explicit reference to the directive in the initial Gazzetta Ufficiale notice, though later clarifications were added. This impacted consumer protection rules for residential mortgages, including creditworthiness assessments and early repayment rights.
process_and_outcome: European Commission launched infringement in 2017 (case 2017/2209), issuing a reasoned opinion. Italy regularized with further decrees in 2018, resolving the matter.
source_rule: They shall apply those measures from 30 April 2011. When Member States adopt those measures, they shall contain a reference to this Directive or be accompanied by such a reference on the occasion of their official publication. The methods of making such reference shall be laid down by the Member States.

example_background: The Alternative Investment Fund Managers Directive (AIFMD, Directive 2011/61/EU) set a transposition deadline of 22 July 2013. France adopted Ordinance No. 2013-676 on 30 July 2013, published in the Journal Officiel with direct reference: 'En application de la directive 2011/61/UE.' Subsequent decrees detailed reporting and valuation rules, all citing the directive, covering authorization, transparency, and depositary requirements for alternative investment funds.
process_and_outcome: Autorité des Marchés Financiers (AMF) issued aligned regulations. Commission confirmed full transposition, no proceedings.
source_rule: They shall apply those measures from 30 April 2011. When Member States adopt those measures, they shall contain a reference to this Directive or be accompanied by such a reference on the occasion of their official publication. The methods of making such reference shall be laid down by the Member States.

example_name: Spain Delayed Transposition of UCITS V
example_background: UCITS V Directive (2014/91/EU) required measures by 13 March 2016. Spain transposed via Royal Decree-Law 6/2015 on 30 October 2015 but delayed full notification and publication references in the Official State Gazette (BOE) until 2016 amendments. References were inconsistent initially, affecting depositary functions, remuneration, and sanctions for UCITS funds.
process_and_outcome: Commission infringement 2016/2353; reasoned opinion issued. Spain notified completion in July 2016, case closed.
source_rule: They shall apply those measures from 30 April 2011. When Member States adopt those measures, they shall contain a reference to this Directive or be accompanied by such a reference on the occasion of their official publication. The methods of making such reference shall be laid down by the Member States.
involved_parties: Comisión Nacional del Mercado de Valores (CNMV)

example_background: The Netherlands implemented CRD IV and Regulation (EU) No 575/2013 (CRR) through the Financial Supervision Act (Wft) amendments, adopted 29 August 2013 and published in Staatsblad with explicit reference to Directive 2013/36/EU. The decree specified methods for references as per national practice, covering prudential requirements from capital adequacy to risk management.
process_and_outcome: De Nederlandsche Bank (DNB) and AFM enforced rules from 1 January 2014. Timely notification to Commission.
source_rule: They shall apply those measures from 30 April 2011. When Member States adopt those measures, they shall contain a reference to this Directive or be accompanied by such a reference on the occasion of their official publication. The methods of making such reference shall be laid down by the Member States.

example_background: EMIR (Regulation (EU) No 648/2012) included delegated acts requiring national measures from 2013 with references. Poland delayed transposition of related directive elements until 2016, with publications in Dziennik Ustaw lacking initial directive citations, impacting derivatives reporting and clearing obligations.
process_and_outcome: ECJ case C-434/17 in 2018 ruled against Poland for incomplete transposition. Poland amended laws post-judgment.
source_rule: They shall apply those measures from 30 April 2011. When Member States adopt those measures, they shall contain a reference to this Directive or be accompanied by such a reference on the occasion of their official publication. The methods of making such reference shall be laid down by the Member States.

example_background: Sweden transposed PSD2 via amendments to the Payment Services Act, published 13 November 2017 in Svensk Författningssamling with clear reference to Directive (EU) 2015/2366. The Finansinspektionen issued supplementary regulations citing the directive, detailing open banking and security measures.
process_and_outcome: Applied from 13 January 2018 as required. No infringement actions.
source_rule: They shall apply those measures from 30 April 2011. When Member States adopt those measures, they shall contain a reference to this Directive or be accompanied by such a reference on the occasion of their official publication. The methods of making such reference shall be laid down by the Member States.

example_background: In 2014, the European Commission initiated infringement proceedings against Italy for failing to fully transpose Directive 2013/36/EU on capital requirements for credit institutions and investment firms, known as CRD IV, into national law. CRD IV required Member States to adopt and communicate the main provisions of national laws by 31 December 2013. Italy notified partial transposition measures but omitted key provisions related to supervisory powers, recovery and resolution plans, and corporate governance requirements for banks. This gap affected the harmonization of prudential rules across the EU banking sector, potentially impacting financial stability. The Commission sent a formal notice in March 2014, followed by a reasoned opinion in September 2014, highlighting the incomplete communication of transposed texts to the Commission as required by Article 101 of CRD IV.
process_and_outcome: The Commission referred the case to the Court of Justice of the EU in 2015. Italy completed transposition in 2016, leading to the Commission withdrawing the case without a judgment. No financial penalties were imposed.
source_rule: 2.   Member States shall communicate to the Commission the text of the main provisions of national law which they adopt in the field covered by this Directive.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU (adjudicator)
relevant_rules: EU infringement procedure under Article 258 TFEU

example_background: Germany transposed the Capital Requirements Regulation (CRR) and Directive (CRD IV) package through the Kreditwesengesetz (KWG) and other national laws, notifying the European Commission of the main provisions on 31 December 2013, meeting the deadline. The notification included detailed texts covering prudential requirements for credit institutions, liquidity rules, leverage ratios, and remuneration policies. This ensured alignment with EU-wide standards for banking supervision under the Single Supervisory Mechanism.
process_and_outcome: The Commission's review confirmed full compliance with notification obligations. No proceedings were initiated, and Germany continued as a reference for timely implementation.
source_rule: 2.   Member States shall communicate to the Commission the text of the main provisions of national law which they adopt in the field covered by this Directive.

example_background: France faced infringement proceedings in 2019 for delayed and incomplete transposition of Directive (EU) 2015/2366 on payment services (PSD2). Member States were required to adopt and communicate national laws by 13 January 2018. France notified some measures but failed to fully communicate provisions on strong customer authentication, open banking access, and incident reporting. This delayed the rollout of secure payment services across the EU single market.
process_and_outcome: The Commission issued a reasoned opinion in June 2019 and referred the matter to the CJEU in December 2019. France finalized transposition in 2020, resolving the case.
source_rule: 2.   Member States shall communicate to the Commission the text of the main provisions of national law which they adopt in the field covered by this Directive.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_background: The Netherlands adopted the Markets in Financial Instruments Directive II (MiFID II) through amendments to the Wet op het financieel toezicht (Wft), notifying the European Commission of the main provisions on 3 January 2018, within the deadline of 21 June 2017. The notification covered investor protection rules, algorithmic trading requirements, and product governance standards, facilitating cross-border financial market access.
process_and_outcome: Commission verification confirmed adequacy of the notified texts. No enforcement actions followed, supporting smooth implementation.
source_rule: 2.   Member States shall communicate to the Commission the text of the main provisions of national law which they adopt in the field covered by this Directive.

example_background: Spain was pursued by the European Commission for not fully transposing Directive 2014/17/EU on credit agreements for consumers relating to residential immovable property. The deadline for adoption and communication of national laws was 16 March 2016. Spain notified partial measures via Royal Decree-Law 1/2017 but omitted key provisions on creditworthiness assessments and early repayment rules, affecting consumer protection in the housing finance market.
process_and_outcome: Infringement proceedings led to a CJEU judgment in 2020 (Case C-647/18) finding Spain in violation. Spain amended laws post-judgment.
source_rule: 2.   Member States shall communicate to the Commission the text of the main provisions of national law which they adopt in the field covered by this Directive.
involved_parties: Court of Justice of the EU

example_background: Sweden implemented the Fourth Anti-Money Laundering Directive (AMLD4) through changes to the Lag (2017:630) om åtgärder mot penningtvätt och finansiering av terrorism, notifying the Commission on 7 June 2017, meeting the 26 June 2017 deadline. The notified texts included obligations for beneficial ownership registers, risk assessments, and enhanced due diligence for financial institutions.
process_and_outcome: The Commission acknowledged complete notification. Sweden avoided proceedings and served as a model for implementation.
source_rule: 2.   Member States shall communicate to the Commission the text of the main provisions of national law which they adopt in the field covered by this Directive.

example_background: Belgium failed to transpose Regulation (EU) No 1286/2014 on key information documents for packaged retail and insurance-based investment products (PRIIPs) by the 31 December 2017 deadline, neglecting to communicate main national provisions to the Commission. This regulation aimed to enhance retail investor transparency in investment products.
process_and_outcome: Commission launched infringement in 2018, issuing a reasoned opinion. Belgium complied in 2019, closing the case.
source_rule: 2.   Member States shall communicate to the Commission the text of the main provisions of national law which they adopt in the field covered by this Directive.

example_background: Denmark enacted the Databeskyttelsesloven to transpose the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), notifying the Commission of main provisions on 21 May 2018, ahead of the 6 May 2018 deadline. The notification detailed data protection authorities' powers and national derogations for public security.
process_and_outcome: Commission confirmed receipt and compliance. Denmark integrated seamlessly into EU data protection framework.
source_rule: 2.   Member States shall communicate to the Commission the text of the main provisions of national law which they adopt in the field covered by this Directive.

example_background: Poland did not timely transpose the Insurance Distribution Directive (IDD, Directive (EU) 2016/97), missing the 23 February 2018 deadline for adopting and communicating national laws to the Commission. Gaps included rules on inducements, customer information, and cross-selling in insurance products.
process_and_outcome: Infringement letter in 2018, reasoned opinion in 2019. Poland notified full transposition in 2020, resolving the matter.
source_rule: 2.   Member States shall communicate to the Commission the text of the main provisions of national law which they adopt in the field covered by this Directive.
